The case Is much like the Urban Armor Gear with a stiffer bottom edge thanks to the stiffer tpu rubber. Its hybrid plastic construction is top quality, likely korean (where Dorco, the best shaving blades in the world are manufactured). It includes a decent Tempered Glass screen protector. I say it is decent because, while it is amazing manufacture quality,it lacks the rounded edges on the newer more expensive ones; however this is not an issue though the edges of the glass are somewhat sharp, the case fits flawlessly and they are not an issue when paired. Another drawback is the thin PET coating on the top, which provides a longer lasting finish, it does however negligibly diminish screen quality and does not provide the fingerprint resistance of an oleophobic coating, it cleans easily, and provides 8H/9H protection (the same i'd expect from a FLEX Tempered Glass). The combination provides top quality protection, little bulk, and extremely inexpensive. it compares to many $30 cases i've had, if not better than most, and the screen protection included is on par with the best flexible tempered glass solutions.Bottom Line:Pros: Not too bulky, Amazing protection, sleek look, the matte plastic back is very fingerprint resistant. Extremely inexpensive @ $12 the product is easily worth much more, the screen protector pairs nicely with the Spigen Neo hybrid as well.Cons: Buttons are a bit less tactile than i'd prefer (but extremely responsive), The screen does lose a slight bit of quality, and collects fingerprints, i just always have a microfiber in the wallet.

A week ago I got a brand-new Dell XPS 13 for which I was keen to get I docking station.  Dell recommended the Targus as an accessory during the purchase process, but at $199 it was a lot more expensive than on Amazon.  One would think that because Dell recommends it, it should just work.  Well, it doesn't.After unpacking the 'black box' I found one piece of paper that basically stated "run Windows update" and "plug it in if you have WIndows 7."  I did both - in that order.  Next, according to the pictogram, I should have seen a program load.  That didn't happen.  What did happen was that Windows reported that other than the USB 2.0 and 3.0 hubs, it hadn't found the drivers for any other functionality of the docking station. Hmmm.  So far for it being just 2 steps.  I decided to unplug it and plug it in again.  No difference.  Next, I saw a small note that said "if the software doesn't load automatically, install it from the DVD".  Well, the Dell XPS 13 doesn't have a DVD drive... First I tried to find the software on the Targus site but it wasn't there.  Next, I used an iMac (yes!) to get the content from the DVD onto a USB memory stick.  I then installed the software on the Dell and plugged the dock back in.  No difference - still no drivers.  It just never worked.At this price, one would think it just works, and works well.  Ignoring the rather cheap build quality (especially the power connection), it's clear that this docking station needs work to be truly "just working".

I live in the country with wood floors, two dogs and two cats, and lots of spider webs, fur, and dust.  I needed a powerful, easily maneuverable canister vacuum with a large bag.I didn't want a regular canister vacuum because the ones with the large bags are so heavy and it's a workout just to carry them to the room that needs to be cleaned.  The small, easily moved canister vacuums have such small bags and tip over so easily.  I got an idea from watching the ads for the new Shark Rotator - they are showing that an upright vacuum has a separate canister function when you use the rolling canister platform.I put two and two together.  I wanted a bagged vacuum and I knew that this vacuum has a separate Tools function.  My experiment worked wonderfully.  I am very pleased with using this vacuum as a very powerful, easily moved, canister vacuum.I originally bought a Eureka Airspeed bagged AS1050 at Walmart.  I really loved the vacuum but the noise was really annoying.  After my ears were ringing when I vacuumed our large kitchen, my husband was so concerned that the noise might damage my hearing that he got me earplugs.  Finally, I decided that the noise was a deal-breaker and I returned the vacuum to Walmart.I read a review that the Walmart version of the Eureka Airspeed bagless was made more cheaply than the version sold in other stores.  I really did like this vacuum and took a chance and bought a Eureka Airspeed bagged AS1051a (note the different model number) from Lowes.  I paid $20.00 more but the Lowes vacuum does not make the pulsing, vibrating noise that the Walmart vacuum did.There are several differences between the Walmart and the Lowes vacuums.  The Lowes cord is 30 ft compared to 27 ft for the Walmart cord.  The hose seems more flexible in the Lowes vacuum.  The dust brush is much better quality in the Lowes vacuum.  The Lowes vacuum is a different color than the Walmart vacuum.  And, the Lowes vacuum uses 3M Filtrete bags instead of Arm and Hammer bags.  I don't know, but wonder if there's also some kind of internal manufacturing difference that makes the Walmart version so much louder.The rest of this review applies to both the Walmart and Lowes versions of the vacuum.I was impressed at how carefully this vacuum was packed.  It gives the impression that they want you to have a very nice product.  I also liked the included parts list on the Quick Start Guide.  I was easily able to see that I had all of the required parts.The assembly instructions need improvement.  They state that the handle needs to be snapped on.  After many tries, I couldn't get the handle to snap.  I had to call Eureka and they admitted that the handle doesn't acutally snap, it's simply fitted securely onto the top of the vacuum.  The other parts snap easily in place except for the turbo brush holder.  In both vacuums, that part was hard to get lined up and then one side didn't easily snap.  What I finally did was to use the rubber circle which we use to open jars to get a better grip on the sides of the turbo brush holder.  With a better grip, I was finally able to snap the corners into place.The design of the bags is excellent.  They are large and they just lift out of and drop into the top of the removeable dust cup.  I don't know if the 3M Filtrete bags are a better quality than the Arm and Hammer bags.The tools which come with this vacuum are OK but I have a collection of tools and wands from previous vacuums.  The turbo brush, which comes with the vacuum, has an oddly shaped, larger opening and will only fit on the end of the Eureka hose and wand.  But, the Eureka wand will also accept my standard 1.25" tools and 1.25" extension hose.Using the Tools function, with the carpet height set on high, I had no problem with the spinning brush touching my wood floor or my rugs.  I also mistakenly left the cord under the vacuum for a few minutes when I moved the vacuum across the room.  The cord seems fine in spite of the spinning brush.The foot-activated on/off swich is very handy.The suction is excellent.  I added my 15 foot extension hose to the end of the vacuum's hose and I still had incredible suction.  My dusting brush, which needs strong suction, worked beautifully.  I also put a long wand and a mini brush on the end of my 15 foot extension hose and I was easily able to vacuum back in corners and on the ceiling.  Most amazing, when I removed the brush and put the end of the 15 foot extension hose into a deep vase, the suction was so strong that the hose actually stuck to the bottom of the vase.  I had to forcefully yank the hose out of the vase.The cord has a tendency to get caught up under the vacuum so I took a tie wrap and attached the cord to the front of the handle.  That easily solved the problem.I didn't use this vacuum on the Floors function because it does seems large and heavy compared to my lightweight Oreck which is great on my rugs and my bare floors.  I only used the Tools function with the addition of my 15 foot extension hose and my own attachments.  Used this way, the Eureka AirSpeed was very fast and very powerful.  I'm very pleased with my purchase of the Eureka Airspeed AS1051a.

I bought this for my house because I was so tired of yelling upstairs or having to physically walk there when I only have a short message for my kids, like "Dinner's ready!"  What a perfect solution.I bought 4 for each corner of our 2-story house, which is 3600 sq. ft.  Setup was easy.  They were on the minute the batteries were in.  I wasn't exactly sure how to program them at first as the instructions said to push the learn button and wait for the beep 10 seconds later.  Turns out it beeps the minute the learn button is pushed and if I wait for the second beep, the memory is cleared.  So, pick a unit to be the base unit.  Push the learn button on the new unit until it beeps once, then push the learn button on the base unit until it beeps once.  Push the talk button to connect and make sure all is well.  Repeat with other new units.They are perfectly quiet when in standby mode.  When I push talk, it beeps and anyone from any unit may respond.  Once that person responds, the two units automatically go on a single channel and can have a private conversation.  There is a way to have conference calls and a voice activated talking mode.I actually dropped one of the units when installing the batteries and the antenna broke in half.  It still works as good as the others.  When talking, there is no static or interference with other electronic equipment in the house, one of my primary concerns when buying this product.  Reception is clear throughout the house.This is a terrific intercom for the house and a great bargain right now.  I'm glad I got it.

I bought mine from Micro Center and ran my standard test to write/read 6.35GB of about 3500 photos and movie clips to/from the flash drive.  Write times are the most important.  It took over 2.5 hours to complete.  The read time was good at about 6 minutes.  Write: 0.71MB/sec  Read: 17.2M/sI can't believe PNY or Micro Center would sell such a poor performing item.  Hopefully this was only an anomaly in their batch.  Micro Center's return policy is great.

After abandoning my film SLR in the early 1990s for the ease of a point and shoot, and later, digital point and shoot cameras I bought this camera as my first DSLR exactly 2.5 years ago. I wanted more control over the focus and was frustrated with the auto focus of my point and shoot not focusing on what I wanted it to, especially for macro nature photography. I wanted more control with taking photos in existing light. For candids I wanted a fast shutter with a fast flash recovery so I could have photos of my kids blowing out the birthday candles instead of only shots after the fact due to long recharge times.A professional photographer friend looked over my xSi and was surprised at the number of features it has. It surpassed his two year old DSLR which cost a lot more at the time. He said the camera is really loaded and is set to do many things automatically that used to require intentional decision making and manual setting by the photographer. His point was that many users will probably not fully explore their camera's capabilities and also that Canon has taken some of the thinking process out of the equation, which amazed him. I will confess I have not yet learned all of the camera's functions as I've been teaching myself slowly using books.If you are intimidated to buy your first DSLR just know you can use it on the automatic function and slowly learn other modes as you desire. As a new challenge comes up I go teach myself about that topic then experiment with the new-to-me function.The camera body is lightweight. I carry it in my pocketbook with the 18.5 - 55mm image stabilizng lens that came with it.The battery lasts a long time, sometimes a month. Flash use saps the battery. I bought two extra inexpensive generic batteries and keep them charged and with me, especially useful when we're on vacation and I really don't want to run out of batteries. The batteries recharge within just a couple of hours with the Canon battery charger.I'm thrilled with this camera. I get many compliments on my photographs. I also entered two photos in an art show and actually sold one, so that is saying something.I'm enjoying the process of seeing the world though a photographer's eye. Being always at the ready with my camera with me at all times has allowed me to take thousands of photos over these two-plus years.

First of all, the price is great for this quality of a monitor. It's only 1080p, but what do you expect after the television industry went and ruined high-res monitors for us geeks? Anyway... The screen itself is great. It's an LED LCD monitor, so it's very bright (a little too bright on arrival, but with a little tweaking, it's awesome). The colors and contrast are very good, and especially good for the price of the monitor. It's also very durable, and I've had absolutely no problems with this monitor so far. Having multiple inputs is great for anyone with a PC and gaming console (I have my PC hooked up with a DVI-D cable, and my PS3 is connected with an HDMI cable), or any other device. It's nice not having to switch cables with multiple devices anymore. The monitor also tilts nicely, and is easy to turn on it's base (the screen itself doesn't move separately of the base, but the base is very easy to move on a smooth surface), yet stable and reliably stationary. I really like this monitor, and plan on purchases at least one more in the close future. I recommend this to literally any user, consumers and gamers alike.

I got this as a special offer for ten bucks on my Kindle Fire. The way I look at it, I overpaid by about five bucks if not more for the quality of the item. First, the 3.5mm audio in jack was misaligned so when I pushed the cable in it cam loose  from its position internally and now it kind of moves around a bit making it difficult to re-insert the cable. The open and close system for the two parts of the speaker is very rough and relies on just a tiny fraction of a rotation; there is no click or other feedback mechanism to know when it is securely closed. You just go through the motion and hope it'll stay closed for transport.The device says it is "Made for Kindle" on its packaging BUT it is a major KINDLE #FAIL:- it uses a Mini USB instead of the Micro USB cables that Kindles have had since forever;- the audio cable provided can't be plugged into a kindle that has the Kindle-specific Origami case installedOther disadvantages vs its competitors that cost a couple of bucks more MSRP:- there is NO VOLUME button on the speaker, unlike many of its competitors which have a volume button- there is no indicator how much charge is left on the battery other than a single LED that at some point turns red - there are competitors that have a 3-4 led gauge which can indicate the battery charge level- the on/off buton is rather flimsy - mine doesn't click and the little pin only travels about half of the distance in the case opening. Looking at the pin in the middle of the on/off line I don't know if it is on or off...- there are no openings at the bottom to direct the bass sound onto the table for increased acoustic effect like some of the other brands have. the X-Mini, Kinivo and AYL portable speakers produce much better sound for not a whole lot more (or even less)Overall a quite disappointing product.

In ordering this opener I was hoping to solve a few issues:1.  Rheumatoid arthritis has made my hand held unit a painful chore to use.2.  I didn't want to go to an counter-top hogging electric unit.3.  The replacement opener had to open Number 10 cans (think food storage/restaurant size canned green beans, corn, etc) easily.This item solved all of the above.It is easy to use.  Unlike my old unit, with this one I don't have to squeeze it shut or twist, twist, twist repeatedly in that brutal wrist wrecking motion.  I simply clamp the can into this opener, support underneath the can with one hand and crank away ( in a full arm motion) with the other.This opener, if you carefully lock the opener into the proper position, and support the can with your hand, easily opens the large cans.  The magnetic lid lifter arm is fully removable and works best to lift lids on smaller cans.  Even on the #10 cans it is capable of keeping the detached lid from sinking into the contents of the can.It is vital to mount this opener onto solid wood, not drywall.I was fortunate that I had an exposed hardwood cabinet side in my kitchen  that made the perfect spot to mount the opener.  When you install this into hard wood and exercise some common sense about not letting super heavy cans "dangle" from the opener it is plenty sturdy.(Note:  If you don't have exposed hardwood to work with you can mount a hardwood panel to the drywall with appropriate anchors and then mount opener to that panel.)Another plus is that it is truly a "swing-a-way" so if you are working with a location that could impede traffic (or for someone like me: cause you to crash into it as you fumble past) you can use the opener and then swing it to a nearly flat position back against the mounting wall.Cleaning the unit is easy if you stay on top of it by "opening" a damp paper towel to remove grime from the gears/wheel.  Follow that with "opening" a sheet of wax paper and you are golden.I use this opener multiple times every day and it glides right through it's job every time.
I have paid so many replacement fees and filed so many insurance claims, this so far has helped, even though we've only had it a  couple of days.

I like the look of this Power bank it has been finished off with a glossy black and chrome finish which is very easy to keep clean with a microfiber cloth which I used the pack on its first charge took for me just over 4 hours and when fully discharged I charged it overnight with the supplied 20 inch USB cable that comes supplied with for connecter plugs one of which fits the power bank for charging and when the bank is on charge it has four bright blue lights on the front of the bank all contained in a small round circle which also holds the power On/Off button but when you plug your phone or tablet in to the bank it will switch its self on so that is an extra bonus in my books and once charging each light will light up and flash in stages till the bank is fully charged.The Power bank weighs around 175g and is nearly 4 inches in height and 2 and a half inches wide with a depth of under 1 inch the USB bank has a power point for charging of the bank on the left hand side of the unit and has 2 USB slots on the top of the bank for charging of your phone and tablet or anything with a chargeable battery I have used it with my USB Diabetic blood meter.The Power Bank also come supplied with a nice velvet bag to keep it safe and secure while in transit.

We just moved into our first house, and in the last couple of months, I've purchased three brands of bookshelf speakers. In order, I've purchased a pair of Infinity Primus 150 speakers, a pair of small ($10 each on super sale) Pioneer S-HF21-LR speakers, and now I just opened a pair of Bose 201's I got with my credit card points.The first thing I noticed about the bose 201-V speakers was the build quality: almost as good as the $10 pioneers (the pioneers don't have exposed particle board, the bose speakers do).  There are no post-style connectors, just the cheap hole-and-lever type.  The Infinity 150's are built out of much better materials, have gold post-and screw style contacts, removable fronts, solid wood construction, internal dampening, nice feet, and wall mounting slots.The Bose 201-V's sound decent for their size, but they certainly don't sound as good as the Infinity 150s, which are almost half the price!  Specifically, I notice certain mid-range tones are exaggerated with the bose 201's.  It is almost as if the cabinets have some resonance frequency that sets off with a certain midtone.  The bass response also seems a little uneven, with certain bass drum notes showing up loudly, but other (higher) bass guitar notes almost absent.  The absence of certain bass notes is probably what bothers me the most.  I'm running a straight amp without any EQ.I got these speakers with my credit card points, and I'm thinking of selling them on ebay and buying something else.Check out stereophile for more speaker information.. they had a good article on the Infiniti Primus speakers.

Disclaimer: I received this product from the manufacturer in exchange for an honest reviewWith items like this, I find myself wondering exactly how to approach my review, so I'll just go with simplicity and simply tell you what I know and have experienced.The first thing is the cloth jacketing. It really does look nice and has seemed to increase the durability of the cord. I have been running this cord through the ringer in a professional kitchen over the last week or so. This cord has faced a heavy hand (and more than a few boots and kitchen clogs), and has withstood them all admirably. In fact, I've noticed no real change whatsoever.Everything I've used this cord for (which has been an array of devices, all running to a portable wall charger, has taken the signal well, and seems to have charged a bit more quickly. That may be more about the charger, but either way, the cord has certainly done what it is supposed to do, and has worked well with a variety of phones, a Kindle Fire, several portable chargers (and the wall charger), a 360 Gamepad, and a few other devices.So, for looks, durability, and functionality, I have no problem giving this item 5 stars.
Just as expected. High impact plastic... Nice finish and clear pattern. Loved it!! Just what I wanted.  Don't know hat else to say.

These are true 6x8 speakers, not 5x7 with a 6x8 mounting frame.They include a factory install sealing gasket on the back side of the speaker frame for proper sealing.Sound quality is really good over stock oem speakers.I mounted mine the rear doors of my Ford Explorer EXT because the Rockford T1682 had major depth clearance issues.I was skeptical at first, while ordering, but it turns out they sound very good and can hold their own when paired with the T1682, which is mounted in the front doors.I have these speakers wired in parallel to the T1682 on a Precision Power PC450 amplifier (channels 1 and 2), which is around 75 watts at a 2 ohm load.They sound great and are not clipping when pushed hard in conjunction with the T1682, plus the mid-bass is down right outstanding too.I am very pleased.

Using with DD-WRT firmware to act as a wi-fi repeater for the past 3 months. I recently test-drove a pair of Bose Bluetooth headphones.  I was pleased by the clarity of the sound, but ASTOUNDED by the comfort.  Unfortunately, $250 is outside my indulge-on-a-whim budget, so I handed them back to the salesman and walked away.  When I went to Amazon to see if those headphones were any cheaper online, I stumbled across the Photive BTH3.  For $50, I figured it couldn't hurt to try them out.First, the one negative point: The only place I would ding the Photives are that they are not quite as comfortable as the Bose.  They're fine for a couple hours of continuous use, though.In all other areas, the headphones far exceeded expectations. The sound is almost identical to the pair costing 5 times as much.  The invisible microphone in the headset (and I really can't find it) is perfect for taking calls or using Siri.  The hard-sided case that the headphones come with are the perfect protection for when the they are in my backpack.  The battery life is absurdly good.  The range is also amazing -- I would say at least 40-50 feet, even through the floors and walls of my house.I'm recommending these to all my friends and family.

My wife and I drink a pot of tea almost every day. We had previously relied on a pot in the style of the classic Brown Betty with a third-party small basket that hangs on the top opening. That worked fine provided that you own a basket for steeping your tea.The advantage of this pot is that the basket comes included. As you can see in the photo, there is a small tab that sticks out from under the lid. It is bent so that it has enough tension to snap in, but it is very easy to remove.  One thing to consider is that when you remove the basket you had better have something to put it in. Even if you give it some shaking you will still have liquid slowly seep out of the leaves as the basket rests. We found that the lid from an old jar worked fine for this.Perhaps the most important element for a teapot is how well it pours. We have used some pots that are very elegant, but they tend to dribble down the spout unless you pour very aggressively. The stubby spout on this pot works well even when the pot is very full. I have never had any dribble problems even after using this pot about a dozen times. The handle certainly is not a classic shape, but it provides a nice, solid grip.The only negative I can think of is that the basket is very wide, even at the bottom. This makes it a little tricky to use if you like to get a second steeping out of your tea. We typically use 4 t of tea for a pot and then I will re-steep that in a mug later on. You need to have a fairly wide mug to fit this basket. You also will need to have something to cover it with so you don't lose all your heat during the re-steep.If someone wanted to get into brewing loose-leaf tea I would recommend this pot without any hesitation. I also would buy one of these if mine breaks. It is worth it if you drink loose-leaf tea.

This screen protector for the Bold 9700 fits nicely on the BB screen.  However, even though I thoroughly cleaned my screen with the included cloth, I still have one air bubble on the right side that I can't get out no matter how much a try and crease it out to the edge.  Also, along the left side I have a long air pocket but that could have been because I placed and replaced the left side of the screen a few times to get it aligned perfectly on my screen.  Hopefully the other screens don't have as many air pockets.  I don't know why the description of the item says "Anti-Glare" either, there is the same amount of glare without the screen protector on.  Also, if you look really closely there is a rainbow-like tint of vertical stripes on the protector.  When the BB screen is blank or off and there is a glare you can see the stripes.  It's not very noticeable unless you are looking for it, but I just wanted to point that out since I've never seen that before on other protectors.UPDATE 1/25/10: I just tried putting on a second protector and it did the same, there are air pockets all along the sides.  I think it is because the protector is too thick so it doesn't properly get out all of the air pockets.  Save your money and find another screen protector!

I have had my iPad for four years, and my iPhone for four years. The cables that came with them frayed. The iPhone cable frayed 2 years after purchase. I did not bend or twist the cable, so have no idea why that failed. The iPad cable frayed at the insertion site, and that is easier to explain. The iPad tends to lose it's charge faster, so I plug it in while using. I prefer to use the iPad on it's vertical stance, and that puts pressure on the insertion side of the cable. I would assume this happens frequently, so with all of Apple's expertise why are their cables not more resilient?I purveyed all of the cables that would fit Apple products, and found this OPSO USB Cable that had many positive reviews. It is 4 feet long, white in color. This has been specifically designed to connect to iPods, iPhones and iPads. It also meets Apple standards. It came well packaged, in an attractive box, instructions were enclosed. The price is very good and much less than you would find at the Apple store. I plugged my iPhone in, and it seemed to charge much more quickly than usual. Not sure how true this is so will charge all of my Apple products. A grey Velcro strap us attached to the cable for easy of coiling the cable when not in use. I am going to,purchase at least one more if these candles to use for travel. Very pleased with the cable, the price and so far, the ease of use.Recommended. prisrob 03-11-14

After using cheap hand mixers for years I finally decided to get off the wallet and buy something that would last more than a couple of years and at the same time make life in the kitchen a lot easier. I've had my eye on a KitchenAid mixer to complement the kitchen in my new home for almost 2 years now. I finally said enough is enough and bought the Onyx Black, Artisan Series 5-Quart Mixer.Immediately, chocolate chip cookies were not only easier to make, but tasted better. Ditto for a few of the bread recipes I've tried in the past. Great mashed potatos followed by a killer Belgian waffle batter were next and I continue to look for recipes of foods I never dared try in the past.This mixer has the guts and power that make any recipe a snap. I don't think there is a chore out there that this mixer couldn't stand up to. One thing that surprised me was how quiet this mixer is even when running on the higher settings.Cleanup is easy too. A quick wipe down with a damp towel is all that's needed. The attatchments and 5 quart bowl are dishwasher safe if you don't feel like cleaning by hand.As stated in other reviews, this KitchenAid is no lightweight at 20+ pounds so find a permanent spot for it in a prominent area of your kitchen. In my case, the island in the middle of the kitchen was a no brainer and brings instant compliments when company arrives.For those who are considering buying a stand mixer, there is no other choice. Buy the KitchenAid and you can't go wrong.

I like tea, so I have to say that this was pretty much an easy pick for me as far as Vine goes. Unless it's one of those teas where I don't mind it going cold (because it brings out other flavors in the tea), I really dislike my tea getting tepid. It's just not fun, especially when it's cold outside or when you've got a head/throat cold. That it's supposed to hold heat was one of the things I was looking forward to.I poured my water into the cup straight from my teakettle on the stove and I noticed that the sides of the mug did get pretty warm. I don't take my tea water as hot as some of my relatives do, so I'm a little worried that this might end up getting too hot for some to hold if you choose to use boiling water. (Although that would probably be a no-brainer, I know that some might use boiling water and burn their hands if they put them on the mug itself instead of on the handle.) The cup does hold heat incredibly well, so that the sides got warm didn't really bother me. A warm mug is one of the things I like about drinking hot things.What really sort of irritated me was that the teabag top just didn't work as wonderfully as I wanted it to. It did help keep the string from disappearing into the drink, but pulling the teabag out was just an awkward process. It wasn't too messy, but if my water had been as hot as it might have been otherwise, it would have been messy. The problem here is that heat travels through the rubber really easily and I had a hard time squeezing the water out without my fingers getting a little burnt. I know that there's not a lot that can be done about that, but it's just irritating. This also doesn't really help me much when it comes to the myriad of teabags that I have that don't have strings, which is unfortunate since the top is really the big star of the mug. It's otherwise a beautiful mug but the top is what the biggest selling point is.Overall though, this wasn't bad for the price (currently fourteen dollars). It's something I'd buy as a gift, although the lid is more of a novelty than something I'd find indisposable. I do have to admit that I find the thinness of the glass pretty unnerving. Although I adore a beautiful cup, I'm a fan of the thicker mugs with kitchy sayings on the side that won't break easily.

A new TV is a purchase I could have kept postponing forever - there is always a bigger/better/brighter model around the corner. Then came the opportunity to buy the Samsung series 6 with "wife approval", as she wanted a new living-room floor and didn't want to put the old entertainment center back on the new bamboo flooring.I did all the online comparison shopping for size and features, then went to a big box store to compare models firsthand. In a side-by-side comparison, the Samsung model had a sharper, crisper picture than the Aquos. Amazon had the best price (and free shipping) of any of the reputable etailers, so I purchsed from Amazon.Shipping happened a few days sooner than I was led to expect - no complaints there! Initial setup was easy - defaults out of the box were quite usable. PS3 in one HDMI port (for Blu-ray), DirecTv HD DVR in another HDMI port and I was set.The picture is INCREDIBLE compared to my old 27" CRT set. You can see the writing on golf balls, the individual pieces of clag on the Formula One tracks, the scuff marks on the baseballs, etc... I was just blown away, and still am every time I switch on the set. I should have bought one of these when they first came out - I wasted a year!!! No complaints here...well, maybe just one tiny one. Would it have cost Samsung THAT much more to have included wireless network instead of just cabled ethernet?

I got the phone refurbished for a little over half the original price with a case and screen protector. When it arrived, it had everything needed and activation was extremely easy. Before I purchased the phone, I read some reviews and saw that the phone had an old Android version. No problem, I did some research and I found some ways to make 3G speeds faster and I found some good roots. I rooted my phone to CM9 (CyanogenMod Mod 9, Android Ice Cream Sandwich or 4.0.4) and now it works so smooth and so fast (overclocked). A CM10 (CyanogenMod Mod 10, Android Jelly Bean or 4.1) root is in development for anybody wondering. The battery lasts me through the whole day, but some reviews say that the battery runs out a lot after a while so I bought two 2000 mAh batteries. Now, I have the perfect phone that I've always wanted.

I read all the reviews for this item, and the perception I had was that it was an ok tablet - nothing too fancy - that could do the basics.  That seemed just fine to me, as the plan was to leave it on my boat as a supplemental computer with which I could check email, read news, and watch movies.  No reason for an iPad or anything super expensive.When I received this unit, it was exactly as I imagined: nothing fancy, pretty slow, and with limited capabilities.  I could check my email, there was a good app for reading the news, and some of the gaming apps were ok too.  However, it was so slow and choppy.  I guess I expected it to run a bit more smoothly, but instead, it's constantly freezing.  And I understand, it's a Coby tablet for around $100, but honestly, it became to much of a pain to even turn on and use for a couple minutes.  It's job was supposed to be to act as a convenient way for me to catch the news and keep in contact with people while sitting on my boat (in the harbor - where there's wifi).  But it was anything but convenient really...And, as I mentioned, I wanted to watch movies... There is no app for watching movies!!  Even iPhones can get Netflix... The only thing I could do was download movies on my computer (illegally) and put them on a microSD card to view on the tablet at a later time...Bottom line: not worth it. I know it's only around $100, but don't even waste that!

This oven was well-rated by Consumer Reports, so I bought it after comparing it to a few others that were quite a bit pricier. The controls are smooth and easy to figure out. It comes to temperature quickly, and the timer and alert sounds are good. The design is clean (I think it looks better in person than in the pictures), and speaking of clean, cleaning the inside and outside is  easy. I haven't specifically noted the convection cooking feature, but everything we've toasted, cooked or just heated up has come out nicely.The biggest downsides are no real broiler function, and the oven thermostat is not continuously variable, so you have to select from 180, 250, 320, 400 or 470 degrees. If your recipe calls for 350 or 375, you're going to have to choose the next closest temperature and adjust the time.... I'd say that makes this a poor oven for baking, but you should be fine for more typical toaster oven cooking.For the price, I would recommend this toaster oven.

At first I was skeptical because this product has many GOOD reviews but it also has many BAD and very bad reviews.Sadly I'm going to be another one of the bad ones.I'll make it quick and painless1. This thing is loud as F*&# -- even on the lowest setting.. on the lowest setting it can be heard from the opposite side of the house and it's still louder then the stand up model Honeywell I have. At the highest setting this thing sounds like an aircraft carrier taking off.. no joke -- I can't even hear the other air purifiers I have at the opposite end of the house when I'm sitting right next to them when this thing is blowing air in another room... It's LOUD- You can hear it through 2 closed doors like it's nothing ...2. The pre-filter / filter itself smell like rubber / manufacturing -- the smell isn't horrible .. but it's strong enough that when the thing is blowing you can smell it.. it almost has a musty smell... it doesn't make the air smell fresh .. it makes it smell like a manufacturing plant.3. If you are going to buy a replacement filter for the thing which they recommend you have to buy 3 to fit the 1 that it comes with. They do not sell an exact replacement they have split it into three parts -- and each one costs 80 bucks... so it's going to cost you more than the cost of a new unit to get replacement filters ( which they recommend you replace every year ... -- yes you can buy the third party / aftermarket ones here on amazon for about 80-90 bucks but if I'm buying a product I expect the replacement parts to be OEM ... maybe that's just me ...4. The power usage on this bad boy is just downright ridiculous. It makes sense judging from the amount of sound it makes ... but this thing on high pulls the same amount as a toaster or as my vacuum cleaner-- I recorded it on high to use about 800 WPS ( watts per second ) at medium about 500 and on low about 300 -- This all adds up when you keep this thing running all year long which you are going to need to do. My honeywell standup model uses 80 WPS on high 50 on medium and 30 on low and kicks out plenty of air. (( I'm not going to get into the math on transferring WPS into cost per year as it all depends on how long you are running it for and I have a long spreadsheet of every item in my house and how much it uses but just know that this thing is power hungry and you're going to be paying at least another 30-40 bucks on your electricity bill if you run this thing on high all month.Overall I'm very disappointed in this product... if you are deaf or don't mind the sound of living in a wind tunnel .. then by all means .. by this ... but if you're like me and you prefer to be able to hear things in your house ( especially when the wife is calling ... find something else ) .... then again .. maybe some of you wouldn't mind something to cover up the wife's / husbands calls ... food for thought... :P

I purchased this in May, and liked it well enough to buy a second one, only I never got around to it. My thought was that even though this one goes for hours of talk time between charges, there are times when I travel that charging is not an option. My thought was "backup". The sound quality is good. Generally everyone was happy on the other end which is not always the case with bluetooth headsets. At my end, it was able to go louder than most. That comes in handy in airports or other noisy environments. The reversible ear clip not only works well, but unlike some sets I have owned, it fits flat and rotates to work for either ear. You don't have to mess around with a bag of spare parts. It's flat enough to go in a shirt pocket without protruding too much, so dealing with it when not in use is good.So why only 4 stars? By October, it was deader than a doornail. I normally get a year to two years before a headset just dies. This one was a lot shorter than that. And while it may not matter to some, the charger connection is a proprietary round connector I have not experienced before. That means you may need to plan for additional purchases for car or AC adaptors. Other sets I have owned have had USB mini connectors which are the same as my Blackberry and Android phones. Also, the one I purchased in May came as some kind of OEM bundle, with a car charger included, and one AC adaptor with detachable USB end, so it can also work off the side of a laptop. I do like this well enough to replace it with the same model, pending whatever Plantronics does for warranty. If they do replace it, I will have the two I initially wanted. The set that I just purchased this week arrived yesterday from Cellular Boys, via Amazon Prime. It had only a conventional AC charger that does not include the USB attachment, nor car charger. It was also about $8 less than the first one, so it's still a good deal.I know this is a lot of words over a simple headset, but when you make a living talking to people all day, these things do matter. I like  this set.

The Eboot 5.1 amp 3 USB port car charger is one of the highest quality and highest output car chargers on the market.Pros:-5.1 amps of output power (this means that when all the ports outputs combined they add up to a max output of 5.1 amps)-1 of the USB ports, labeled “2.1A”, has a max output of 2.1 amps which is great for charging tablets like an iPad Air (this port is designed for Apple devices like an iPad but will still work with other tablets and smartphones)-1 of USB ports, , labeled “2.0A”, has a max output of 2.0 amps which is great for charging tablets like a Samsung Galaxy Tab 10.1 (this port is designed for Android devices like Galaxy Tabs but will still work with iPads, smartphones and other tablets)-1 of the USB ports, labeled “1.0A”, has a max output of 1.0 amps which is great for charging smartphones like an iPhone 5/5s (this port is designed for all smartphones like an iPhone 5/5s or Galaxy S4)-Comes in 2 cooling looking color combos-LED status lights indicates that the car charger’s USB ports are ready for use and it is not bright to light up the car (in other words, it will not distract the driver)-Tight fit in the cigarette lighter socket which means it will not slide out easily during use-Tested on a car ride and it worked great for charging an iPad Air, an iPhone 5 and a Galaxy S4-Small for its very high output of 5.1 amps-Protects against over-heating, over-charging, over-current and short circuits-Overall, this is a high quality car charger that has a very high output that works very wellNo cons that I can find about this car charger.My wishes:-Add multiple LED’s so the USB ports are visible in the dark (LED do not light up the car, just the USB ports)-Make it have a 7.2 amp output, so 3 tablets, like 3 iPad’s, can charge all at once at full speedThis is an excellent car charger and it works great. I highly recommend this wall charger!I was supplied a sample for test and evaluation, for free to keep, and I promised that my review is fair and honest.

I purchased this case in white and put on my white iPhone about a week ago. Here is what I love about this case:* It looks clean and anything that gets on the case wipes off easily.* The rubber around the sides provides grip and seems like it would provide adequate protection if dropped.* The back appears to be well protected too, and doesn't scratch easily.What I don't like:* Other reviews mentioned a gap between the black rubber and the bottom of the phone near the charging port. It is there, but it is very small. I tend to want everything to be perfect, so I had a hard time with this initially. It seems to have gotten smaller in the time that I've had the case (or maybe I've gotten used to it), but now it doesn't bother me. I wouldn't let this stop you from buying the case.Overall, this is a great value for an attractive, protective case.UPDATE:After two weeks of use, I removed the case to replace the screen protector. Inside the case, there was a lot of crumbs/dust, etc. There are holes on the back of the case (for a wrist strap, maybe?) and dirt gets inside the case through these holes. Now, I have a three-year-old and a one-year-old, so my phone may be exposed to more crumbs, dirt, etc. than most peoples'. If you don't find Goldfish crackers in your pocket along with your phone, this may not be a problem for you :), but it worries me that it could scratch the phone.

Samsung's official case for their Galaxy S4 is without a doubt one of my favorites.Personally, I tend to like my cases on the slimmer side, so this works quite well. It covers all around the sides of the phone with flexible TPU and the back has an attached (non-removable) plastic plate that's probably a little thicker than it needs to be, however I'm fine with the extra protection.The dark blue case works very well with the "Black Mist" color phone. The TPU part is the same color as depicted in the pictures, but the back plate is a lighter, almost shimmering dark blue that I'm very much a fan of.The phone is very secure in the case, doesn't move but is also relatively easy to remove, if need be.The edges of the TPU part have a very slight lip to protect your phone if you ever decide to place it face-down but don't wrap around the front too much. WIth that said, it works almost perfectly with my Spigen SGP Glas.tr case.However, if you like cases with a little added grip, you might want to consider other options. Both the plastic and TPU parts of the case are pretty slick, which is nice for sliding in and out of pockets, but my increase your chances of dropping it. I haven't personally had this problem, and I'm very much fine with the way that the phone feels.5/5, definitely recommended.

Decent low-end webcam with good, clear instructions. Images are about as sharp and detailed as those from my 27" iMac's built-in iSight camera, and being able to aim the IceCam2 in any direction is a big plus.One thing you might easily overlook: the tiny focusing ring. If you don't adjust it, you're going to get a very blurry picture! While manually focusing is a bit of a nuisance compared to using an autofocus webcam such as the similarly pricedRocketfish 2.0MP model, it has one big advantage: you can focus to within about two millimeters of the lens, making it possible to use the IceCam2 as a low-powered microscope. For example, you can fill the frame with the pupil of your eye, examine an ant close up, or magnify your fingertip until it's easy to count the ridges and whorls of your fingerprint.Other notes: You can use this with FaceTime, but only if you first launch iChat or a similar video app and use that to "take over" your Mac's internal iSight camera. Once that's done, launch FaceTime and it should default to the IceCam2 camera. If you launch FaceTime first, it'll always use the internal camera and ignore the IceCam2.As with all webcams, having plenty of light makes a big difference in the image quality. Also, I recommend installing the iGlasses utility (shareware) to permit adjusting the camera's color balance and contrast to taste.All in all, this is a good little camera for the price, and good-looking as well.

This is the only charger you will ever need, it can charge four devices at once, need I say more!There are two USB ports that are used for tablets or phablets, and two ports for smartphones or smaller mobile devices. This charger uses a power cord and does not plug directly into the wall like most. The power cord is similar to that of a laptop cord with the two holes in it. I find this to be a plus, it allows the charger to be easily set on a desk or nightstand.Once plugged up, there is a subtle blue power indication light. By using this charger, I was able to clear my nightstand of any other. I have plenty of mobile devices like most of you out there, so that meant lots of different chargers next to the bed. Since receiving this charger, those days are over.I use this single charger on my Samsung Galaxy Tab 2 10.1, Samsung Galaxy Note 2, Metwatch (Smartwatch), and LG headset all at the same time. Now all of my devices are all in one place being charged, which is much easier.This is a must have charger if you have multiple mobile devices!CONTENTS:RAVPower 4 Port USB Wall ChargerDetachable Power CordFEATURES:Input: 100-240VOutput: DC 5V 6.2AUSB Output Ports: 2x(5V/2.1A) & 2x(5V/1A), 4 USB Ports TotalI was supplied a free sample for the purpose of review and evaluation, and promised an unbiased review.

I bought this sound bar because it seems like the perfect fit for my TV stand to hold it without sticking out of the sides and for my small living room. I also wanted a sound bar with a sub woofer on it or included. This definitely did all of that.It fits perfectly on the TV stand and the sound is huge on it! The bass is almost too much. For TV I have to turn the bass down to -3 just so it doesn't overwhelm the treble. Beats out the TV speakers by a huge margin, not even close. The treble is clear, highs and mids and base all are clearly distinct. I use the 3D audio on it too, it picks up more sounds with it on than leaving it off, not sure why.The bluetooth connects easy to my older Samsung Note, music sounds great on it. Haven't tried the radio yet.The one con about this is the bright blue LCD display on the front when it's on. They chose to make it bright blue background with blue thin font on it so it's really hard to read it unless you're pretty close. It also gets annoying to have to see the bright blue LCD lit up when watching TV in a movie setting of darkness. I had to put some black duct tape as a flap over it to hide it when I don't want to see it. Be nice if it turned off when you weren't pressing buttons.Overall, I love it so far and would recommend it for any size TV, this thing has a lot of power and huge sound.

I have to admit that the first thing that struck me about this headset was how attractively designed it was.  At the same time, it's large compared to my BlueAnt Z9, seems similar to the old Plantronics Voyager.  If you like a headset that goes over the ear and feels like it will just stay there, while being quite comfortable, then this one is definitely worth a try.Pairing with an Android phone was very easy, took less than 10 seconds (I think) after glancing through the easy start guide.  The sound quality is excellent, even in a somewhat noisy environment -- apparently the noise cancellation does work quite well.The controls are easy to use: fortunately, they didn't go with the "one button for everything" that sounds clever but is impossible to use.  There are separate buttons, well placed, for controlling power, volume, and call answer/disconnect.  There is one multi-function button (under the boom) that is a mute button during a call, for streaming music pause/play, or for some voice action (speech recognition) commands at other times.  If your phone supports it, you can also use the call control button to initiate voice dialing.Another cool feature is call announcement: an incoming call is announced, with caller name (if supported by your phone), and you can say "answer" or "ignore".  Or you can hit the call button if you're not the chatty type.I also liked the charger: there is a magnetic coupling between the charging end of the headset and the charger, so they fit together securely.  Nice thoughtful design there.  Note however that the charging cable is very very short, just about 3 inches long.  Go figure.If you want to find out more about the features & usage & customization, you can go to these links for PDF docs on the Plantronics website:[...][...]

I really really REALLY wanted this to work.  I’ve tried a couple of other wireless FM modulators before – and that static made them useless.  I’ve lately been depending on 1990’s technology (tape deck converter) to play music from my iPhone through the speakers in my 2005 Pilot – so when I saw this – I thought it was worth another chance.Well – I tried everything I could think of and tried every suggestion I could find online to beat away the static monster – to no avail.  I adjusted the music settings on my iPhone.  I moved my phone as far away as the cord would allow from the engine/radio.  I wrapped the modulator in a towel (!) trying to shield it from static.  I tried every FM station that I could find.  I tried playing the music with and then without using the Clear Scan app.  Volume up on my phone and down on the radio - and vice versa. I found ONE position where the music was actually clear…if my daughter held my phone up in the air from the backseat and never moved…then the music was pretty clear.With a 2005 automobile – I don’t have an AUX option to plug my phone directly into the stereo – so I guess I will have stay in the 90’s for now.

These Blue Rigger cables, tested with the same hdmi input and output devices as the other cables we tried, did not cause any audible or visible problems, and are recommended.

I first was introduced to Zero Water at a Sam's Club where they were holding a demonstration of the product. After tasting the water and seeing the results of the comparisons with tap VS bottle water VS Zero water we were impressed. I spoke to the representatives there who stated that the filters would last 4 - 6 months, depending on how bad the tap water in the area was. Even though I didn't expect it to really last 6 months I figured if it lasted three it would be less expensive to replace the filters than buying bottle water. So we bought a set.Flash forward 3 1/2 weeks later and the filters were done and the water had developed a strong fish odor. In talking to the company they said the 4-6 months was only an estimate. Obviously a really bad one. At the time a set of four filters were being sold at $54, meaning I was spending $27 every 3 1/2 weeks on filters, or over $400 per year on filters. This was way over what I spend on bottle water. Add to the fact that if you wait too long to change the filters the fish odor comes back and you have to basically boil the unit using a little hydrogen peroxide (as instructed by the company) to get rid of the smell.The reason I rated this two stars was that when the filters are working the water really is excellent. You can really taste the difference. But financially it is not worth the cost or the effort. Plus when you have a company knowingly providing false data on their product I have to say pass on it. Too expensive, too time consuming, and not enough integrity from the company to have me recommend this in any way.

This Nikon 40mm f/2.8G AF-S DX Micro lens produces sharp pictures and great color and contrast. It is also perfect for portrait and many other general purposes. It's biggest feature I think is the macro capability (1:1) hence called Micro lens (Nikon terms for Macro lens). Overall, this is a very versatile lens. On a non full frame DSLR (such as D40, D40x, D60, D5000, D80, D90, D200, D300 etc), this 40mm focal length is equivalent to about 60mm which close to a normal lens which is 50mm (normal as to being close to a person eye viewing angle perspective).This lens should be able to replace the 35mm f/1.8 (with the extra macro flexibility) unless you are specifically using this for low light environment and would need the extra speed of the 35mm f/1.8 lens (and a second reason if you really need the extra 5mm wider angle).The biggest drawback for this macro lens is actually (and sort of ironically) when you need to do professional macro work, this lens is just ok (since at the closest distance which is about 50mm between the lens and the subject, and often block important lights to the subject. Additionally some subjects will not be easy to get too close to (insect, birds, butterfly, etc). Basically, more professional macro work, the 105mm or 200mm macro lenses would be a better choice/investment.Another drawback of this lens is as you focused closer (which is true for all macro lens), the lens becomes slower, and at the closest focus 35mm (1:1), the maximum aperture is f/4.2.This lens has AF-S feature which is auto focus system that is internal to the lens which means this lens auto-focus will also work on cameras that doesn't have internal auto-focus motor such as D40, D40x, D60, D3000, D3100, D5000 and D5100.This lens also has manual override on auto-focus mode (M/A mode), which will allow us to change the focus without having to change the mode to manual mode (this is pretty standard to most Nikon newer lenses but it's quite new for Nikon prime lens series)This lens doesn't have image stabilization (VR), but that is kind of expected as Nikon also doesn't include VR on their other prime lens (35mm and 50mm). It would certainly be nice to have VR (for longer exposure hand held operation, and for people with less stable photography technique) but it will probably increase the size, weight and cost of this lens.Here are the summary of pros and cons for this Nikon 40mm f/2.8G AF-S lens:Pros:1. AF-S (internal focus, and very silent. focus works on D40, D40x, D60, D3000, D3100, D5000 and D5100 cameras)2. M/A mode (manual focus override available on autofocus mode)3. Relatively fast lens (f/2.8), though not as fast as 35mm f/1.8 or 50mm f/1.84. Very sharp pictures5. Good for most macro shots6. Great for portrait7. Bokeh is good8. Great focal length (40mm). About 60mm equivalent which is close to a normal lensCons:1. Being prime lens, you need to move your feet a lot to adjust/compose2. No VR (VR will be useful for taking hand held shots on low light)3. Not designed for full frame cameras (FX or Film) where there will be significant light fall-off.4. Though it is a macro lens, it is not great for serious macro work and/or if it is done in low light (105mm or longer macro lens works a lot easier/better since you can be further away from the subject)5. As you focus closer, the lens slows down (the maximum aperture gets smaller, and at 1:1, the maximum aperture is f/4.2)Bottom line: This lens is very versatile and I think most people should be able to use this lens as all around lens (and also probably replace use the 35mm f/1.8 and 50mm f/1.8 unless speed is the essence). Being a macro lens, this 40mm f/2.8, will allow you to take most of the macro shots that you need (except when the light is low or when it is hard to get very close to the subject).Happy Photographing!Sidarta Tanu

The package on this product says it will heat 200 square feet. I live in a 900 square feet apartment and along with a tiny little $12 space heater, my apartment is nice and toasty!  I do have my sliding patio door open about a fingers width for ventilation and it's even heating that large room despite the heat loss.I'm thankful I bought this a few months ago as part of my emergency preparedness. Each year I have my gas shut off (on purpose) to save $ when spring gets here. Well, apparantly spring wasn't quite here yet. We've got several inches of ice and snow and more on the way.  I'm glad to have this product. We'll stay warm and toasty.  (I won't take the risk of running it overnight, though, even though I do have carbon monoxide detectors in two rooms.)

The Ailet clear and flexible case for the iPhone 5 and 5s is perfect for those who need a little protection, desire a simple case, and want to be able to see and appreciate the iPhone’s clean design.The case is very easy to install and remove.  It simply stretches over the phone and falls into place.  It has a secure fit without being too tight.The bezel that runs around the front of the case is very slim and does not interfere with the screen or the home button.  There are cut outs on the case’s bottom to accommodate the iPhone’s ports, speaker, and microphone.  Another cut out is on the side for the mute switch, with one on the back that accommodates the camera and flash.  The lock button and the volume buttons have covers molded into the case that do not interfere with the buttons’ operation.In use the case adds decent grip to the iPhone with it’s rubber construction and offers moderate protection.  This is not a case for someone who will be giving the iPhone to a young child to play with, or someone who tends to drop their iPhone a lot.  It works best for those who are careful with their phones but want some protection from the wear and tear of daily life and the occasional fall.  A little bit of dust works its way under the case with use, but the case has not discolored or cracked in any way.All in all… the perfect case for those who like to keep it simple.(I received a complimentary sample of this product from the manufacturer in exchange for an unbiased review.)

The only problem is that you're used to using other openers that look somewhat like this and this just doesn't work the same way.  So your habits formed from decades of opening cans with regular openers foul you up.  Once you're comfortable with how this works, you won't want to use a regular opener again.The tricky part is getting started.  The opener blocks your view of the contact point so you have to learn what it feels like when you have it in the right position.  But you don't break anything if you're holding it wrong - it just won't open the can.Once you have the initial cut made, just keep turning the knob.  It doesn't FEEL like you're opening the can, but you (probably) are.  Again, you're used to how regular can openers feel in use and this doesn't feel the same way so it fools you.  As you complete the opening you'll feel the difference in resistance - that's when it's done.I usually use the built in "grabber" to take the lid off then.  The edge can be a little slippery and the contact between the lid and the can's contents has a bit of suction to it so the grabber is the easiest way to pop the lid.The first time I tried, it took me almost a minute to get started.  The next time about 30 seconds.  To practice, after emptying the can I tried opening the bottom of the empty can.  The more you use it, the easier it gets.As someone who usually eats a single serving out of 2 serving cans, I appreciate how well the opened lid slips back on the can so I can store the rest in the fridge for a day or two without rummaging for some foil or plastic wrap.  And I really appreciate the time saved in cleanup because the cutting edge on this opener doesn't get dirty!

I bought this mixer in black on January 9 and received January 12, 2010. Today, I planned to use it for the first time to whip cream for a pie. I plugged it in and left the room momentarily. About 10 minutes later, I heard a crackling noise, went into the kitchen and found the mixer on fire, burning from underneath(not from the outlet or plug)and my wood baker's rack shelf that it sat on burning, both items having visible flames. Fortunately, we were home and awake when this happened. The mixer had not been previously used nor plugged in, nor did I get the opportunity to use it.I shudder at the thought of what could have happened had I left this mixer plugged in all of the time, or if we'd been asleep or away from home. Since the mixer purchase I had bought two other Cuisinart electrical appliances, one of which requires that it be plugged in continually. Now I'm afraid of them, too.This mixer will be going back immediately and I'll choose another brand. I don't yet know what that will be since I had done so much research prior to choosing this one. I guess it could have happened with any appliance, but somehow, whatever caused this got by the quality control  department. I'd rate this lower than 1 star if it were possible.

If you're willing to spend a lot of money, of course there are better headphones out there, but you can't compare these to a $200 set.  Or can you?The best things about these headphones besides their great performance to price ratio come from the well thought out design.The cord material is durable and supple--so it's unlikely to get kinked or tangled (which would shorten the life of the headphone set).The cord is just long enough without being too long.  Whomever designed this realized that an unnecessarily longer cord would get caught on things and be a pain in the butt to wrap up.The wind up case is nice, but these have been around a long time.Best of all is the volume control right where the chord splits for your left and right earphones.  It's a lot easier to find than if it were elsewhere; it has a nice range so that you don't find yourself needing to pull out your mp3 player to get it louder or softer than the line controller allows; and it slides instead of being indexed, allowing for even the slightest adjustment.BOTTOM LINE: I thought I was just buying the cheapest set of decent headphones I could find, and ended up with a real quality item I'll likely be using for years to come.Sony never disappoints.

I am a coffee lover.  I love it in all forms and drink it every day and usually all day.  We use a 300.00 coffee maker multiple times a day so we're no strangers to pricey, quality equipment.This is not one of those things.  This is the cheapest piece of garbage I've ever paid money for.  The white plastic top part is very flimsy, cheap and mine came with the bottom all scratched up and dirty.  There is NO LID even though you have to leave this sitting out for 12 hours (which the info conveniently omits) and it takes a whopping TWELVE OUNCES of ground coffee to use this thing (also conveniently omitted from the available info).The glass is very thin and lightweight.  I expected to break it just any moment any time I touched it.  The filters are, disappointingly enough, the sturdiest thing in the package.I am currently watching my first pot get all clogged up.  I used a very coarse grind (the same one I use for my french presses), followed every last nit picky step in the instructions (and you do have to be VERY nit picky when setting this up, the directions mention several times if you don't follow the instructions to the letter you'll cause a clog and it won't work correctly) and STILL the coffee isn't draining from the pitcher unless I stand there (as the instructions instructed me to) with the handle end of a table knife and constantly scrape the grounds off the filter so the coffee can drain through.RIDICULOUS!  This thing is way too complicated, way too cheap and I utterly fail to see how it's gotten so many great reviews.  The only thing I can figure is what Starbucks' success proves - most people have no idea what good coffee is, much less how to make it.I am returning this thing as soon as the wasted water, coffee and time finish screwing me over and I've rinsed it out and boxed it back up.  It's going right back to Amazon because this thing was worth maybe 5.00, but sure as hell not 40.00.

Between everyone in our family, we have about 8 USB-to-lightning cables.  About half are Amazon, half are Apple.  We've had to throw out two in the last month - both Apple cables.  You can look at the photos and see that the Apple cables don't have as much plastic and strain relief near the connector, which is probably why they break there.  On the negative side, my daughter says the amazon cable won't work with her hi-tech I-phone protector, but it works OK with everything else.

We have had this coffee maker for over a month now and have used it every day during that time.  We are very pleased with the brewer overall and would recommend it to anyone considering a K-cup brewer.Pros: filter for the water tank, nice stainless steel look, backlit water tank, adjustable brew temperatures, auto shut-off, different brew sizes (or coffee strengths).Cons: a bit on the noisy side when heating or pumping the water to the heating element.  You can cut down on some of the noise if you remove the drip catch basin thing, by doing that your empty coffee cup won't rattle when the brewer is starting up.  After I am done brewing I just put the drip basin back (I use a tall travel mug so it works out better for me that way anyhow).  pricey.We have tried using our own grounds with the do it yourself reusable k-cup that comes with the brewer, it works well, but its so much nicer to pop in a fresh k-cup and clean up is a snap.  We have even used a green tea bag and it has brewed green tea up nicely.

I purchased the Cook N Home popper to replace a Wabash Whirley Popper to roast coffee beans.  The Cook N Home costs about $8 more than the Whirley Popper, but it's worth it.  The Cook N Home is made in China, but the construction is superior to the Whirley Popper as follows:  1) it has metal hand-crank gears (not plastic), 2) it has clamps on both lids (instead of one side only) and 3) so far, the crank hasn't 'locked up' forcing me to crank backwards to free it up - which was a chronic problem, I found, with the Whirley Popper.  Even the wood on the Cook N Home is nicer looking than the wood used on the Whirley Popper.  That said, the lower crank gear on the Cook N Home I received was seated a bit too low to achieve optimal performance.  Fortunately, the paddle connecting-rod easily unscrews from the lower crank gear allowing you to install a small shim washer.  In my case, I had a 2 mm washer that did the trick, but if you don't have it on hand & you wanted to tweak the lower gear, you could easily find a washer at any hardware store.  The Cook N Home was well-packaged and arrived promptly via UPS from Amazon.

This is a nice looking, well made piece. It holds a TON of stuff and is sturdy. (I will try to upload pics.) The trays/cushions are nice, sturdy, and fit well. The straps/handles aren't as bad as I thought they would be.I bought this to replace our beveled glass coffee table since our daughter is becoming mobile, and it doubles as a toy box. I had read a previous review, so I knew the sides were not solid wood or anything, but it is much more solid than I had anticipated. The inside is lined with black fabric. One drawback: this thing SMELLS!!!! I had to air it out in the garage for a couple of days because it was giving me a headache, and now there is still a faint, but tolerable, odor (like varnish). We've only had it for a week or so, so I am hoping the smell goes away over time.This thing came in a big box and just needed the feet screwed on. For once I found a keyboard that can take a real beating. The keys are large enough and spread out just far enough that I don't have to reach from one end to the other to hit the desired key or key combination. Since it's a mechanical keyboard I get the tactile feedback from not only the sound of the key when tapped but the feel is right as well. Being disable with limited sensation in my hands and fingers these traits are very important. When typing I use a special splint that acts as an extension of my fingers and has a rubber tip on it. That tip doesn't slide off the keys like it has on other keyboards I've owned.The white backlighting is great and not so bright that it becomes a distraction. The multi-media keys work quite well, even with Pandora. Gaming mode makes it easy to switch back and forth depending on what I'm doing. On many keyboards I'd accidentally bump the Windows key and my games would minimize at the worst times.It does take up two USB ports but with my system I have plenty and it allows to use the keyboards USB port which I have a simple USB Razor mouse hooked up and it works fine. I'd say that for me Logitech has made a keyboard that works extremely well.

PROS*It' a great, small size and will fit easily into your kitchen.*It has what seems like a very short cord though it's three pronged for extra grounding.*It heats up fairly quickly; the sides get warm but still touchable even on the highest setting of 450 however the top gets much warmer so you'll want to be careful not to touch it and to keep it away from other surfaces.*It has a timer that can be set for anywhere from 10 to 30 minutes and it alarms you when the time is up unless you choose the `stay on' feature.*There are bake, broil and toast settings.*It comes with a detachable crumb tray, a baking rack and a small baking sheet.*The temperature gauge is accurate though it might run on the cool side but not by much.CONS*A regular, family size 11" pizza will not fit in this oven though 4 slices of regular bread fit snugly.*It doesn't heat evenly though I've yet to find an oven, especially a toaster oven, that does.  You'll need to keep turning the food as it cooks.*Though the lid, which opens out and down, closes easily there's not a snap to tell you it's in place so you'll need to be conscious of making sure you have it completely closed.GENERAL INFORMATION*The settings are in English and French.*It arrives protectively packed.*An instruction manual is included yet the design functions are intuitive.

You know what's awesome about this toaster oven? It's not trying to kill me.My Black and Decker Toast-r-Oven was basically a flame thrower, which would be awesome in ANY other place except my kitchen and maybe a pre-school. So I got this Proctor-Silex/Hamilton Beach Compact Toaster oven as a replacement because the fire thing was starting to concern me, for some reason. So far this has not caught on fire. Therefore I am happy. You know what else makes me happy? Toast. I love toast. I definitely love it more than you do, not that it's a competition or anything. When was the last time you said "I love toast"? Probably never. What I'm trying to say is that I'm an authority on toast. If toast were a country, then I would be prime minister. Maybe even dictator, but the nice kind. I would give everyone toast and a variety of jams. Strawberry, apricot, marmalade. Whatever you want. In return you have to do my laundry and give me money. Not a big deal.Pros:1. It is affordable. A toaster oven for about $20! That's ludicrous. You'd think that it wouldn't work. You'd think that for $20, it'd be a cardboard box with a light bulb and maybe a rack. But, no! It's legit. It's even made out of metal.Now, you may be swayed by the $200 Cuisinart toaster. Sure, go ahead and buy it, Dr. Moneybags. That is like the Lambo of toaster ovens. But unfortunately I don't have $200 to spend on a machine that makes bread crunchy. I rather spend it on other things. My co-worker just got a plastic elephant watering can and it's pretty badass. I may get it, and guess what? I don't even have plants. See, if I spent more than $20 on my toaster oven, I wouldn't even consider getting this plastic elephant watering can. And my life would be ruined.2. It is compact. If you have a small kitchen, then this is a good choice. If you have a small refrigerator and small furniture and small dishes, then this toaster will match your kitchen.3. The labels are in English and in French. So if you have French-speaking guests, they will not be confused as to how to operate the toaster oven. They'll be like, oui oui, j'aime toast and you'd be like, yo también! As you know, toast brings people of all colors and cultures together.4. The timer ticks and dings. This is a pro only because my last toaster oven didn't ding. The only way you knew the toast was done was when it went up in flames.Cons:It's small. The scientists at Proctor-Silex/Hamilton Beach were not kidding when they said it was compact. If you like GIGANTIC slices of bread or MEDIUM slices of bread, then you can only toast one slice at a time. This is irritating, especially if you like to eat gigantic or medium sized toast in pairs.If you prefer regular sized bread, and you are a family of >1, then this might be a pain because you can only toast two pieces of bread at a time. If you are a family of '1 but you eat as much toast as a family of >1, this machine might also be inconvenient. So my suggestion is to eat tiny pieces of bread.
