Recently I bought new DVR but didn't like the remote that came with it.  The biggest issues with that remote were 1) buttons I use most (pause/play, forward, rewind, stop) were tiny and 2) the buttons weren't arranged logically.  So, when looking for a universal remote, I came across the Logitech Harmony 300.When compared to compatiblity and ease of programming verus the typical universal remotes which have numeric codes to enter or continuous clicking to power button the difference is night and day.  The two strongest points of the Harmony is that 1)the remote programming is done on computer (online via their database of devices) and if your device is not included and you have the original working remote, 2) there's a command teaching capability where you line up your remote behind the harmony remote and then press the button you want and reassign that command to the harmony remote.In my case, the remote was not found on their database so I used option 2 to map out the harmony remote with commands from my orginal remote.  The remote contains a "Watch TV" button which gets programmed to turn on both my DVR and TV with that button which is nice as that saves me from extra clicking each time to turn on the device separately.  Additionally, the Harmony 300 has five user defined keys (numbered 1 through 5). For example, I use these defined keys to retain button commands like page up, page down, input source from my old remote.Pros:----- Easy to program either by Logitech's database of devices or the "teaching" function- buttons are logically organized- larger pause/play, stop, fwd, rewind buttons than on my old remote- "Watch TV" button is nice to turn on more than one device at same time- not expensive- runs on 2-AA batteries (should last longer than most remotes that uses only AAA batteries)Cons:----- no color contrast between remote and buttons.  Remote is black, buttons are dark gray with primarily white markings.There was one feature I wished for, but the remote didn't have. That is the ability to store several button presses and to store those presses as a single command to assign as a function on the remote.  For example, my old DVR had the ability to skip forward 30 seconds at the touch of one key to skip past commercials.  My new DVR doesn't have this feature.  I was hoping to simulate this feature on this remote, but unfortunately to string button presses together in the teaching mode is not possible with this remote.Overall, the Logitech Harmony 300 is a great replacement universal remote which I know will make my viewing experience more enjoyable.Update (10/6/12): I missed to skip forward 30 seconds feature too much that I ended up getting a remote that has macro capabilties.  Here's a link to that item:URC A6 Pre-Programmed and Learning Universal Remote Control for up to 6 A/V Components. With this other remote I was able to simulate the skip forward I had missed.Yet, if your needs do not require macro programming, then this remote is still a great remote replacement.

Expoimaging makes high quality accessories, and this was no exception... I added this to my small Flashbender, and it drastically changes your on-camera flash when the two are used together.The attachment is velcro on each side, a small velcro tab at the top, and a front tab that goes under your Flashbender's strap, onto your flash.  This accessory is just a bit narrower than the flashbender, so when you attach it, it automatically makes a hollow area that ends up above the flash.  There are some gaps at the top and bottom after it's all put together, but no light will leak directly onto your subject.  It's solid on the flash, I didn't have any issue whatsoever with it slipping off or feeling flimsy.The end result is a very pleasing soft light for your subject.  The white balance doesn't change, you just get a much more pleasing lit subject.  The light is diffused and just as importantly, is quite a bit more off-axis.  You will get slightly more shadows under eyebrows, but not much.Highly recommended if you own a Flashbender to attach it to!

I have been carrying my Lowepro CompuDaypack bag for several months and could not be happier. I have a Pelican case for carry-ons, and a Tenba 623-343 for carrying everything I own should I need to, but this Lowepro bag is my daily shooting bag. I don't need a quick access pouch, because I use the BlackRapid RS7 sling that gets the camera out of the way and protects my neck. What I need is a day pack that I can put the basics I want to carry for a shoot. This bag does the trick without being too cumbersome for daily use like my travel bags are.Here's what I fit in my bag:Canon T3i with battery gripCanon 24-105mm f4L IS mounted on said camera (with or without lens hood reversal)Canon 70-200 f4L IS (I can mount this on the camera as well, with the lens hood reversed)Canon 580EX Speedlite in its caseCanon 430EX Speedlite in its caseWireless triggersSmall journalSeveral sets of batteries of each type (camera battx4, AAx4, AAAx8)Graduated filter in its caseSeveral sharpiesLens cleanersiPad with chargerNetbook with charger (I stack these vertically)A couple of magazines--(All this fits in the bag at once, but I cannot fit much else)I fit these in my bag by removing the partitions in the bottom compartment. To be on the safe side, I actually lined the bottom of the bag with these partitions to give the camera extra cushion. I stack the 70-200 lens in a Lowepro lens case #2 on the bottom, with the two flashes on top. The rest goes in the bag's pockets, with the batteries in the external front pocket.The straps on this bag work perfectly with the Lowepro lens bags, too. The back flap of the lens bag (Lowepro Lens Case #2 seen here: [...]) slides into one of two mounting positions on the strap. So, if I want to put more in the bag, I can mount the lens case on the strap and it is very secure there.Bag construction despite the large amount of stuff I put into it, is holding up very well. In heavy daily use (I carry this bag with me almost everywhere), there are no signs of the bag falling apart.I am very happy with this bag, and the funny thing is that I bought this in a crunch at a local store without reading any reviews. I simply made sure my camera with 70-200 mounted on it would fit in the bottom. I felt encouraged by the brand name, and they did not let me down.Great bag.Highly recommended.

Microsoft Office 2013 turned me against Microsoft with it's insistence that it be connected to Microsoft's cloud. Word wants to back up our documents, and Mr. Snowden's work shows us where the contents of Microsoft's servers end up. I've gotten so anti-Microsoft that I'm rewriting my visual basic apps in Java and Java Script. I'm moving my office to Libre Office and Thunderbird, but we have scheduling/billing software that requires Microsoft Word. At least we can by Word without getting the rest of Office. Buying this on Amazon was easy, but Microsoft handles the delivery on their web site. My trying to make a new Microsoft account failed because their e-mail to verify my e-mail linked to a non-existent web site. I had to use an old Microsoft account, and even then, they e-mailed my some secret code number to enter into their web site. And when Microsoft Word 2013 was finally installed in the target computer, it sets itself up to backup it's documents on the Microsoft cloud, with not easy way to prevent It from doing so,

I replaced an original Droid with the RAZR Maxx.  I really like the phone and would recommend it.  Even with the larger battery the phone is still super thin and I would not recomend the Droid RAZR as you won't notice the thickness but you will notice the battery life.PROS:- Battery, battery, battery - Can pretty much use it all day with GPS, Bluetooth, Wifi and everything else turned on and not run out of battery.- Comes with Swype delivered- Screen is bright and clear- Love the internal battery - one less door to deal with and allows the phone to be thinner than with a replaceable battery.- Smart Actions are great - There are apps in Google Play that do the same thing but this app has a better and more intuitive interface.- Comes with a messaging app that is better than stock Android as it allows a consolidated inbox (Gmail, corporate, other)- External Storage Card - Comes with a 16GB card.  Replaced that with a 32GB card and I have all my music on the phone (great for plane rides and no need to use bandwidth to play music).- The home row of 4 buttons can be customized (mostly, see below) to have whatever apps you want appear on every page.- 4G LTE is lightning fast.CONS:- Crapware - this phone comes with so much useless junk apps that cannot be uninstalled that I cannot begin to count them.  Luckily with 3GB of app storage it doesn't create memory issues.- Its slippery - sides are glossy and rounded so it could easily slip out of my hand.  Will be getting a rubberized case- Its hard to use with 1 hand and I wear a men's large glove- The bezel around the screen is wide.  I would have preferred the phone to be slightly thicker and have a smaller bezel making the height/width less and easier to handle and hold.- Dislike the Motorola OS overlay (MotoBlur or whatever its called).- The Application app (used to access the apps) is locked into the home row.  It cannot be removed.- Finicky Wifi (see note below).- No ICS (but should be coming ... hopefully without MotoBlur)- Included charging cord is only 3' long and is 2 piece (plug with 2 USB ports and USB cord).Additional Note:  When I set up the phone to access my Wifi it initially had problems.  I downloaded Wifi Analyzer (great app) from Google Play and found that every 2 seconds the signal would decrease to 0 and then come right back.  To fix this I had to set my router to broadcast the SSID (before I had it hidden) and this fixed the issue.

Since French press coffee does not go through a filter, all of those delicious oils and the magnificent fresh-brewed fragrance remains.  That, of course, explains their popularity with coffee connoisseurs.  With fresh boiling water and good coarse-ground coffee you can have a steaming cup of tasty java in just four minutes.If you enjoy a bold-tasting brew and aren't concerned about the health issues that come from leaving the oils in your coffee, this press will do a good job and is inexpensive.  Considerable medical research suggests that LDL cholesterol (the bad kind) increases with the consumption of coffee that does not have the oils filtered out.  That said, all of us probably take pleasure in consuming tasty foods and beverages that are not always good for us.I did find a puzzling omission on this otherwise well-made coffee maker.  Although there are brewing instructions printed on the borosilicate glass carafe, there are no markings showing how many cups of water are in the coffee maker.  Since every other coffee maker that I've ever owned has such markings, I thought this was a strange omission from an otherwise very capable French press.

Picked this headset up after buying the TL7610 by mistake. I have only had this for about 48 hours but it has already had about 20 hours of use. I have had 10 different phone systems and headsets looking for nirvana. My job has me on the phone all day. Sometimes 14 hours a day or more so I'm becoming an expert in phone systems and headsets.Setup:First of all, the configuration was VERY easy. You need to follow the instructions for your particular base system and NOT the instructions that come with the TL7600. My system (TL8609)pairing a headset instructions were in the very back of a very large manual. Press the "Hold" button for 4 seconds and BAM! registered the headset in 5 seconds.Comfort:I agree the over the ear piece is a pure torture device. Hard plastic, non adjustable in-ear piece and did not even come close to fitting in my ear. If it fits your ear, great but the hard plastic is going to hurt with extended use. I never like anything in my ear anyway so I use the headband. I find the headband on the TL7600 very comfortable. In 2 days I have had it on for at least 20 hours. After a while I forget it is even on. I can't comment on the behind the head strap as I never use those.Ergonomics:The base is a little odd. You really need to slide the headset in from the top. Makes it a bit of a pain but not a major issue. The headset sits right night to my mouth and the overall feel is excellent. I find the mute button is in the perfect spot for my use and the little "beep" every 15 seconds is nice to remind you that you are on mute. The only real issue here is the way you need to transfer calls to the headset with my particular system. In this one area the Plantronics was better. With this, you need to dial a number then hit the "select" soft key twice to transfer to the headset. If you get an incoming call on line 1 you can simply push the power button on the headset. If a call comes in on line 2 you must push the call to the headset. A minor annoyance but I'm already pretty fast at doing this.Sound Quality:Far better than any other wireless headset I have tried. I had a Plantronics which was 4X the cost and people constantly complained that I was fading in and out. The further I walked the worse it got. That system is considered to be one of the best and now it just sits in my closet as I went to wired headset thinking I'd never find anything better than the Plantronics. So far this thing is amazing. People say I sound clear and I don't need to shout over people on a conference call to be heard. Some have said the volume is too loud but I do not find that to be the case. Mine is set a couple notches up from the lowest setting and it is the perfect sound for me. I walked all over my 3600 st ft home and kept connected with no issues anywhere in my home. I did not go into the basement.Build Quality:Seems like a well built unit some complained the headband is cheap and breaks. I find just the opposite. The headband is steel and has a nice padded piece for your ear and the other plastic parts seem sturdy to me. The base is solid with only a single power line with a small wall wart on the end.Overall I am very happy with this purchase. My headsets get more use in a month than most would get in a year so I'll report back in about a month.

No cons, its perfect.==Windows 8.1 Tips & Computing Safety==_______________TIPS Windows8.1_________________Bring back the start button: Installing one of these two software will help new Windows8.1 users to ease into "Modern" user interface of windows 8.11) Classic Shell (free) or 2) Start8 (Stardock $5)___________________________________________________________________Avoiding major damages caused by those pesky malware,virus etc by simply not using an "Administrator Account" with your main MS Account.Viruses will likely cause more damage and will be impossible to completely remove if they gained Admisitrator's right and  have dug deep inside your computer.  An "Administrators" account is a backdoor route viruses/malware uses get in and cause unrecoverable distruction to your data. With Admin rights those malware can easily infect deep within your "recovery partitions'! meaning, these's not turning back and you're royally screwed. Theres many more problems that can happen but I think you already get my point.GOALS: 1) Don't use your Main MS Account as an" Adminitrator" but instead "standard"2) Create a secondary account called "local account" and set that up as the Administrator instead.Account Management: steps creating a local account(administrator) and MS Account(standard)Charms search: type"control panel" > "User's Accounts and Family Safety" > "Remove User account" > " Add a new user in PC settings " located at bottom of window > Bringing you to Metro > click "add Account" > "Sign in without MS accout" located at bottom of page > "Local Accout" > create new user name and PWYou have 2 options on Signing IN1) Microsoft account or 2) Local Account1) Create or sign in with your MAIN Microsoft Account. If you already an MS account or uses windows phones/ xbox live or Hotmail/live/MSN email address you can use these as well to link those services to 1 master MS Account.2) Create a Local Account. Thise secondary account will not connect to MS's ecosytem but only in your local computer. I identical to USERS login on Windows 7,Vista,XP etc.Once you've setup these two accounts, you must set each account type to: >Change ACCOUNT TYPE for Main MS Account to "Standard"Change ACCOUNT TYPE for 2ndary Local Account to "Administrator"Please do these steps, it only takes 5minutes.  Its a small price to pay considering, it may  potentially rescue your entire data from a completely erasing your data after sending it for repairs.__________________________________________________________________________Making Back ups/ EtcAnother tip is to make a recovery image.First install Setup all your programs , setup your custom settings, etcAfter personalizing your PC.  > Its time to Create a Recovery Image right after you've set everything up.  This image will allow you to revert back to this pc's state in time, and in perfect working order.Sometimes system recovery doesn't solve your problems so having secondary backup plan like Recovery Image is key.________________________________________________________________________Not optional, must practice these 3 regular computing habits.#1  (Install McAfee Antivirus, which is now free starting 2014, per Intel's 2014 Keynote at CES) or Paid programs such as Kaspersky Internet Security 2014  is the leading software in the market today. $50. But please dont go without using all 3 programs more than 5-8 Months in between scans.________________________________________________________________________Another way to avoid  spreading your personal information out there into the wild unknowingly is to un-google yourself.yes ladies and gents! google or yahoo especially yahoo LOL..... but google is key here.Microsoft sells Primarily Software.Apple sells Primarily HardwareGoogle sells neither, that's why their products are so CHEAP, once they suckered in their backdoor ridden ecosystem, Google Inc. sells Primarily Everything about YOU!_____________________________________________________________________________________________________Steps in upgrading from Windows 8 to Windows 8.1  (2-3hrs)Key here is to ensure your windows 8 is fully updated before upgrading to win8.1Therefore repeatedly "check of updates" on windowsHow to upgrade to windows 8.1 for dummies.Step 1: Go to Metro and not desktopStep 2: Start typing : "Control Panel"Step 3: Click "Windows Update" > "Check for Update"Step 4: install updatesStep 5: restart computerStep 6: Repeat step 1 -5 until result "no more updates"Step 7: Run HP Update client, install all updates from HP to ensure all drivers and bugfixes are updated.( or whichever OEM you may have, lenovo,dell, sony etc.)Step 8: Uninstall your Antivirus Software (important) then RESTART PCStep 9: Go to Windows Store and upgrade to 8.1Step 10: leave computer on for 2hrs as it upgrades.Step 11: Repeat Steps 1 - 7Step 12: if you're not a complete fool, 8.1 upgrade should be straight forward. \After steps 1-8 ONLY THEN you should install WIndows 8.1 at the marketplace. This case is a great case. So far, it is the first case I have purchased for my HTC G2 from T-Mobile. It is very clever in it's design, has the right durability vrs bulk to make it maybe the only case I will ever need.Some things I didn't expect with this case: 1) With a hard outer shell and a rubberized interior, the phone is kept sung and protected. More so than a standard case. 2) It is pretty bulky. Not so much that it becomes the heaviest brick ever, but certainly any lighter and maybe one might doubt the effectiveness of it. 3) It protects the buttons, inputs, screen (comes with a film) and camera lens. 3) The slide still works as awesome as it did before the addition (except if you are in bed and holding it above your head to work with, in this case the screen tends to want to fall forward to the closed position, not a hassle to me). 4) It really does not let any of the G2 design shine forth. To me this is a benefit as it makes the phone less identifiable and less likely a target for theft.All in all, I have nothing to complain about this model phone case. Yeah, I guess I could have bought a ten dollar case, but it wouldn't have come close to this case in terms of protection. I like it so much I got the MyTouch 3G slide case (Commuter) for the other phone on the plan.

In the interest of honesty, I will say that this charger was supplied to me by the vendor in return for an impartial review.  So here is my honest opinion...I love it!There are four ports, one specifically labeled for iPad.  There are 4 amps here so that power-greedy iPad  can be charged at the same time as a smartphone.  And it's fast!  My phone took a full charge in about an hour and the iPad took a bit longer but in a couple of hours it too was fully charged. You can't expect the world from this product, but you can enjoy a great deal. It is lightweight, balanced and sturdy. Although labelled a stir-fry pan, it is much more versatile than that. Because of the secure, flat bottom, it is ideal for omelets, pasta sauces, frozen-dinners-in-a-bag and more. My husband turned an egg over with a flip of his wrist. Please note the sides are quite curved, so you probably won't be browning pork chops in it, but you can do practically everything else. The ergonomic design of the handle, tight fitting see-through lid and oven compatibility are plusses.Do read the directions. It isn't meant to be used with non-stick spray. If you want to use oil, you should apply it with a paper towel. This is, after all, a non-stick pan. Use moderate to low cooking temperatures or you may warp it. Instead of scrubbing and scratching it to clean, the instructions say to boil a mixture of vinegar and water. Finishing a frittata or omlet in the oven is great, but again, you may warp it if you put it under the broiler.As with all products, carefully following directions is the key to optimum usage. This is a well-designed, versatile pan that I am happy to add to my kitchen. My one quibble with it is that it is called a stir fry pan, but shouldn't be used at high heat as most stir fry dishes call for.

To say that it's slow would be a massive understatement. I have a 3-year-old Buffalo LinkStation Live (the low-end one) which actually works better with Time Machine. Over an entire week I could not complete a 200 GB backup over a pretty fast WiFi network (I didn't try to do it over Ethernet as my Mac doesn't have such an interface). Having read the other reviews, I logged in with ssh and looked for processes hogging the CPU but there was no obvious culprit (I had all the media features turned off).As far as I can tell, it's practically unusable with Time Machine and my rating reflects just this use case. Getting the NEX-6 and both lenses for this price ($1,076 at the time), is a great bargain.  I just love the quality I'm getting from both lenses, and I am continuously impressed by the many features of the NEX-6 as I learn them.  My only complaint is short battery life.  The OEM lasts a third as long as the one in my Alpha 350, and the after-market battery about half of that.The bundle is okay.  The two UV filters were immediately installed.  I'm sure there's better glass out there, but these haven't degraded the images that I've noticed.The polarizer might be useful; I can't imagine ever using the florescent. The camera bag is an El Cheapo, zippers jam, not well designed, and I've already replaced it. The lens cleaning kit, dust blower etc. are cheap throw-ins that every bundle includes; inexpensive, but useful none the less.Bottom line, if you've settled on the NEX-6, which is a great camera, and these two lenses fit your shooting style, then this a grand bargain.OE MONTH UPDATE:  The price has gone up $70.  The "Sports" scene isn't too good.  The camera picks an incredible high shutter speed and the frames per second in full sun is pretty slow.  Switching to aperture priority and manually changing the focus and shooting mode, then adjusting the aperture to get a 500-650th/sec shutter speed solves the problem.  That gets me about what i was getting with my A-350 in raw, 6-10 fps with good autofocus on a moving target.  It's a lot of fusing around to get a few shots at the dog park, but the results are fine.The only app I've installed is the free "smart phone-remote".  Incredibly cool!  You can see the shot on your phone, then touch the phone to take the shot. I haven't gotten any of the pay apps.  I've seen some results of time-lapse video on YouTube, also very cool.  I may get that one for $10.I have also updated the firmware.  A time consuming 20 minutes, but fairly painless.  It's good to stay current.The in-camera HDR works really well, but it doesn't work in raw. Also cool is the 'hand-held twilight" mode that takes 5 or 6 really fast exposures in low light high ISO and, combines them to eliminate noise.It's hard to believe a camera in this price range doesn't come with a battery charger and you have to charge in-camera.  I have ordered an after market charge.

Have used Spigens screen savers in the past and have always been satisfied with them, so I figured that I would buy this for the anti fingerprint feature. Now always with anti fingerprint screen savers there is a reduction of clarity to the screen because of the material used so I expected that.  This screen saver comes with a bottom layer that you peel off as you apply it to the screen on the phone.  Usually not a problem.  On this product I had to pull and pull and pull the get the two pieces apart.  I thought for sure I would rip the screen saver.  Finally got them apart, and the screen saver then went on pretty smoothly, although previous Spigens savers seemed to go on easier.  Maybe I was stressed a bit after the tug of war to get the two pieces apart.  Like I said before, with ant finger print savers it will reduce clarity to the screen but this seemed to do so more then normal.  Go outside in the sun, crank up the brightness on the display, and I can't see the screen.  But here's the real killer.  I can't see the screen but what I can see are my fingerprints all over the screen saver. So it's not anti fingerprint and it reduces visibility way too much.  That and the fact that it's nearly impossible to to seperate the two pieces upon installation makes this a major disappointment.
unless you have a clean room it's hard to put these one without getting some dust underneath.  I prefer the moshi visor but they are like $30 each so when you're on a budget these will do.

Who doesn't love the haydays of PSX and PS2 domination? The dualshock controller design was great back in those days. It brought about dualstick design, all the button locations made logical sense and it worked for every type of genre out there. When the xbox came out, I like the triggers function but it was too bulky and the black/white button locations were awkward to reach.Since then, MS really improved their design to where 360 controller have since passed PS controller in terms of user friendliness. Last generation, a converter existed to use PS2 controller in xbox because that's what people prefer. This generation, it's the opposite, there's a converter to use 360 controller on the PS3. There's no arguing that MS improved their controller design by copying features from the dualshock controller (e.g. multiple shoulder buttons), but they also retained what was good about the original controller (analog triggers and size of xbox S controller).The 360 controller is superior for several reasons:-Dual sticks on 360 controller are shorter than those on PS3 controllers, allowing for more precise control-Resistance from the triggers on the 360 controller are linear. Triggers on the PS3 controllers resistance increase the more it's pushed in-Triggers on the PS3 controllers curve in, on which fingers can easily slip off of. 360 controller triggers curve out, like triggers on firearms-The 360 controller turn itself off after couple minutes of inactivity to conserve battery. The PS3 controller doesn't, often draining the battery dry

I purchased this headset thinking that it would fit all my needs, but it is lacking in 3 areas. First off though, the positives are similar to what others have posted about it. I didn't try the iPhone app or the Vocalyst service, so won't comment on those.Pros:+ Easy to pair+ Long battery life+ Quick charge time+ Extremely light+ Good volume+ Has A2DPnow onto my issues. I have tried to keep them objective, but I of course realize that when making a product review, it's mostly based on one's own personal needs. I only mention this because the issues I describe below may not be issues for you, and so don't let my comments detract you from this product.1. Quality of calls. I can hear people just fine, and the volume is great. I usually don't have to set the maximum volume either. I'm usually using the mid-range volume and that's enough. However the people I've talked to have said that my voice is either too tinny, a bit distorted, or too digital. If the volume is set to maximum, callers report they can hear themselves echo. I suspect that when the volume is at max, there is a bit of feedback from the callers own voice which is picked up by the mic and that is what causes the caller to hear the echoing.2. The answer/hangup button. This button presents 2 problems for me. First, it's small enough, and located exactly in the center of where your ear is, resulting in an uncomfortable feeling when you press the button in to answer a call in case the headset doesn't hear you say "answer". Also, this button can be used to answer an incoming call from what I understand although as of this writing, I haven't tested it. Second, since this headset has A2DP support, meaning you can listen to music, podcasts, or GPS instructions while not on a call, I would have hoped that this button would, in addition to answer a call, also act as a pause/resume for the currently playing audio. I've had other BT headsets where the answer button doubles as a play/pause button as well and so find it rather inconvenient if I'm wearing the headset and listening to a podcast and want to pause it, I have to do it from the phone itself. To me, this defeats the hands-free experience. This may not be an issue for some, but it's the dealbreaker for me.3. The fit in the ear. I left this one for last because the results can vary from person to person. For the most part the headset fits ok in my right ear using the medium earbud. However if I put it in my left ear, my ear gets sore after a while. I think that my left ear is slightly smaller than my right ear and so it causes this slight imbalance. Unfortunately using the small earbud in my left ear is too small, but yet the medium one seems to be too big. So I'm stuck with having to hear it in my  right ear for best comfort and I much prefer my left ear. When I am wearing it in my left ear, I don't dare press the answer button because it presses it uncomfortably into my ear and that just makes me want to remove it.All in all, this is a good headset. If it fits well in your ear, if the lack of play/pause audio isn't an issue for you, and if callers can hear you just fine, then go for it.

for the patterns to make batik fabric-wrapped clothesline tote bags or fabric bowls or placemats. I have a lot of battery packs for all different types of charging needs from smaller devices such as a Kindle right on up to the power hogging iPad.  This type of battery charger, because of it's small size, has been dubbed a "lipstick" charger.  This type of charger is almost intuitive to operate.  On the interfact you have a reset button, a micro USB port, a USB output port, a small reset button, and a LED status indicator.Unlike many of the battery packs, this one does not have LED bars or a digital indicator to let you know how much charge you have left.  The intent of the Arctic is to use as a backup when you are a little low on charge or, more likely, a single charge.  The assumption is that you'll be recharging it when you arrive home or at your office.I like the design, a compact and attractive one.  It's for all those small needs and would be best suited for the person who is generally good about keeping their phone charged up.  If you need a little juice now and then, the Arctic would be perfect.  It would be especially nice to take along on a trip to keep those Bluetooth headphones or Kindle charged up.SPECS:► Battery: Lithium ion► Capacity: 2,200mAh► Input: DC 5V / 500mA► Output: DC 5V / 1000mA► Charging Time:  4 - 5 hours► Casing material:  Aluminum► Product Weight: About 79 g► Dimensions: 23.1 (W)x 58 (L) mmCERTIFICATIONS:☼  CE, FCC, RoHsCOMPATIBILITY:►  Compatible with All SmartphonesBlackberry,  iPhone, Samsung, HTC, Motorola, Nokia, Sony, LGSample provided for review.

This set is a good value for the money. The protective screen seemed to make the pages a little dark, too. I'm still trying to work out what the "band" is for. If it's a wristband, then it's not for me. I'm 53 yrs. old! A little childish for someone of my age, I think. The earphones malfunctioned after only about 3 uses. One of the buds loses its sound altogether after a while, and you have to shake it about to get the sound back; the volume on it is extremely low as well, even when you turn the kindle up to it's max; the earphones have no volume control, and for the price one wouldn't expect it to.  But they also don't fit my ears well and are always falling out unless I stay still. The chargers are terrific, so far. I have no complaints about either of them, and I like that they both have a USB port so that the cord that comes in the kit (though not pictured, I think) is interchangeable. I think I'll buy a second chord though since I don't think it's very practical trying to use the one chord both in the car and in the house.Despite the earbuds, the useless band, and the dark protective screen, I still think that for the chargers alone, it was worth the $6 or $7 I paid.

I didn't have the highest of expectations for these earbuds, based on a number of unfavorable reviews.  But the idea of a carrying case that includes a built-in charger appealed to my inner geek and I took the plunge.I'm glad I did.The unit comes with different sizes of earpieces and, after some experimentation, I found the size that blocks out about 75% of background noise.  This goes a long way toward improving the quality of the sound because it tends to "seal-in" the high and low notes.  So they sound great to my un-audiophile ears!The charging case comes in handy and is good for a couple of full recharges before it, too, needs a visit to the charger.  As a bonus, you can charge the earbuds at the same time you're charging the carrying case, using only one micro-USB cord, so YAY!There are two minor gripes I have with them:1) Because the earpieces seal so well, any movement of my head that drags the wire across my shirt collar telegraphs the scratchy vibrations into my ear.  This can be annoying listening to very quiet passages of music.  I haven't found a work-around.2) Battery life really could be better.  I wear these to listen to audiobooks while working in the yard and after a little over 3 hours the batteries will be completely gone.  Admittedly, they recharge really quickly.  But having them be so short-lived does limit their usefulness.Bottom line is, I'd buy them again, and I am happy to have them.

I bought this camera in December of 2007! (wow, time flies!) It was an upgrade from myCanon SD300 4MP(my first digital camera, bought around in 2004. Paid $500 for it! But it was a good investment since I'm still using it, and it's still working the same -- close to 5 years later! I've taken it on many trips and it has taken a lot of beating.) Ironically, this SD800 camera, that was an upgrade for me, was less than half the cost. Was it worth the upgrade for me? Yes. The larger screen, wider lens, SDHC support (SD300's now outdated technology was limited to 2GB), LCD auto-rotation (portrait and landscape), image stabilizer (it was a lot harder to hold still with the SD300, especially when asking people to take pictures for me without using flash; to get around that, it's best to shoot in snapshots and hope that one of them comes out sharp) and the added mega pixels for larger prints/cropping was definitely worth it. However, I don't find the SD300 obsolete because it's still much smaller and still shoots great photos.Many cameras these days shoot in HD (1080 or 720) now. However, I was surprised to find out the video quality on this camera (max resolution at 640x480) still beats the low-end HD cameras, with poor sensor, that just interpolate to higher resolutions (it looks so awful - I'm talking about you, Mr. Sanyo TH-1!). Even though the video capability on this camera is a bit outdated now, I still find use for shooting with this camera.Even after all these years, I still love both my point and shoot cameras. It's just a no question that Canon is a leading camera company. I also recently bought an old Canon A-1 35mm film camera (made in the 1970's) and the build quality and technology on it still impressed me. Canon has my trust in cameras. Period. Design, functions, results - they usually don't disappoint.You really shouldn't be paying $500 for this point and shoot camera now. For that kind of money these days, you should be getting a DSLR with more features.

At first this worked with only the HF11 after I bought my HF10, so I was out of consideration.  But then rumors started.  It worked in HF10's with noise on the audio line (the external input, which I use all the time with radio mics and the cam-3AZDEN CAM-3 On-Camcorder Mini Audio MixerSo I connected up a senn ENG100P3 and turned down the output on the receiver then turned up the input on the HF-10 camera.  NO noise while running the lanc signals - none at all.So basically the "it only works with HF11 or newer" (in my lab) is a big lie.  Maybe there are some HF10's out there that are old and don't work with this, but all advertised functions worked perfectly with no noise issues.I have an employee with another camera in the HF10/20/200 series who is going to bring his into the lab for test which will be another datapoint.PROs:  Adds LANC (which so many people need on a VTR or camcorder)Cons:  Looses the single mini-s terminal, so no light or shotgun mic while using lancNewer cameras integrate lanc on the camera body and thus do not have this con.My advice is if you have a HF11-200 series camcorder to try it before it no longer becomes an in stock item due to lack of popularity.  As it stands today it is only officially supported on the HF11, which is rare, so get one while you can.Enjoy.

For the price these chargers are great alternatives that come with many MID tablets, I don't suggest charging with the LCD screenon at full brightness since it can overload out these chargers. (and stock chargers) (only if the device has reached its full charge then you can turn the display brightness up to full, it seems too much for it to handle both at once.)I suggest you always test power supply's before using them, I hooked it up to a Digital Multimeter and the voltage on mine and reads around ~5.5v,  (The stock charger that came with my tablet device reads 5.7 so it is very close.)Amp load tests are more difficult (especially with a tiny 2.5mm plug), so I was unable to test the amp load to see if it delivers a true 2AProduct comes from china so expect a few weeks for them to deliver it, I didn't mind the wait since I was just purchasing them as alternatives to have around.(note: sometimes you will get different power adapters one has a yellow tip and slightly bigger brick, one has a black tip and is smaller, both have different model numbers, but same volt and amps)I would order this product again.

1 star for design, working, and 5 stars for customer service!Good customer service doesn't solve my problem though, because even if they replace my unit, I am still getting a bad product..I hardly used it for 3 months (once in 2 or 3 weeks) and it started giving me troubles.Pros:1. Looks good2. reasonable price for what it claims (but fails to deliver).. so not sure if this is a plus3. Quite operationCons:Several design issues:- Coffee powder gets stuck easily and clogs the opening to the powder container. Every few minutes of use, you have to clean this hole as well as between the blades (by removing the bean container and the blade assembly.  When you clean this it spills all over the counter. One big messy grinder is all you get...- If you don't clean this properly, the grinder stops working. I thought the motor burned out and so called customer service. They were helpful and sent a UPS ship label by email for replacing the unit. But then I went to clean it once more and then it started working.- The hole that drops the coffee powder to the plastic container is in a bad place - hard to clean.- Also coffee powder accumulates between the blades and gets stuck after some uses. This also stops the motor. Worse still, the bean container won't properly seat once this happens, so you can't put the bean container in once this happens, until you thoroughly clean the blades also.- The timer. If by mistake you set a full timer, and there are no beans to grind, it has to run through the course. I am afraid of forcing the timer back to zero.I was using aCuisinart DBM-8 Supreme Grind Automatic Burr Millbefore this one, for about 2 years. It was a great product with NONE of the issues I am mentioning for the Capresso one. The only issue was that there were plastic parts around the burr blades and that broke after 2 years of good use. After seeing this Capresso one, I am tempted to go back to the Cuisinart and am willing to replace every 2 years.Summary: Look elsewhere if you are looking for a trouble free, easy to maintain grinder; When it worked, it was fantastic. I am hoping Capresso will fix these issues and come out with a better product soon, and I can buy Capresso again. They have a great customer service, great product build quality (just design issues with this one), and very reasonable prices.UPDATE: 4/18/13: turns out that I get better results with the beans at room temperature instead of straight from the freezer; and this was not an issue with the earlier cuisinart

Why is it, I always wondered, that American recipes always call for sifting the flour first? You never see that in recipes from other countries. The reason, I finally learned, is that all other countries (and all professional bakers) measure out ingredients by weight, not by volume.Sifting makes sure that the flour you measure is always the same density. It's a holdover from the first "modern" cookbooks of the late 19th and early 20th Century that were written for the modern woman who might not necessarily have grown up in a house with two or three generations of women to teach her how to cook and bake. Measuring cups and tablespoons is much easier and in the past, faster than weighing everything out.Today, of course, we probably have as many men as women cooking in the average household, and we have inexpensive, accurate, electronic scales like this one from MIRA. I've been a convert to weighing for some time, although up till now I've been using a postal scale that shares duty between my home office and my kitchen. I was looking for a dedicated kitchen scale when I was offered an evaluation sample of the MIRA to review.First impression: it's very compact, and I like the spill-proof solid top. There are no cracks or gaps for spilled liquids or powders to seep in and gum up the works. Turning it on, it wakes up and does a quick auto-tare, and it's immediately ready for use. The readout shows tenths of an ounce, and it seems to be pretty repeatable. It also seems to be pretty indifferent to where on the scale surface an object is placed.I first tested the scale on my dining room table, where it had the annoying habit of not always returning to zero after weighing something. Sometimes it even shifted zero just sitting there, suddenly showing 0.1 or 0.2 grams. The manual said to make sure it was on a flat surface, and I figured a tablecloth ion a dining room surface was pretty flat, but just to make sure I moved it to my stainless steel countertop. Bingo. No more errors. It returned to zero every time, and measure were repeatable to a tenth of an ounce.One nice feature of this scale is that it will measure in fluid ounces and milliliters. This only works for water, or liquids with a very similar density, like vinegar,  but it's useful four getting a more accurate fluid measure than you can with a cup.One very minor annoyance: The scale, like a lot of electronic devices, came with three "Heavy Duty" AAA batteries which went right into the recycle bin, as standard carbon-zinc cells (and off-brand cells of all types) have an annoying tendency to leak corrosive liquid and ruin devices. I inserted three good quality (Duracell, in this case) batteries instead, and I'd recommend you do the same, if you want your electronic devices to last.Overall I'm pretty impressed with the accuracy and clean design of this scale, and I think it'll get a lot of use in my kitchen.

This Miracle Blade is a good set of knives; but, they are not my favorites.  I purchased my knives at least six years ago. My favorite & most used items in this set are the shears, santoku-style Rock & Chop followed by the Filet. I got this case for my husband. This was originally my phone, and being a lady I had a pink case for it, he was not having that; SO I ordered him this one and he really likes it. It makes this small, thin phone bigger and better to handle.

The good part is that this case looks very good, and is fairly easy to put on and off.  It's very unobtrusive, and my wife didn't even notice it was there - she had used the phone some, but is more into what is happening on the screen than how the phone itself looks (silly, I know).The bad part is that it seems like it would mainly just protect the back of the case case from scratches, etc, but not the screen.  It implied that it extended a slight bit more in the front, but it didn't seem to.  It seemed to be more or less flush with the screen, but was a little hard to tell for sure. Unfortunately, I installed the screen protector first, so I can't tell for sure.  EDIT: see end of post for update.Measuring with a micrometer gives a total width of 9mm (matching the spec of .35"), but the thickness of the actual material is 1.5 mm, so it seems like the iphone 5's 7.6 mm would actually extend slightly above rather than below the lip.  It seems like it should be at least 1 mm more in width.Otherwise, I like it, and it has a nice feel, but is not rubbery so it is easy to put in a pocket, yet still has a little resistance to keep it from sliding too much.  But I think it would not offer any protection for the screen, and I actually ordered a different case for my son's.  They have a case that looks a little bit better for protection (FlexProtect), and actually costs less, but it was advertised as iPhone 5s only, so I didn't see it until I received a reply from Tech Armor about this issue.  Their only solution to my complaint was to buy a different case, even though they said in the description "-Engineered precision lift to ensure iPhone 5 display glass does not scratch when face-down".  So, I subtracted a star for not meeting the description, and not seeming to feel any need to even apologize for the discrepancy, or offer any solution other than buying a new one like the FlexProtect.UPDATE:  I was contacted by their customer support about my dissatifaction, and was sent a FlexProtect case to replace this one.  The new case is more what I expected as far as protection goes, although it doesn't look quite as low-key as this one.  But it's nice, and I ordered 4 more of them for gifts to other family members.Also, I was able to try this case on a new iphone 5 without a screen protector, and it seems like it does extend beyond the screen, although very slightly.I think that this case does have an advantage in that there is no ridge around the screen, so it is very smooth and almost invisible from the front.

I finally dumped the low battery time, irrational malfunctioning Razr phone in November and upgraded to the Blackberry Curve 8310 in red. It's like upgrading from a Yugo to a Mercedes SLK. I have had none of the negative issues some of the other reviews mention - access through the 3G system for the internet, phone calls, email etc is speedy and consistantly works when asked to. I had AT&T; service before with the Razr phone in the same location and had problems with reception all day long. That problem vanished with the Curve. I am finding that my email appears on my phone within just a minute or two after it appears on my computer.LOVE the clarity of photos taken with this phone (it's a phone camera so photos are never going to be as nice as with a high end digital camera, but they are far superior to those with my old phone) I have also found it very easy to sync with my computer and swap/download music, files, photos and the like between the phone and computer. Overall, I would spend full price for this phone any day [...]. This is the first cell phone in 7 years I have been totally pleased with and glad that I bought.

I received this OXO Stainless Steel Rotating Utensil Holder on 03/16/2013 and am very pleased with my purchase overall.It replaces another OXO utensil holder (OXO Good Grips Brushed Stainless Steel Utensil Holder - same price), namely the elongated, non-rotating model which I found to be less convenient during daily use over time.The rotating feature of this round model is very useful and makes finding that specific tool that you are looking for a much easier task and in general provides more of an overview of all of the utensils in your collection. The stationary OXO model definitely comes up short in this regard.If you happen to have an extremely large number of these kitchen implements, you can simply purchase a second one of these round, rotating utensil holders and place them side by side to easily solve all of your storage problems.The elongated, non-rotating OXO model (and a similarly shaped holder by simplehuman also available here on Amazon.com) cannot as readily be used in this manner. I personally find that doubling up is a much simpler task with round containers, especially if they also rotate such as these.This kitchen item qualifies as another one of life's simple pleasures and is highly recommended to make your day just a little bit brighter.

The purple on the site is a very nice, rich purple, and in person the case is a paler, lighter purple.  It has a bit more silver in it and frankly, looks metallic rather than plastic. The back is a hard shell-like plastic with a slot in the middle and the front is a flat black flap with the Marware name and logo.  The Kindle snaps into the shell which is a hair taller than the device.  When the flap is closed, the case is only the slightest bit bigger than the actual Kindle.  The plastic is has a brushed feel and feels sturdy without the I'm-gonna-drop-it feeling that some plastic cases give you.Unlike on the Vibe, the strap on the MicroShell is wide and seems to belong--its not as jarring, probalby because the flap is black.  The strap is also wider and because of the thinnest of the cover the elastic strap is needed to hold the case closed.To use as a stand, the flap is folded back and lodges in a slot on the back of the case. It sounds flimsy, looks like it may be flimsy, but I stopped trying to break it because it was obvious that I would break it trying to prove any flimsyness.  You won't have to worry about it falling over unless you don't get the back of the flap into the slot. I'm not sure if this will get irritating or not since I keep doing it with the back of the device facing away from me before remembering to turn it around.  The back of the case has speaker 'grills' directly over the Kindle speakers, but I didn't test the sound with this case. (I'm at work)The shell is snug enough that your device won't fall out by accident, IMO, they should have covered more of the sides of the device, leaving the cutouts only for the volume/power buttons.  To be fair, this is clearly shown in the images on the Amazon site if you know what you're looking at.  Like the Vibe, only the outside corners of the Kindle are covered, leaving the entire bezel free to show off your favorite skin.The Microshell case is well made and light enough for easy portability.  I am probably biased towards low profile cases, but I totally see why it is $10 more than the Vibe case.  I'll definitely be keeping this one and only see replacing it if I find a low profile leather case.Grade: B

Fire AND water proof hard drive...AWESOME! And a data recovery service, if needed, to boot...double-AWESOME! All at a fairly resonable price. A few key points that influenced my decision to purchase this include:A) Subtracting the cost of the hard drive, the price for the ioSafe enclosure was not too exorbitant once I found the product discounted from the "MSRP" via Amazon.com - I must say this product was at the upper limit of what I was willing to spend for a backup solutionB) The affordable Data Recovery Service (DRS) option, which I upgraded to 3 years at time of registration, was extremely appealingC) An informative website and what appeared to be a company that not only has different product offerings, but also has a progression from older designs all made me trust investing in the ioSafe company itselfAs far as reviews on the ioSafe, I did read a couple 3rd party reviews, but, what really made me believe the product worked as advertised was watching other people on YouTube put the ioSafe through a real-world torture tests. Setting the device on a raging fire for a number of minutes until smoke was pouring from the enclosure. Then drenching it with large amounts of water as expected from a fire department. Followed by opening the enclosure and successfully accessing the hard drive from within.I am fairly impressed with the ioSafe. Granted, I've only had it for a couple days, but the registration and DRS upgrade was easy and quick, the device connected and mounted as expected, and I've started copying my most important information to it without any issues. I truly wish to never come across any worst case expectations, however, now seeing the solid construction first-hand I feel safe (pun intended) knowing my information will survive most tragedies that I am likely to encounter.About the ONLY downside to this product I can think of is the somewhat large size of the enclosure. I don't really mind the weight, and considering what this thing is desinged for, I'm starting to accept the extra large size of the case without any major problems.Regarding new and/or existing features, I would like to see a little more secure (perhaps just different) method of protecting the ioSafe against theft. Specifically, some sort of plate that can be anchored to a solid surface to which the ioSafe gets attached and locked thus covering up the mounting plate. I do own a few Kensington cable locks, but actually think the standard floor mount on my ioSafe is more secure to begin with and I therefore wasn't sold on that feature being available only on the more expensive models. I am planning on purchasing a much thicker steel cable and padlock system than what the Kensington solution can offer and think something more integrated, as mentioned, would be very nice.I would like to say this is literally a "bullet-proof" backup system, but I'm not in the mood to test this and risk trashing something I just paid a couple hundred bucks for...just yet! Sufice it to say that I highly recommend this product to anyone concerned with an almost 100 percent guaranteed preservation of irreplaceable files in about the easiest method I've seen.

This is my first bread machine. Everything on the unit itself and in the manual are clearly labeled, so it's easy to figure out. The instructions come with some recipes as well (Rosemary, sea salt and olive oil loaf is the best!).I believe no matter what setting you're using, it always makes a high pitched beep 6 or so times to let you know you can add mix-ins... which can be annoying for someone like me who is usually just making plain bread. Then you still need to stay nearby to remove the mixing paddle towards the end, otherwise your bread will rise and bake around the paddle and make it difficult to remove the whole loaf when it's done.I don't think it's the most evenly heating appliance ever. Almost every loaf I've made, I use the "medium crust" setting and the bottom comes out very dark - almost burnt and dry, and the top is golden and fluffy. And then when I tried "light crust" it didn't even look like it was finished baking all the way it was so light.In retrospect, I wish I would have sucked it up and purchased a higher quality brand. But, not knowing if a bread machine was something I was going to really get use out of all the time or not, this was still probably the safest bet without spending too much.

I got this to replace my aging N52TE gamepad. I started off with the original N52 when Belkin made it, moved to the TE when Razer bought it, and now that my TE's dying I hoped this'd be an upgrade. My opinion is it's a huge waste of money.To explain, let's go point by point. The gamepad itself is easily twice the size of the N52. I mean that literally, it's huge and heavy. Heavy's good, it keeps it from moving around during use but the fact is it's massive. To use it I have to turn my keyboard sideways and slide it back on my desk to make room. I don't have a small desk either, nor do I have small hands. Point 2, it has a huge number of buttons. I thought this'd be a good thing, I always wanted another couple on the Nostromo plus the Nostromo lacks an analog joystick. This has a joystick and it works great....but it's tiny and way too sensitive. The buttons on the G13 are tiny and numerous, meaning to use them you need to have super long fingers the size of knitting needles. The N52 keys are normal keyboard sized, so while they aren't huge, they're not a problem either. Also, to use all the N52's keys you just need to move your fingers. The G13 is so huge and the keys angled so badly you can't use the handrest because your hand's doing the time warp all over the thing's keyboard. I can use the N52 for hours at a time. Literally, I played through all of MW3's campaign in one sitting (Breaks for lunch and dinner) when the game came out with no hand or wrist strain. The G13 on the other hand, I had to stop using after 20 minutes because my whole forearm hurt. Lastly the software. It can do basic keymapping only. Volume controls? Nope. Mouse buttons (Wheel up/down/middle) nope. Other media or non key controls? Nope. A save icon so you can save your keymaps after modifying them? Nope, gotta click this little totally unlabeled icon in the lower left corner. The N52 software may be ugly and a kludge, but it's 10 times more capable. Top all that off with zero documentation and the quick start manual has no words. None, it's 4 pictures and there's no directions on keymapping, what all the non-game buttons do, or how to use any of it.I'll freely admit the G13 is built well. Tough, I love the variable lighting, the handrest is well built, but only if you're just resting your hand on it. The software is, I'll admit, easy to use for basic keymapping and making a new keymap for a new game is easy as pie. Modifying it's easy also, at least until you try to safe the modifications. The thing looks good, has some admittedly really cool capabilities with the built-in LCD, like a clock, system monitor that displays CPU and RAM use, RSS feeds (Though good luck getting one you actually want, the interface for adding one's useless) timer, and media display (Not controls, just track name and so on)Do I regret this purchase? Yep, bigtime. I'd been hoping to unify all my inputs (Mouse, keyboard, gamepad) under one bit of software (Logitech) since right now I'm running 3 different kinds (Logitech, Razer, Saitek) and this hunk of junk voids that plan. Would I recommend this thing to others? Unless you've got spider fingers and don't need advanced features then no, I wouldn't. Yeah the Nostromo has some glaring flaws (The D-Pad most people use for movement is 4 directions only, it's so light it skids around a bit, the software's overly complicated) but do yourself a favor and get one of those.

We use many OXO products in our home (salad spinner, pizza wheel, spatulas, can-opener, knives, soup ladle, measuring cups, bowls, colanders, trivets etc.) and we've been very pleased with just about all of them. Recently, our downstairs bathroom has been getting more use than normal and having only one towel bar has become a bit of an inconvenience.  Thus been looking for an additional and easy way to hang/store towels to accomodate the increased use of the bathroom. Enter the "OXO Over-the-Door Towel Rack." Here are my thoughts;Pros+ Quickly assembles in three easy steps.+ Door hook bumpers prevent rack from scratching, marking or sliding on door top.+ Spacious and sturdy towel bars.+ Spring backed hook accomodates many door widths.Cons- Plastic is very hard and feels like it could crack, snap or chip easily.  I was quite nervous when snapping the towel bars into the back as it felt like the pressure was too much for the plastic.- Towels bars would be more useful if they were a tiered waterfall design allowing towels to dry more evenly rather than laying on top of one another.- Rack can still bounce around on the door when opening or closing regardless if it's empty or full of towels.- Expensive at around $40.The "OXO Over-the-Door Towel Rack" is a convenient and useful over-the-door holding solution. It makes hanginging and storing both your wet and dry towels worry free while keeping your bathroom neat and organized. The best feature of this rack for me is the easy and out of the way storage as we have somewhat limited space in our downstairs bathroom. Even though I really like this towel rack, I really wish the hang bars were varying depths creating a waterfall tier so towels could hang more neatly and dry easier. Other than this minor drawback, I would recommend this towel rack to anyone if they're willing to pay slightly more for the OXO name and quality.
This item works great!  It helps reduce the clutter of individual chargers.  I can charge 2 nabi's, a galaxy note 3, an iPhone 5s and s galaxy s4 all at the same time!

I simply cannot figure out what reason there could be for using a 1 AMP charger instead of 2.1 AMP charger. While a 1 AMP will work fine with most smart phones, including the iPhone 5 (which I tested this on and it did a speedy job), as Belkin even notes, if you want to charge something like an iPad, you'll need a 2.1 AMP charger. It will charge it, but at an extremely slow rate.Now if a 2.1 AMP charger was considerably more than a 1 AMP, I could understand wanting to save money, especially if you didn't foresee having to charge an iPad in the car, but 2.1 AMP chargers aren't that much more expensive.In fact, this charger is really expensive for what you get. For a few dollars more you can get a voltage inverter that not only comes with a 2.1 AMP USB port, but also an AC outlet (as well as provides better protection for your car and the electronics being plugged into it). It may not be as compact, but it offers far more functionality.

These attachments are some of my FAVORITES (along with the ice-cream maker) because they truly deliver a product that is unlike anything that you can buy in the store.  Home-made pasta, which I'd never had before I bought these attachments, is amazing.  I use the recipe from the Mixer Bible (sells on Amazon) for basic egg pasta and it is fantastic.  I've made it about 10 times and just once it was too sticky to use, but that was a great learning experience.  In other words, it's not difficult to make and it definitely gets easier as you keep making it.  There's a good you-tube video I watched a few times on how to use this attachment the first time.  Once you get the hang of it, it's really really easy though slightly time consuming.  However, it's worth it.  You won't save money b/c store-bought pasta is already so cheap, you won't save time b/c store-bought pasta is so easy, but you will have discovered a completely new type of food, and it will enhance all of your pasta sauces.  I also bought the other set of 2 attachments and enjoy making a variety of noodle sizes.  I HIGHLY recommend this attachment!!

I bought this to take photos and video of my kids with my T1i.  They move much faster than I do so it's hard for me to be where the shot is- this lens lets me quickly close the gap between where I am and where they are, then zoom out when they come back my way.  The wide range also lets me change perspective from stunning wide angle to compressed telephoto without having to dink around with lens changes.  I lug enough stuff around when I travel with kids, so I don't want to have to carry extra lenses.  Nor do I want to risk both lens and camera changing them on sandy beaches or when trying to chase my kids.  This lens lets me get the shots that I wouldn't get otherwise, because of that I think it's perfect for my needs.Of course, there is no such thing as a prefect lens, every lens has some compromise, whether it is flexibility, image quality, weight, or your pocket book.  This has more compromises than most, which is to be expected from a lens with this zoom much range.  As you'd expect, image quality is not its strength- there is very noticeable barrel distortion at 18mm and vignetting at longer zoom ranges.  I try to stop it down to at least f8, as it is not sharp wide open.  Even wide open, it is pretty slow- f5.6 at most focal lengths.  Because of these issues, I wouldn't use this lens in situations where I have the ability to carry multiple lenses and have the time to change them.  I also wouldn't use it indoors, it is too slow for low light photography and not sharp enough when wide open.  If you pixel peep or are inclined to complain about image quality, don't waste your money on this lens, it's not designed for you.This is also a fine lens for video if your camera can do that.  The zoom range is helpful and it has a quiet image stabilization mechanism.  Image stabilization is critical for video, maybe more so than stills.  I'd previously used a Canon 28-135 lens, the IS on that was noisy and audible on the video- this one is not.I can't afford to buy every lens that I want, but even if I owned a large assortment of L lenses, I'd use this one for family trips where most of my photos are outside.  On a Canon crop body, it is light enough to not be a huge burden (though quite a bit heavier than the kit lens).  It is far easier to travel with than having a backpack full of lenses and it lets you get the shots that you'd otherwise miss.  I've seen some suggestions that a superzoom compact camera is a better choice than this lens- I don't agree.  Even at ISO 1600, you'll get higher quality images with this on an SLR than with any compact that I've seen.  Plus you get the better viewfinder and focusing that an SLR will give you.I've owned this lens for more than a year and am very pleased with it, but don't buy it if you can't live with its compromises.

First of all I must say I love this juicer and recommend it. It came well packed in a very pretty box. It comes with a very nice recipe book and picture type instructions for putting the juicer together. At first I thought it was weird to have only pictures to put it together, but I had no problems at all. It is fairly intuitive to put it together. The juicer is very sturdy and extremely well built. It looks great on my counter.Two months ago I bought my daughter the Hamilton Beach Big Mouth Juice Extractor 67650 for her birthday. This was the second centrifugal type juicer I have had. They have both been loud, messy, and hard to clean. The KRUPS juicer is a masticating juicer. It crushes the food with a large spiral auger wheel. I was really amazed at how quiet the KRUPS was when I turned it on. It moves with slow and steady pace. The three feeder holes are smaller than my other juicers, so I had to cut the apples in pieces smaller than quarters. But having 3 holes allowed me to feed a lot in at once and let the juicer take them in as it needed. I also added half a lemon to make apple lemon juice which is really good. The really dry pulp slowly falls out of one ramp on the juicer and the juice comes out the other. The unit comes with 2 pitchers, one for juice, one for pulp. The pitcher has a foam separator insert which keeps most of the foam out of your glass when you pour. I really love that.Cleanup. Cleanup of both my previous juicers was always a chore. Juicing is messy. The KRUPS cleaned up faster then the other two I had, but It's still a chore. I don't see how cleaning a juicer can be made very much easier at this point. You still need to clean out the basket with the included brush. The auger wheel gets a lot of pulp up inside the bottom, but the end of the brush handle seems to be made to fit up in there and get it out. It took me about 5 minutes to clean it.My Experience with Centrifugal Juicers:- Loud- Messy. Pulp gets shot out fast and sometimes even comes back up the feeder tube and onto the ceiling- Hard to clean- Larger feeder tube- Can juice a wide variety of foodsMy Experience with the KRUPS ZB500:- Quiet- Solid and stable. No problem with the top staying put.- Pulp comes out slow and dry. Does not shoot everywhere.- Not as hard to clean as the other two Centrifugal Juicers but it does take work.- Looks really cool- Smaller feeding tubes but has 3 holes to put food into. Food needs to be cut into smaller pieces.- The foam separator is a piece of plastic that fits into the pitchers and keeps almost all the foam out of your glass. Really cool.

I am a professional and purchased this for a recent assignment that took me from Denver to Tampa to photograph three executives outdoors.  I needed a quick set-up and a specific umbrella size- this one fits that to a T.  However, one thing I quickly discovered is that there are some drawbacks:1- The black outer covering is removable only after unscrewing and replacing an end cap.  It is attached at the pole-end and although you can in theory use it as a shoot-thru, the black will be dangling somewhere in the way unless you take the time to remove it. There is no silver on the inside of the black, so this is used just to prevent light spill.2- Once collapsed to its smallest size, the outer black comes off of the ribs ends in many places. Its just the way its designed and is a side affect of the extra joints required to make this collapse so small.  It makes setting it up a prolonged experience because you'll have to re-attach all the ends before you can attach it to anything.  Definitely frustrating.3- If wind blows INTO the umbrella, it easily reverse-collapses (i.e. goes from concave to convex in shape) so if you are photographing in any wind or gusts greater than 15mph- be aware of this.  I have never used an umbrella of ANY size that did this so easily.  Mine did this near the end of my session and it fell and broke one of the rod connectors...Hoewever, if you are working in controlled environments the size of this cannot be beat for travel.  I just wish Lastolite would add a bit more 'last' to this lighting modifier.

Seriously. Why are we using water product installment? It's very inefficient and risky.I tried installing this thing 2-3 times, and all the time I would get some bubbles (and some bubbles are undetectable because they're filled with water.)Pros:Very thick screen, feels like its really gonna protect your screen. Not easily scratchable.it's "anti-fingerprint" only if your hands are fairly clean all the time. (assuming there's not an overdose of oil on ur finger.)Cons:Water installment requires that you wait 24-48 hours before everything settlesInstallment is one of the longest ive ever had in the history of screen protectorsAfter a few days, my screen didn't feel "anti-fingerprint"-like anymoreIf all bubbles aren't released(which is almost impossible with this water-procedure) you end up getting weird lighting on some sections.The thing i hated the most is that this screen is way too thick. My touch keyboard ended up triple tapping letters randomly, and sometimes it didn't even respond to touch in some areas effectively.I honestly am not a fan of clear screen protectors. I thought this protector would change my mind, but I am still going to stick with matte protectors.

This is super light and small. It doesn't jam up my car's console and is hardly noticeable. This is the smallest and lightest dual car charger I've ever seen. I like the dual inputs and the placement on two different surfaces so the cords don't get tangled. The blue color makes it easy to find in my bag or on the floor of my car.I like that the telescopic cord is included, and appreciate that it keeps extra cord out of my way. But the telescopic cord has some faults. It is inconsistent in its retraction, and it is poorly designed because you need to insert an attachment to make it micro-USB compatible. Now the attachment *is* included, but it's not like there are other attachments to swap in and out--so why not just make it micro-USB from the start?Anyway, I was looking for a car charger, and the cord was just a bonus, so the fact that it was a little disappointing doesn't hinder my overall opinion of this item.

I bought this TV in the beginning of 2013 having read of potential clouding/flashlighting issues when viewing dark scenes. I had no idea how bad and distracting the effect would be.For bright pictures and images, the TV looks fine (I won't say great or mesmerizing, but roughly on par in terms of picture quality with the 3-year-old Samsung LCD I had hoped this would replace). However, for scenes that are dark or have black, there is prominent flashlighting at the corners, and splotchiness/cloudiness in the center of the screen. I have read on AVS forums that this is a known shortcoming of edge-lit panels, but if you're paying anywhere close to $2000 for a TV, that's not acceptable.I replaced the TV once and the replacement TV displayed the defect even more prominently. It's too bad, because I had been a loyal Samsung TV customer for almost a decade, and had sung its praises to friends and family.I have returned the replacement unit and am going to try my luck with a Panasonic Plasma TV specifically, thePanasonic VIERA TC-P60GT50 60-Inch 1080p 600Hz Full HD 3D Plasma TV.If you are a purist when it comes to image quality, avoid this TV.

Sound quality is very good, though some static and not very loud. Quality is excellent and battery life is excellent, especially if you leave it on accidently. Even Standby was impressive. With that said, one of my bigget frustrations is having to constanty adjust the fit. It just doesn't stay in my ear, and with the sound somewhat weak, it is very hard to hear. I tried both ear pieces, and neither made a difference. I think the problem is with the over-the-hear attachment. Nothing has ever been as comfortable as my Oasis (and nothing has ever had better sound quality). Controls are easy to use, and my 50 mph w/windows down test was very good. People only complained of a little background noise, but considering the wind noise *I* heard, that is totally acceptable. For the price, this is a good headset, and I would probably pick this over the 1800, as it feels slightly better balanced. One other problem I had was my phone often does not recognize my voice commands, and instead of making a call, I am asked if I wanted to send a message. Also, it does not recognize yes command when I am trying to confirm a contact to call. Another point of frustration. I should point out that this ocurs with the windows closed, radio off, etc.....but I had the same problem with the Samsung 1800, so maybe it's a problem with the Samsung mic.

This radio was purchased to replace a Panasonic RF2200. The product was delivered with a set of NiMH batteries, charger, vinyl carrying case, a nifty "long wire" antenna, and stereo earbuds - everything you need for great portable listening. The older Panasonic (still a great radio for AM and FM)had reasonably accurate analog tuning for international AM DX, but was beginning to fall short on all the busy amateur bands where SSB predominates. The Tecsun selectable wide/narrow bandwidth button is very useful for pulling out weak SSB signals from adjacent channels. When listening to international AM stations the synchronous detection circuit likewise helps reject adjacent channel interference. So far I have been using the included "long wire" antenna strung across the room, which works very well, but look forward to spring so I can set up a new outside antenna. Tuning has been spot on. If you know the frequency of a station, just key it in and there it is. In all, the sensitivity and selectivity is greatly improved (as expected from digital technology) when compared to the 20+ year old RF2200. The Tecsun PL660 falls a little short, however, if you like to DX the AM Broadcast band (the huge, adjustable,ferite rod on the RF2200 is far superior). Also, audio output is significantly less than the RF2200, but that's expected for a radio that is 1/8 the volume with a speaker 1/3 the size (hint: the Sony noise cancelling headphones I use on airline flights work great). Overall, this is probably the best receiver under $500 on the market - highly recommended.

I'm very disappointed with the reduction in the time it takes to convert AVIs to MP4s/M4Vs. I was hoping for a halving of the encoding time, but I'm actually getting about a 25% reduction. Examples: On the 10.5.1 MacBook Pro, one 22-minute TV show took about 13.5 minutes to convert to iPhone format with the Turbo.264, but QuickTime alone only took 17 minutes. Another show, about 45 minutes in length, took 29 minutes with Turbo.264 and 39 minutes without. Right now, the 10.4.11 Mac Mini is in the midst of converting 12 videos and it looks like it'll take about 9 hours. This is not what I was expecting for $100.I didn't formally test how much (if any) more responsive the system was, but the Mac Mini did seem less burdened by the tasks with Turbo.264. The MBP didn't seem to care whether I used the Turbo.264 or not.I'm not a video connoisseur, but the Turbo.264 video seemed equivalent to the QT video. It looks good on the iPhone and AppleTV.To add insult to injury, UPS royally screwed up the delivery. I paid for Next Day Air delivery. Amazon's email indicated a Tuesday delivery, as did UPS' website. The package sat at a UPS facility in Harrisburg, PA for over 30 hours. Later in its journey, it visited Louisville, KY, then Columbus, OH, then back to Louisville, KY! Did they take my package sightseeing?!?!? It finally arrived on Thursday afternoon. Three days for Next Day Air? Thanks UPS! Normally it takes the US Postal Service to screw things up this badly!
