This 5-In-1 device is a great addition to any power mobile user.This device is in a class of its one. It falls into many categories, which instantly gives it added value. You can use this device on multiple devices and platforms.- FileHub Wireless SD Flash Card Reader -This option gives you the ability to use a SD memory card and share it with up to five users.- USB Storage -This option gives you the ability to use a USB thumb drive or external hard drive to share with up to five users.- NAS File Server -This option gives you the ability to access the installed memory on your local network.- Power Bank -This power bank (battery charger) will give you 3000mAh, which can be used to charge any mobile device in the need of power.- Wi-Fi Hot Spot -This option gives you the ability to bypass you router, so you can access any data on your FileHub while simultaneously surfing the internet.Once in the hand, it feels great, nice and solid and no moving parts. You would think this is just any other battery charger, but that is where the similarities stops. Hidden inside, is where all of the magic is hidden. Being able to share data between mobile devices are some great functions. I find myself streaming movies and music to my phone, tablet and my SmartTV. The best part of this device is that it works with Android, Apple, and Windows products.If you are a mobile power user with multiple devices, this will make using them even easier.CONTENTS:RavPower FileHub Wireless SD Card Reader w/ 3000mAh BatteryMicro USB Charging CableFEATURES:Wireless SD Flash Card ReaderUSB Storage Media SharingNAS (Network Attached Storage) File ServerPortable Wi-Fi Hot Spot (Wi-Fi Standard: 802.11b/g/n)3000mAh Battery ChargerBattery Input: 1.5ABattery Output: 1ABattery Charging Time: 2.5 Hours w/ 1.5A ChargerSize: 4.64 x 3.03 x 0.51in (118 x 77 x 13mm)Weight: 4.37oz (124g)I was supplied a sample for the purpose of review.

The Lorex LW2201 Weatherproof Ultra Wireless Surveillance System works as well as advertised.  I only have a few minor gripes about the system.The set-up is incredibly simple.  Just plug the power supplies into the camera and into the receiver, screw on the antennas, and plug the A/V cables into some kind of monitor/TV.  My camera was already paired with the unit, so I didn't have to do anything else.  Pairing the wireless unit with the camera, or another camera is simple, just push 1 button on the receiver and a big yellow button on the camera and it's good to go.  They claim the pairing function securely connects your camera to your receiver so that no one else can grab hold of your camera's frequency, but I have no way to test that.  I'll just take their word for it.  The unit comes with a small arm stand for the camera, and a couple screws to mount it.  You might need extra screw mounting pieces if you want to mount it into something like brick, though.  Regardless, set-up and installation is a snap.The image is very good in daylight.  However, the night vision is only "acceptable" quality.  The far-red LED lights create a bit of a halo effect around the center of the camera's lens.  So everything in the center of the image looks clear at 20-25ft or less, but the stuff on the periphery of the center of the image is a little darker and fuzzier.  Still, you can see what's going on.  Also, be sure to note that you can see the red LED lights of the camera.  This is good if you want everyoen to know that you have a camera, but if you want a more stealthy hidign spot for your camera, it's definitely going to give it away. The mic works well at about 25 ft or less, but after that it definitely tails off.So to sum up the problems: I think the night vision image is a little bad at the edges of the image, the mic isn't all that great at longer distances, my unit had one dead LED bulb on it (I don't think it really affected the image quality by itself), and the far-red LEDs are not truly infrared so you can see them with the naked eye.The problems I stated are pretty minor in nature, though.  The Lorex system is solid and you can expand it to include 4 total cameras, and even view them simultaneously split-screen in your monitor.  Overall, I recommend this unit for a simple security solution.

This VANGUARD Xcenior 36 Photographic Equipment Shoulder Bag has a sleek look, large capacity, and some very nice "protective" features.The top flap opening to the bag's main compartment is attached to a broad and sturdy T-shaped handle, and is secured lengthwise with velcro and zippers. A set of side-wise locking-mechanisms layered on top of the zippers provides additional security.The main compartment can hold 1-2 pro DSLRs with grip plus 6-8 lenses, a flash unit, and a wide screen laptop that is up to 14" in size. Adjustable dividers in the compartment can help with the organization of the photographic gears. Zippered interior pockets can be used for holding small things like memory cards, etc.A full-size tripod can be secured to the bag's sturdy T-shaped handle with support from the provided two adjustable straps located near the handle.Two smaller, zippered compartments located externally at the front and back of the bag provide additional room for miscellaneous stuff.The anti-scratch and moisture-resistant fabric as well as the anti-shock feet on the bag's bottom provide different kinds of protection for your gears.Finally, the bag comes with a broad, anti-slip adjustable shoulder strap, very good quality rain cover, a name holder, and sleeves for use with a trolley.The bag has an attractive design, and the workmanship is top notch.Comment

Lasted less than a year.  Then one day I picked it up and the top stayed in my hand while the base (with the salt in it) stayed on the counter. I was able to line it up to get it back on, but now I have to be careful to press the top against the bottom when inverted, or the top falls off into whatever I'm grinding the salt into.  Loved it while it was in one piece. Will definitely NOT be buying other products from this manufacturer.******* original review below *******It took a while for me to get why this mill is so special, but now that I do I wouldn't ever buy any other style. At first I thought, "works, but wish I could leave it top down." You can't because the top is slanted instead of flat. But turns out that is a good thing, because that's why it doesn't leave a mess of salt wherever it is stored. You have to flip it to use it, instead of just picking it up and rotating the top. That extra wrist action is worth it for the reduced clean up later.Very attractive looking and quality construction. Grinds my Himalayan salt crystals quite well. Will probably buy other products from this manufacturer, since they make a quality product.

I purchased these Sony MDR-7506 headphones a couple of months ago. More recently, I've also purchased Sony's very similar (but not identical - see below)Sony MDR-V6 Monitor Series Headphones. My reason for purchasing a second pair (and getting both models) was because the V6's were very inexpensive at the time I ordered ($30 cheaper than the 7506's), and because I was curious to see if I could tell a difference between the two. In reading many of the reviews here on Amazon and elsewhere, I knew that the two are constructed very similarly but there were different opinions regarding both the differences (if any) and which has the superior sound.These are both headphones in the $100 and below price category, which seems like a reasonable price to pay for high quality sound. I do not like to listen to music using earbuds, I prefer this style of "over-the-ear" headphone. although it does take some getting used to. I also have very inexpensiveKoss KSC75 Portable Stereophone Headphonesfor occasions when I don't want to use my more expensive Sony's.I am very satisfied with the sound quality from these headphones (both models), and with the comfort. They are the first "quality" headphones that I have owned, and the level of detail in the sound, lack of distortion, and overall quality is simply superb. I am completely satisfied.Now, regarding both similarities and differences between the Sony MDR-7506 and Sony MDR-V6, here is what I have found:- externally, they are almost identical in construction and appearance. The V6 has red stickers on each headphone "for Digital", and the 7506 has blue stickers "Professional". The phono jack for the V6 is silver metal (all metal), for the 7506 is gold (rubber and gold). Those are the only visible differences I am able to detect.- when I listen to the two headphones back to back, at the same volume level and with the same music, I can detect a slightly clearer treble and midrange from the 7506, and very slightly clearer sound. This is consistent with several of the other reviewers on Amazon for the 7506, who say they can detect a slightly better sound separation and more open sound for the 7506, with the V6's sounding a bit warmer. One reviewer also says that the internal grounding for the two headphones is different - the 7506 uses a separate internal ground for the left and right channels, where the V6 uses a single ground for both channels. (It seems to me that both grounds are going to the same place anyway, but  if this is true - and I am not able to confirm whether or not it is - then maybe this contributes to a slightly different sound).- some reviewers state that the internal speaker drivers are different for the two headphones. However when I went to Sony's website to check the specifications for the two models, that is not what I found. And here is what the Wikipedia page states for these headphones: "both models share the same part number for their driver, but the magnet therein is known to vary. The MDR-7506 was introduced with a samarium-cobalt magnet, as is currently used in the MDR-V6. However, at some point, the MDR-7506 switched to the slightly more powerful neodymium magnet. These changes were made without changing the driver part number."- The box for the 7506 states "neodimium magnet". The operating instructions included with the V6 state "samarium cobalt magnet". Possibly either or both of these are out of date (as Wiki states, they may make spec changes over time) - the 7506 box is copyright 1994, and the V6 copyright is 1996.- The 7506 box includes an exploded view and parts list, which is very nice to have. The V6 box does not.- Both headphones include a soft case for storing/carrying the headphones, and both include an adaptor plug so that the headphone can be used with either a stereo mini-plug or a normal size plug.UPDATE 7/22/13 and 7/30/13: The Sony MDR-V6 and MDR-7506 headphones have just recently been reviewed by CNET, with similar comments to my own regarding the small differences in sound quality. Both headphones receive a very high recommendation from CNET. See the MDR-7506 review at [...] and the MDR-V6 review at [...]According to the specifications on Sony's website (which should be up-to-date), the only difference between the two headphones is frequency response:- MDR-7506:- Neodymium magnet, 40.0 mm driver, 10-20kHz frequency response, 63 Ohm impedance, 106 dB/W/m sensitivity- MDR-V6:- Neodymium magnet, 40.0 mm driver, 5-30kHz frequency response, 63 Ohm impedance, 106 dB/W/m sensitivityI am not able to explain how Sony is claiming different frequency response for the two headphones if all of the other specs are identical, but this is what they are saying. And I do believe that the two headphones have slightly different sound.Although there is a slight difference between the 7506 and V6, the sound from both headphones is very neutral. The "professional" choice between the two seems to be the 7506, and it is the more expensive model. If you read other reviews you will find that the 7506's are commonly used by studio engineers, because they are designed to reproduce the sound just as it is, without adding emphasis to the bass as some other headphones are designed to do. At this point, for me they are both excellent, and for thirty bucks less I'd go with the V6 if I could have only one of the two.I've been using these headphones with theAudioengine D1 24-bit digital-to-audio converter, and I am extremely pleased with the quality of the sound.As a final comment, some owners of these headphones comment that the ear pads wear out over time, and some owners also prefer different ear pads for better comfort. I also purchased a pair ofBeyerdynamic EDT 250 Velour Padded Earcushionsfor this reason, and although I have not yet given them a try to compare them with the ear pads that come with the Sony headphones, I do like the fact that it is possible to fit different ear pads if desired in order to provide more comfort, as well as replace them when they have become worn (some owners use these headphones for many years).I notice that there are a very large number of reviewers for these headphones, many of them long-time owners who feel very strongly that these Sony headphones are the best. I'm just beginning to use them, but I have high hopes that they will be durable over time and that I will continue to be as satisfied with them as I am today.

I went from my regular keyboard to a Logitech cordless and have been happy that I did so.  The next step is this couch-friendly, remote, mini keyboard/control pad, which I am using to run Media Center on my Vista PC.  For quality, ease of installation and functionality, I give it full marks.  For ease of use, I deduct a star.  When you shrink all this functionality into such a small space, there is bound to be some penalty.  Certain characters ( _ + - = \ " ' ) have been moved from their usual homes to other keys, where they require you to use the function key to type them.  As at least a couple of them ( - ' ) are very common, this can be irritating.  Also, I found the ClickPad, when used as a touch pad to point and click, very difficult at first, and I am still not comfortable with it.  The trick is to use thumb tips for everything.  This unit is not a replacement for your keyboard and mouse.  But, for couch potatoes determined to take root and for those keen to eliminate clutter, this elegant, light little gadget might be just the thing for you.

I am a small business owner. I own and utilize quite a collection of Cisco and Linksys products. Let's see, I have a Cisco PIX router, RVS4000 router, UC320 / SPA8800 / SPA504G Phone system, 3 Linksys WAP54G's that all funtion well and then there's this "Piece of Crap" WAP4410N (access point) that I purchased through Amazon. This thing was a pain in the ass right out of the box. I called Cisco and I gave them the serial number. They told me that the unit was out of warranty. I had to email them my Amazon receipt in order to get any help. The tech rep helped me access the device and load updated firmware. This damn Access Point will never stay connected for more than about 5 minutes.It is now a year since my purchase and I was trying to connect my laptop wirelessly today and I encountered every kind of strange behavior from the WAP4410N that you can imagine. If I power cycle it I can get it to connect for about 5 minutes and then it drops. I downloaded and installed 2.0.4.2 Firmware. I set up the security settings and entered a new USERNAME/PASSWORD and SAVED my settings. I then accessed the GUI with the new username and password. A few minutes later the GUI indicated that I had used an incorrect username and/or password. Now it required the default admin/admin username and password, which indicates that it independently reset itself to factory settings. I've never owned a more frustrating wireless access point. Typically Cisco support is quite good and I will likely give them more of my business, but the WAP4410N is one Cisco device I regret buying, even more than the SPA9000/SPA400 phone system (which was also a disaster). Amazon should suspend sales of this device.

This however will ware off soon I hope, and I don't think it will remove anything from the true suction power that kept it in place.For cars it comes with a disk you can glue on your dashboard, which is something I would never do, so it depends on your car configuration if you can sucker this against your dashboard, especially if you do not want to use the round sticky circle.

The desk arrived with a hairline crack in the keyboard tray.  I ordered 'chocolate' but to me and everyone else - it looks black.  It's too heavy for me to wrestle back to the Post Office to return it, or I would have.  It took two people over 2 hours to assemble.  Once I removed the useless shelf, (I put it on my work bench in the garage) I had more room for my printer and could move the monitor back about 3 inches.  The tower used up all the space below the drawer, so I wasn't able to use the shelf that came with the desk.  There isn't enough storage at all.  I had to buy an additional separate storage unit for computer paper, canned air, envelopes etc.  The shelf for the keyboard, constantly moves in and is very wobbly.  It's very annoying to be typing while the keyboard slowly retreats under the desk!

I broke my Samsung Stratosphere by dropping it while walking, and thus had to get a new phone.  Well, I decided to spring a few extra bucks for this guy.  I'm really glad I did!  I thought that getting used to a pure touch-screen interface would take a while (I bought the Stratosphere because of the slide-out keyboard,) but it didn't.  I would say I had pretty much adapted within an hour or two!This phone came with Ice Cream Sandwich, which is a tremendous upgrade over the Gingerbread OS that I was used to.  Things seem much more refined. It looks like us Nexus users will be getting Jelly Bean in a week or so, which I'm looking forward to checking out.Now onto some thoughts about the phone itself  Even though it has a huge screen, it's pretty light weight and feels very balanced in your hand.  The slightly concave screen makes for easier interaction with the touch screen.  The AMOLED display is AMAZING!  I have a much easier time reading smaller text on this than I do on my friends iPhone. The battery life leaves some to be desired.  I could make it through most of the day, so long as I put it on the car charger to-and-from work.  Picking up an extended battery is recommended for those who don't like charging their phone during the day.As far as storage, this guy comes with 32GB of non-expandable internal storage.  Since it's such a large amount, the non-expandable part ends up not mattering much to me.  I've put plenty of music on this device, and I still have plenty more space!While I'm not one to purchase a phone FOR the camera, having a nice one is certainly a nice perk.  The camera here is 5 megapixels, and it's wicked fast!  It'll snap off shots nearly as fast as you can tap!  The picture quality seems to be great, if you're in ideal lighting situations, and just OK in non-ideal settings (I'd be upset if this were only a camera, but it's so much more!)When it comes to the actual phone part, I have absolutely no complaints!  Only praise!  Call quality is great.  I haven't had any problems with getting signal in places I'd expect to get signal.  Since the 4G in my area is strong, I haven't had any issues with internet connectivity.Ultimately, I highly recommend this for anyone looking for an Android device through Verizon!**************************************************************************************************************Personally Recommended Accessories:Diztronic TPU case - adds minimal bulk/weight to the phone; good protection; Revision 3 took care of the issues with the cutouts for the buttons.Samsung Extended Battery (2100 mAh) - Adds minimal bulk/weight to the phone.  Allows one to go all day with medium to medium-heavy use without charging.ArmorSuit Screen Protector - A wet-apply screen protector.  ArmorSuit makes a high quality/high performance screen protector (I even use them on my PS Vita!)**************************************************************************************************************

These reviews are not easy to write and make them interesting. I ordered a couple of these OEM phone charging devices and they work as designed. I like the portability, and having the USB cord plug into the 110v receptacle is great because I can use just the cable to charge the phone via USB, and my device can be synchronized like an external drive.

One never knows when the power will go out or for how long it will stay out. Our longest time for pioneering it was about a week, and it wasn't fun. Recent events show that times without electricity can be much longer so we need to be prepared. This Epica cordless battery is a small device that could easily fit in a coat pocket. It's easy to carry in one hand and it even has a convenient handle so you can carry the radio with one finger.There are multiple charging options: hand crank, solar, or USB.  The large solar receptor does a good job charging the batteries; I've been leaving the radio on the windowsill so that it is always charged. Turning the handcrank isn't tedious; a minute of robust cranking will give you 20 minutes or so of radio time. The built in light is surprisingly bright; it can be used to find your glasses, other flashlights, or even to read for short periods.Reception is pretty good though it can be tedious pressing the tiny buttons to bring in a station. The volume buttons can produce a strong sound when turned up high. Quality of reception depends on location, direction, and the antenna. Some initially weak stations became robust and clear with a little movement of the unit and its antenna. WTOP, an FM all news station comes in great. I also like having the weather band which also comes in strong and clear.Want a just in case radio and not have to worry about replacement batteries? The Epica Emergency Solar Hand Crank radio is a viable option.

I am writing a five star review knowing that there is a chance these may not hold up. The wires for the headphones and remote are really thin. With that in mind, overall I must say I think they are impressive in value.The pros:Comfortable standard earbuds, with standard sizes included (small/medium/large)Remote that works great with iOS. Volume up/down, and standard universal remote button (start/stop).Microphone seems to work without any complaints from the other side of the call.You won't get any where near this good of sound and features for the price with other headphones I could find on Amazon.Negatives:Worried about the extremely thin wires not holding up.

I purchased both sizes of the Takeya tea maker - the 24 and 64 oz sizes.  I love them.  Since I purchased them a few weeks ago they have been in constant use with no complaints on my part!  I find the tea basket inserts very easy to use and to clean. When using loose tea a sharp whack on the counter gets the majority of the tea leaves out and a quick rinse removes the rest.  When using tea bags, cleanup is even easier.  I make sun tea, so I appreciate how tightly the lid screws on and the handles are perfect for toting them outside!  Nice feature!  The 64 oz pitcher is a little tall and I did have to adjust a shelf to accomodate it, but I didn't consider that a deterrent.  The plastic pitcher remains perfectly clear.  Although it says dishwasher safe, I would not bother since it's a quick wash and rinse job - and I wouldn't risk clouding the plastic.  All in all one of the best ice tea pitchers I have ever used.

I'd guess that about half the price of this item is the premium for the Powermat charging option. If you don't have Powermat product, and don't plan on purchasing one, then this product is not for you - why pay for something you'll never use?.  If you are a member of the Powermat family, then it's a great idea. Powermat adapters are available for almost any device - not just Apple devices - heck, even some cars now have Powermat chargers on the dash.The battery is not all that powerful - it will charge most phones 3-4 times, and the average tablet once. I rarely use battery packs to just charge devices - I'll have them attached on long commutes so the device runs off the external battery. Then when the external dies, I switch to the device - then I know EXACTLY how much time I have left.  This battery pack has a USB port so you can plug in your device's charge cable for non-powermat devices.So, if you're a Powermat user - this is a big thumbs up.  Not a Powermat user? Skip it. Four stars because of the mediocre battery capacity. I can't find an exact capacity given anywhere. The unit has "15.54 Wh" on the back. This means you have to do some math. Divide 15.54 (watt-hours) by the battery voltage (volts) for Ah (Amp-hours). USB is 5 volts, so I'll go with that - bit it's a guess. Multiply by 1000 to get mAh. This gives you 3100 mAh - equivalent to two good quality rechargeable AA batteries, or 4 cheap AA rechargeables.

I have purchased hundreds of products on Amazon, and I have been happy with the online service.  Most of the items I have kept because they stood up to their reviews, and I was happy with the products when I received them and consequently used them in my home.  However, I have only negative experiences with the Kworld HDmi Dvi VGA Qam/atsc External Digital TV Turner Box Hdtv.  I spent over 12 hours trying to get this device to work.  It simply does not do what it claims to do.  I have hooked up a computer monitor to it, a flat screen monitor (without a digital tuner) and when I moved the input to VGA, nothing happened.  No signal, no response.  After trying every available attachment and configuration, I decided the unit was defective and I sought to get a refund.  The reason why I use Amazon is for their convenient return policy, and I have never had a dispute about returning an item until now.  Instead of approving my return and sending me a return postage label, I'm now being asked to give my telephone number to the seller so I can be called about my return request.  What is the issue?  If I no longer want the device, just give me my money back.  I feel that Amazon should seriously consider not allowing this seller to advertise on its website if customers are going to be given a hard time about returning an item.  I'm very upset and I want everyone to know about it.

To keep our heating costs down, we've turned down the thermostat in the house and have started using space heaters in the areas we are currently using in the house. DON'T DO THIS! I was dumb and didn't read the instructions before doing this. The power supply on the heated got EXTREMELY hot. Like catch paper on fire hot. I have two of these cases, one is the USB 3 version which is basically the same as this one. If you have a USB 3 capable main board, get the other model! This one is USB 2 and eSATA on the front panel. PROS: The 3 big fans are not too noisy and keep this case and its components nice and cool. Power supply (not included) mounts in the bottom so it needs to have longish connectors and a bottom fan to utilize the filtered air (only for the ps) inlet  in the case although the front and back fan type power Supply works just as well. There is just the right amount of room to use a CoolerMaster Hyper 212 CPU cooler. Side cover is made for additional fans or radiator/fan setup and there are two grommeted holes in the back for tubes and or wires to pass through. The quick release drive bays are nice, plenty of screws are included if you prefer to use screws. 3.5" bays are separated nicely for air flow and the blue led lit fan in front blows right on them. A 2.5" hdd quick release adapter is provided that can be used in any of the 3.5" bays. Cable management options are good.CONS: Dust collects quickly and shows very plainly on the black mesh inlet for the front fan. Front bezel is glossy black fingerprint magnet. It pops off with firm finger pressure (a plus IMO) but use a tool to remove it and it is very easily dinged around the edge. If you need to pry the front loose with a tool, start on the bottom side then use fingers.

I saw this on Cooks Test Kitchen and figured I would try it out since they reviewed it and rated it so highly. It's the best.This garlic press works so much better than the Pampered Chef garlic press I have. The Pampered Chef press is made of aluminum. I use and review a number of kindle cases, and my biggest problem I've found is that cases often do not hold the kindle secure enough for my tastes. Since I buy kindle cases primarily to make sure my kindle is protected, this is a huge issue for me.First, it is a keyboard + case. The keyboard is a small Bluetooth keyboard which syncs quite well with the device and is easy to use and set up. The case itself folds out to create a kind of stand, and when not in use the case folds back up so the keyboard touches against the kindle's glass screen. I found the case itself to be poorly made. By necessity the top half of the kindle when seated in the stand will be unsecured. This poses a big problem because when not folded out as a stand, the Belkin case could slip off and the kindle will fall out. Second. The triangle stand kept collapsing when I tried to use it. 75% of the time it seemed to collapse under the weight of the kindle making it really unstable. Disappointing.The keyboard itself is tiny,and when the case is collapsed the keys are pressed up against the glass which seems an unlikely design.Overall, the case itself needs a re-design or the one I have is defective. My case did not hold my kindle securely, and the stand was too flimsy to hold the kindle properly upright to use as a screen.

This is a great universal remote but you need to spend a little time programming the Logitech remote to your liking. This is a onetime process and worth it. The main area that needs attention are the Additional Buttons: those are the buttons in the LED window. It seems as though Logitech just put them in alphabetical order. The Standard Buttons (the raised buttons on the remote control) are pretty much well programmed but you should add extra controls to your liking and it's very easy to do.I am controlling my Sharp LED TV, Yamaha receiver, Sony Blu Ray, Sony tape player, and my HP personal desktop computer.Once everything is set up, the remote should work great. I recommend the battery powered unit with AA batteries because it seems like many people are having trouble with Logitech's rechargeable unit with the charging cradle. In addition, why would anyone want to add extra bulk in their entertainment room with a charging cradle? Also, it would be nice if this remote can handle 7 devices versus 5.I had an issue downloading the software but found a work-around after calling Logitech technical support. I simply went to the Logitech.com website and downloaded the Logitech Harmony Remote Software (7.7.0). The technical support told me it's better than the logitech/harmony download.Once you're done programming it's best to close the Logitech Harmony Remote Software window, restart the program and then sync the program with the remote control.I was also having an issue getting the Logitech Harmony 650 remote to change my new Sharp Aquos LC-70LE733U to TV input mode but I found a work-around. Hopefully Logitech will fix this issue by making the remote's InputTV change the Sharp to TV input mode automatically.SOLUTION: Here's a loophole that will fix the problem. If your remote has a Favorite button you can set that as your TV input. Your Logitech remote will transmit the Favorite input signal and the Sharp will switch to the TV input. For example, I would set my Favorite A button to go to TV broadcast channel 2, and set the Watch TV on my Logitech remote to go to Favorite A, then my Logitech Watch TV button will change the Sharp to TV channel 2. Logitech needs to program InputTV to go directly to the TV input on the Sharp TV. Hopefully they'll fix this bug soon.UPDATED RATING TO 3.5: I still like this remote but I agree with the reviewer that the battery clips are not made well. As soon as you drop the remote onto a hard surface the battery clip will break. The remote will still work if  you can Mickey Mouse the battery contact with the battery again. I would like to give this remote a 3.5 because Logitech did not use durable springs in the battery compartment.

I have had this drive now going on a month. It was very easy to install into the second drive bay in my Mac Pro. All I had to do was remove the front CD Tray Plate before installing (if you don't do this, the tray will not be able to fit through the drive bay door).I have burned CD's, DVD's, and so far, one Bluray 25 GB data disc. Not only is the drive fast, but it is extremely quiet. It took about 30 minutes to burn a 24 GB Bluray disc, and the drive was so exceptionally silent, I had to put my ear to the computer to determine something was really happening. The output verified with no errors on all the discs I've burned.I have always purchased a Pioneer drive for my secondary burner on my Mac's. They are compatible and dependable in my experience. This one has, thus far, proved no different.

The GOgroove BlueGATE RCV is awesome. I have an older car that has an auxiliary port, but no fancy built in bluetooth like the newer model cars are all coming with. Rather than spending hundreds on a new radio I thought of giving this a try first, boy am I glad I did. Sound clarity is amazing. I am able to keep my smart phone plugged in to charge and in the cup holder, select a favorite play list, set it to shuffle, and go! Also tried hooking it up to a speaker in my home that wasn't bluetooth and it performed great there as well. Perfect for walking around the living room cleaning etc and having the music play through my favorite home theater. No more changing cd's or being limited to a radio station. Pairing is easy, simply press and hold the power button (while off) until the LED starts blinking red and blue, and then scan for a Bluetooth device from my phone. It shows up instantly and connects in a few seconds. From there, you simply plug the attached AUX cable into a AUX IN port on a radio or speaker and press play on your music. It couldn't be any easier! Another big plus is the charging cable, it comes with a micro USB cable for charging it, which takes about 3 hours and after a full charge I have observed about 15 hours or more (as advertised) of playback time. In case you lose the charging cable, you can use any other micro USB cable you have, this is a big plus for those of us who routinely lose charging cables. I highly recommend this product, for the price and ability of turning a non Bluetooth audio output into a Bluetooth enabled one, it can't be beat.

I was fortunate enough to inherit a Sony DSLR, and quickly got up to speed on the basics of digital photography and photo composition. As I gained more experience and knowledge about what I was doing, I quickly learned that the kit lenses that came with my camera were okay, but limiting what I could accomplish with my photography.This is the first lens I ever bought. On an APS-C sensor, it hits more like 75mm, which was a *bit* too narrow for what I really wanted. That said, I made do and forced myself to begin experimenting with the extremely open aperture and fast response that this lens provides.After I bought this lens, I never, ever took it off my camera. Not once. That's how much I liked it over the kit lenses.I shot several family weddings and events with this lens and camera, and the results are spectacular. Some of these photographs are online:[...][...]After growing beyond this lens and camera, I decided to move up to a full-frame body, and at the same time made the jump to a Nikon body. I still keep my Sony DSLR around for its size, weight and portability, and when I use it, there's no other lens I bother with.

*Disclosure: I was contacted by Arctic and given a sample of this product specifically for the purpose of giving my thoughts on it.*Test Devices:HTC One and EVO 4G LTESamsung Galaxy S2, S3, S4, and NexusGoogle Nexus 7 (original)Tegra NoteKindle Fire HDXiPad Mini, iPad, and iPad AirI admit to having quite a collection of Bluetooth speakers, finding them to be very useful devices.  The Arctic model is similar in specification to most others on the market, but with an admittedly higher price tag.  Pairing up with each of my test devices was a cinch, both manually and with NFC (on the devices that support it).  The controls worked fine, but I think Arctic missed two steps here.  With other speakers I have, such as theSatechi BT Touch, the volume controls on the speaker toggle on the device they are connected to, rather than being individual. For example, turning the volume down on the speaker turns the volume down on the device.  The way it is now, the Arctic speaker and whatever device is driving it both have individual volume adjustments.  Secondly, no track control on the speaker itself.  The sound output is good, better than than the el-cheapo models I've come in contact with.  But, there isn't as much bass or total volume as there are in the better models.  The lack of volume seems to be a theme across the Arctic line, as I noted the same in their other products.  The battery life is great!  Though it's only rated at 8 hours, my unit completed 14 hours of playback at mid volume and still have power to spare.  The finish on the unit is something I liked, a matte rubbery finish, very subdued in appearance.  Arctic tries to do some value adding to justify the premium price by including a zipper pouch to keep your speaker in and decent quality cables.  It's a good speaker, that's for sure, but, myself, I'm thinking the current price ($50) is too high compared to the competition.

It took a total of 15 mins to get this modem up and running. 8 mins on the phone with Comcast and 7 mins for the modem to update. The self install screen did not come up so I had to call Comcast to get it set up. Comcast asked for my phone number, customer serial number on the modem, and MAC address for the modem. They were very helpful.Speeds with the old modem:Speakeasy speed test: down: 17 up: 4Comcast speed test: down 15 up 4Speeds with new modem:Speakeasy speed test: down: 25 up: 4Comcast speed test: down 23 up: 4Both speed tests showed the same amount of improvement and cut my ping time nearly in half.I did not get improvement in my upload speed, no big deal for me. I also noticed improved on line game play for my PS3. You can't go wrong with this modem.

I've decided I'm just too old to keep buying things that need to be assembled, BUT there is very little out there that is assembled and fits in a tight space. The case itself is such a disappointment.  Really it could be decent, but the silicone is far too loose and flexible.  It falls off the S5 while coming out of the holster, and isn't improved by even mild use.  It's just not a good fit and the hard plastic back doesn't lock it onto the phone properly.  Also the kickstand is too hard to use without feeling like you are going to break it.For this alone I would give it 1 star (not worth your time or money).  HOWEVER - the holster redeems the phone.  It fits PERFECTLY with the Spigen Slim Armor case: http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00I3UWGJW/ref=oh_details_o05_s00_i00?ie=UTF8&psc;=1 and holds it both securely and makes it easy to retrieve.  Seriously, if Spigen produced this holster, I would have bought it alone for this price.  The holster has a standard belt swivel with detents to with normal 15 degree stop points with strong clasp.  It also has a kickstand to hold the clasp open that will serve as a decent stand, but with the Spigen case, you can't put the phone completely in the holster with the screen forward.  You can, however, use it as a stand by resting the phone on the bottom.  So it's a useful alarm clock or maybe even video use, but the phone seems like it could flip out easily.Anyway - the case is junk.  BUT, if you, like me, found the Spigen Slim Armor to be the best case for the S5, the holster here is tip top.  Giving it a 3 overall based on that fact alone.  Otherwise, don't buy.
this case protects phone very well i love the case i have had it for about a year no complaints at all

Pros:They actually fit in your F****** ear!So inexpensiveFantastic sound qualityI have been through so many pairs of headphones it could be a joke. I was riding my bike one day and I yanked these out of my ear on accident. the cord got tangled in the pedals and i ran over one of the buds, cracking it. I upgraded my Denon 2809CI for the SR5008.  The SR5008 chassis size is not as deep so it doesn't take up as much space on the shelf.  Since this is the bedroom I have a simple two channel setup with Axiom M60 floorstanding speakers and HSU Research STF-2 subwoofer.  I like the attention to detail such as the little vibration absorbing material (looks like Sorbothane soft rubber) on the feet to protect the electronics from the nearby subwoofer.Previously I could not use Audessy with the older Denon because of the simple speaker setup.  Now with the Marantz SR5008 I was able to fly through the Audessy measurements and the sound difference is NIGHT AND DAY!  With the Audessy calibrated stereo setup, the sound is 3-dimensional and super clear.  I'm also selected the bi-amp option for the front speakers just to try it out (not sure if that makes a difference, but it's cool to have the option to put those extra Watts to use).  I would describe it as having a "warm" sound which I love.  I used to be a musician and I can distinguish subtle differences in sound quality.The on-screen menu is very simple and easy to navigate.  When streaming Pandora Internet Radio, the song name and play timer is displayed on the Marantz portal window so I can still see the song name while the TV is turned off.  Some review on the web mentioned the lack of WiFi, but I think it would be silly to integrate WiFi into a high end AVR receiver because WiFi technology is changing so fast (802.11G, N, AC, etc. will be obsolete well before the receiver is outdated).  Simply get a tiny WiFi adapter such as the Netgear WNCE2001 or WNCE3001, plug it in and you're set for wireless as well.

AmazonBasics 3.2 feet 1.0 Meter USB Charging/Sync Cable for Apple iPod, iPhone, and iPadI have been searching for a cable that would work with my IPOD Classic, at first I could find was the original Apple cable which was a tad bit costly. I decided to research Amazon to see if they happen to have a cable for my IPOD, what I was looking for was an Apple 30 Pin Cable to USB for an IPOD. Ninja Master Prep Pro System $54.66 @ Amazon.com. My model has a 450W motor and 3 processing containers, including 40 oz. processing bowl, 48 oz. Pitcher, and the 16 oz. Bowl plus lids, blades and splashguards for all 3.  I had another blender type product on order for $209, but canceled it after I found this one.  This has worked well and will work well for everyone IF YOU PAY ATTENTION AND PLACE THE PARTS TOGETHER PROPERLY BEFORE OPERATING.  My blender has NO DEFECTS, it is OPERATOR ERROR to NOT PAY ATTENTION WHEN OPERATING a machine with metal blades and then find bits of plastic in your food BECAUSE the blades are not lined up with the base of the pitcher and the motor.  PAY ATTENTION! This blender has plastic containers, plastic lids, and plastic spindles and this is what I expected for only $50. The blades are VERY SHARP and I cut myself just removing them from the cardboard - again OPERATOR ERROR!  I should have been more careful!  I have noticed, after using this blender 2-3 times a day for 2 weeks, that it helps to use the PULSE method of blending as instructed in the manual.  BLEND.... STOP... BLEND... STOP...  It helps to let some of the larger ice cubes or chunks of food benefit from gravity and drop back to the bottom.  This has worked each time for me.My vote is 5 stars.  It is a blender with 3 different containers to hold what I have blended, with removable blades for easy cleanup, lids for all three containers, and the power is ADEQUATE for the $50 price tag.  My green smoothie is not as smooth as yogurt, it is grainy, but that is what I get when I use a $50 blender. If I wanted a smoothie the consistency of YOGURT or WATER from a blender, I would BUY A JUICER.

This is a flat little matte nylon pouch with a "brushed' finish to it. Lightweight, and for its small size, it has 3 compartments inside -- the middle one being quite capable of swallowing up my Fuji FinePix A 340 and keeping it well protected behind two layers of padding. There was still room up front for the flash cards, albeit (logically) in their little plastic cases, as there is little or no protective padding for a card by itself.The flap in the front of the case fastens with velcro -- it needs a slightly longer velcro piece there to stay closed more securely and to be less prone to having the front flap open after the wearer brushes up against a hard object.The belt "loop" also has an adjustable velcro fastener, and that seems to be staying fastened, very well. The case, with camera in it, fits nicely on the belt and rides on the belt comfortably, without being in the way.The wrist strap is a handy touch, even though my camera has one itself -- the one that comes with the case can also be detached and stowed in the rear inner compartment.Although I have just started to "field test" this camera case, I am quite happy with it.UPDATE JULY 2012 : Still using this and it has held up VERY well and continues to protect my camer. Excellente.UPDATE November 2012 : Wow this is one tenacious and rugged little pouch!!!!UPDATE - May 2013 -- Wow this is a durable little case! Even though some of the inside padding wore out from constant use, it is still VERY useful and utlilitarian!!! My Fuji bit the dust but this case is working out just fine for my newer camera.

Let me start by saying, if you aren't going to root this phone, I would rate it a 2.  Only reason this phone gets a 4 is because it can be rooted.*No Message Notifier - In order to see if you have a new email, text or missed call you have to hit the button to turn on the screen (work around install NoLED app).  I just want to say, just because somebody (iPhone) does something one way, doesn't mean you need to copy this. Good Job Samsung.*Can't take a picture while on a phone call with default software - I have no idea what is up with this. Every phone that has a camera does not have this limit.For now I'm using Camera 360, which allows me to the camera while on the phone.  Good Job Verizon.*Verizon Bloatware - This part is the worst!  There are plenty of apps that come pre-installed that you can't uninstall (without rooting) that are a pay service.  One example is CityID.  This software tells you what City and state your incoming call is coming from.  Well, you'll get a popup to Agree or continue to use trial mode.  If you happen to accidentally click Agree, you will be charged $3/month.  Are you serious?  Verizon already has the most expensive plans out there and now they want to milk more money from the consumer with this kind of stuff.  Good Job Verizon.Reception - Reception is questionable on this phone.  There are plenty of discussions of how this phone will get less reception than the Droid X at the same location.  Hope Samsung corrects this in an update.  Good Job Samsung.*No mute (physical) button - What is with these new phones that don't have a mute button?  So you are on the phone, you need to mute.  You have to unlock the phone, and then hit mute.  Or if you are doing other stuff on the phone, you have to drag open the notification bar and hit mute.  Again, just because iPhone does something, doesn't mean it's the best way.  Good Job Samsung.*When on a phone call, the phone loves to lock itself -  Even is you have the timeout for 2 mins, the phone will sometimes go dim and lock itself after like 10 seconds.*Not enough customization setting options - Like you can't set one sound for email and a different sound for sms and another sound for mms.  it's just one setting for 'notification'*2GB core RAM - argh!  Come on now.*Battery Life is OK - A basic day of usage and the phone is crying for a charge.  This isn't with game playing or leaving apps open and such like that.  Just some texting and calls.Ok, that's enough of the negative.  And based on that you would think I would not like the phone, but I do actually like the phone. The sad thing is it could be so much better, but it's the best out of the selection we have.  Rooting does help with some of the complaints I have and it's not that hard to do.It's thin, lightweight, snappy, picture quality is great for a camera phone, screen is crisp, uses standard micro-usb for charging, easy to navigate the interface.

Motorola MotoACTV wrist strapI used this wrist strap for running several times and did not encounter any problems. I also have a Garmin Forerunner 305 and the strap on the 305 started to crack at one part where the buckle is found but it did not impair the functionality of the strap. On the MotoACTV, the wrist strap has two buckles  to split the load. There's also a metal plate with 2 studs that helps retain the free part of the strap. Others complained about it coming out, but if you look closely it is designed to do that. There's nothing permanent about how it clips onto the strap. Overall it is a very sturdy design based on a polymer resin material and metal buckles. I don't have any issues with the strap and I found that this is the best and easiest way to use the MotoACTV.Overall: 5/5 stars

I've tried a number of rubber tipped styli in the past and they didn't have a good feel on my Galaxy Tab.  This stylus has a great feel when writing due to it's fiber tip.  It also is just the right length to fit perfectly across the top of my tablet, I use an elastic strap to attach it to the top.  It also has a ball point pen which is very handy.  Sometimes when taking notes there is just too much information (numbers especially) for me to quickly copy into my tablet even with the best handwriting recognition. In this case, I have a few post it notes on the inside cover of my tablet and I can switch to the pen and start writing notes straight away.Pros: Good feel to the fiber tip on the stylus.Right size for my application at 11 cm.Has a ballpoint pen that is easy to use.You can replace the pen cartridge when it runs out.Cons:  I wish the pen barrel had a little more texture to it, but I haven't had any problems writing with it at all.Overall a great product at a great price.

All in all, this is a good screen protector. Zero color distortion. Zero image blurring. Nice thick plastic. Application was very easy - just follow the instructions: apply the taped edge to the tablet, from the bottom first.  Then with a ruler or flat-edge gently press the screen protector to the tablet, sliding up.  Work slowly to prevent air bubbles.

I have several different tablet products since I evaluate software for tablets.  While most tablets work just fine with your finger, using these stylus pens makes it much easier to tap a small icon (I have very large hands) and also keeps the amount of finger prints on the screen down.I have had these in use for several months and have had no issues with them.  They still continue to function correctly and the tips are still in one piece!My only issue with them is that for me they are a bit short in length.  I have very large hands and if they were just an inch longer they would be perfect.

1st off:  this company stands behind their guarantee.  Great customer service.2nd:  Once applied-  you can't tell its there.  If not for a little more definition to the iPhone 5s home button - you'd be hard pressed to tell it's got a cover.REALLY does resist fingerprints.Cleans up easier than original screen.to clarifiy my first comment:  I applied this screen to my phone - but had problem with corner.  It wouldn't seal at the very corner.  (I have since realized I did not have the alignment perfect).  I sent email to Tech Armor and they immediately replied and sent me a replacement to try.  Second attempt was flawless.Tips:  your result will only be as good as your prep.  Get it clean.  tiny dust results in air bubbles.  I used an ultraviolet light to check for dust and then used the supplied dust removal tabs (which work GREAT).Another TIP:  position the screen protector on your phone -  DO NOT APPLY PRESSURE - you can just lay it on the phone - and still be able to shift it into place gently from the edges.  Once you have it in place- apply pressyre from the middle - and it sucks itself down to the phone (from then on -it's on...cant really move it).

When the Blurex case I got for my Nexus 7 didn't work as advertised, I bought this case.  It's engineered so that folding the case open doesn't cause the tablet to sleep, which is what the Blurex case didn't do.  However, the handstrap of this case is located too close to the spine, so if you have the N7 mounted with the non-mountable flap left (basically, if you're a righty) then you can't hold the case comfortably in one hand with it folded fully open.Otherwise it's a decent case.  It holds the tablet quite securely.  The notches are great to set it on a desktop or your lap to watch videos or type.  Shame about the hand strap.Full disclosure: a Blurex rep has contacted me and offered to send me a replacement of their case with the magnet issue corrected.  If it does indeed work, I'll be returning the MoKo case to Amazon.  The hand strap issue is noticeable and fairly omnipresent, and it really interferes with functionality.

This heater has two heat levels but the low level only works at MAX temperature.  At high heat level it has temperature control from 60 to 85 F.  Why not temperature control at low heat level??? For a foot warmer under my desk it is way too powerful.  Back it goes.

Up until this point, I have always used the Spigen Tough Armor case.  Prior to that, I was an Otter box or Speck fan like everyone else.This case by New Trent is a step up in terms of protection without sacrificing slimness and weight.  This feels very much like the Tough Armor case except there is a thin sheet of plastic covering the screen.  This is the best part.  I have strayed mostly away from water resistant cases due to space between the plastic and the screen, bubbles, and deformities.  With this case, you can't even tell the plastic is there.  The only hit being where the home button is, which is understandable.The ports are all well covered and I would have no problem recommending this for those of us in the Pacific NW.  Perfect for skiing, snowboarding, snow shoeing, x-cross skiing, etc.  Remember, this case is not intended to be submerged (waterproof), it's covered with respect to being water resistant.  I see no issues with heavy rain, or any of the other activities listed above.This case really does have everything covered in terms of what competitors have done in the past, but they've done it better.The only area I can really pick at, is the screen is not completely smudge proof.  I use the Tech Armor Ballistic glass as a screen protector, and it would be nice if the clear plastic part of the case was coated with some type Anti-fingerprint "Oleophobic" Coating.  I have some give in this area, as the idea may be to reduce overall cost, but nonetheless, a feature I would like to see in future versions.Personal preference:  I would probably find myself using this more during the winter months.  The reason being, I need quick access to the vibrate function, charging outlet, and headphone jacks.  Between this and the Tough Armor case, I have all occasions covered.

This is my third Pioneer head unit and by far the best one I have ever owned.  I have Pioneer's XM tuner and HD Radio tuner installed in my car as well.  With this head unit, I have 5 sources to choose from: CD (w/MP3 & WMA support), XM, HD Radio, USB input, and aux in from my Zune.  I connected a 16 gig flash drive to the rear USB input and it works really well.  You can use the display to browse to whatever artist and album folder you want to listen to.  The large display also supports the HD and XM tuners really well.  With XM, I can finally see the artist and song names at the same time. This TV is fantastic good quality image with a lot of calibration settings with 2 custom setting and is the best passive 3d in the market.But I return 2 of this TV because of the Back-light bleeding. This TV has serious problem with that and I want an uniformity screen.I purchase my TV's from my local Best Buy and I have 30 days to test the TV's before I return it.I did't want an active 3D TV but I choose the Panasonic Plasma P55ST50 that I can use the cheap Samsung glasses for $20.The 2D quality image of this Plasma is superior to any other TV in this range but I still prefer the passive 3D of the LG over active 3D.If you have a LG without back-light bleeding is a blessing and keep it.

I bought two of these for the autos. First one swallowed the SD card and I had to remove the cover ( you need to peal off the sticker in the front in order to get to the single screw that holds the two halves together. ) I find that the SD card had missed the receptacle and was laying on top. Not in the socket. Looked into why and I see that the SD card slot is not aligned with the socket at all. I took the edge of a pocket knife and scraped away at the slot until the SD card smoothly went in and mated with the socked.I tried the second one and oops...same problem same solution. Now both are at least alive.Now it was time to try them out.  But wait the instructions are in spanish. grrrrFinally found a video on youtube that explains how to operate the camera.The video is dark on both cameras (indoors) I hope it is better outdoors.My biggest disappointment is the motion detection. After getting it into motion detection mode. After you et it into motion detection mode it will begin to record. After two minutes of no motion it will go into standby and wait. If it sees motion it will begin recording again which is what I was after. This saves a lot of memory while you car is parked. But somewhere in standby there is a timeout. It goes into standby and after a period of time it powers down.  You need continually check that the power is on and if not, turn it on and put it back into motion detect mode. Not very useful at all. What I want is for it switch between record and standby constantly monitoring for motion all day long or until the battery runs out.I would not recommend this to anyone that had motion detection requirements.Otherwise it is a very small video recorder that has many uses. So I give it 3 stars.

My wife needed a new case for her S3 and I came across this, mainly because she likes teal.  The tabs or bumpers at each corner can be changed from black, white, purple or teal.  The case is VERY high quality and all the holes line up (Headphone jack, vent hole on the top vent hole on the bottom and USB plug) The buttons have a great feel and are also just as responsive with the case as they are without it.If you're looking for a great case for your S3, this is it!

I really wanted to love this product. The scale is attractive with lots of features and has a nice backlit screen but it was clear from when I opened the product that quality control was lacking slightly. The screen was not glued in at the optimal angle meaning that I could only view it well if I was looking at it from the left side; viewing from the right would exclude a digit.  But the real reason for a 1 star review is that the device did not function properly. The scale would not tare consistently with different weighted bowls. I could place a lightweight plastic bowl on the scale, tare the scale, and then add my ingredients. However if I repeated the same process with a different weighted bowl and then add the same ingredients I would get a different weight in the end.  I thought "Fine. Just weigh on the scale then." This proved the nail in the coffin. The scale would not recognize weight differences of less that 5 grams! Not at all the advertised 1 gram increments. Simply put this is a poorly functioning device and I will not buy Ozeri again or recommend them. I'm not a professional chef but I am a prosumer cook.  I went back to my old cheap $3 scale that at least measures ingredients properly and tares reliably. This scale however may work for you if you are only weighing larger items. It seemed to be better adjusted for heavier objects and would be accurate on comparison. But if you need pinpoint accuracy on lightweight ingredients then this scale is not going to work. Stay away!

The vacuum shipped quickly and in good condition. When I opened the box, the product was in 4 pieces, 1 of those being a screw and it all came together in under 2 minutes.  This is much quieter and much lighter than my full size vacuum and the bonus is I had my 8-year-old using it in the first 5 minutes after I started using it.  I bought this because since buying my last full sized vacuum about 6-months ago, I replaced all my carpeting with wood/laminate and the full size is fairly loud on the hard wood floors.  I gave this a 4-star rating only because I just put it together today and used it for a short time so I am not sure if it will deserve 5 starts yet. I will try and post an update after I have had it for awhile.

When I received this chopper, I wanted to put it to the test. Sure, most things can chop up tomatoes and onions, but what about something hard, like carrots? So I threw about 10 baby carrots into the Pull Chop and gave it a whirl. It did a fantastic job! With about 6 pulls, the carrots were coarsely chopped. I was impressed!I have to admit, I am a *very* lazy cook and I hate chopping items. I think this Pull Chop is going to be a godsend for me because it's an incredibly easy way to chop foods. You don't have to mess with an electrical appliance (which is good, because I live in a kitchen with only one plug) - just dump the food in the bowl and pull.This was also easy to clean up - wash the bowl and blades (careful, they're sharp) and wipe off the lid. Done!I was incredibly fortunate and received this through Amazon Vine. However, I think it's well worth the cost and would recommend it.

I bought a 36-inch power-strip for my workbench and a 24-inch version for attachment to the back of a drawer.  They come with clips that make it easy to attach the clips to the surface and snap the metal power strip into the clips.  The sockets are far enough apart that you can attach almost any "wall wart" without blocking the next socket.  And maybe that important wall wart gets a wire-tie so it doesn't fall out.  Both units were equipped with a snap-covered power switch, which I found a very nice feature.  These are a great value when they are on-sale, and not a bad value when they aren't.There is no surge protection or filtering, just power distribution.  That makes them affordable and long-lasting.

This has many reviews and it is unlikely this will be read, but I would like to state my approval and satisfaction with this.A few notes, in case anyone reads this (which means you probably read many others):* The new model securely attaches the nozzle so it cannot blow off* It is essentially an small rather powerful electric motor like a vacuum, and it there are defects or damage, it could smoke, etc like any other such device. This Tecsun PL-600 is very similar to the PL-660. The differences being the 660 has synchronous detection, separate USB/LSB offset, and the Aviation Band. Overall the PL-600 performs better than the 660: better sensitivity on the FM, MW, and SW bands. The audio seems to be both stronger and clearer. Also susceptibility to EMI is slightly less. For my listening tastes the aviation band is useless to me. Single side band really isn't a major need, although I use it from time to time, so the 660's offset isnt a big deal. Synchronous detection is an improvement but not a major improvement. So for my preferences the PL-600 is the one to go with. Overall better performance for a very attractive price.

2 of my MDs are hot on my trail to eliminate dust mites from my environment. These pillow protectors are helping me to save my investment of several hundred dollars worth of Tempupedic pillows. I've used this Crock-Pot several times in the few weeks that I've owned it, and I can say that I am truly more than pleased with this purchase!  My previous slow cooker was a Rival Brand crockpot which tended to boil my food on low.  I never felt comfortable leaving my old slow cooker on while I was at work for 9+ hours each day.  For any recipe that called for 8 hours of low cooking time, the meal would be ready within 3-4 hours and would start to scortch the sides shortly after!When I started my search for a new crockpot, I considered all options, including high end ranges like All Clad and Breville.  I'm MORE than thrilled that I went the route of this Crock-Pot.  Yes, it isn't as "programmable" as some (it's options for cooking are ONLY low: 8 or 10 hours and high: 4 or 6 hours - it also has a WARM setting).For my very first use, I decided that I would try it on a Saturday, when I could monitor how the slow cooker actually cooked the food.  I didn't really want to leave the house using an appliance for the first time.  I noticed that it cooked the chicken very slowly.  I was thrilled with the results.  An 8 hour recipe really did take 8 hours, which was SO unlike my other slow cooker!  It really does cook on low - barely a simmer - which is exactly how a slow cooker should be!  After the cook time is up, it automatically switches to the WARM feature, and remains on WARM for up to 6 hours (which is a nice safety feature in case you forget to turn off the pot).Clean up is a breeze!  The insert is dishwasher safe, and the brushed stainless steel wipes up perfectly.  It is a very large appliance, so be sure you have the counterspace or the storage space for it!  The lid fits snuggly on to the pot - it has a rubber seal, which really helps at preventing a sliding or bubbling lid.Overall, I'm thrilled with this purchase - I'm so glad that I decided to take a chance on this slow cooker instead of spending 3-4 times the amount on a high-end one!

When i bought this Product i had no intentionof putting it on right away as it is my Back upto another tempered glass screen protector.But the Price was right with good reviews overall.ALWAYS investigate before purchasing, as i Do.I'm almost positive i won't be let down once i use thisproduct.

Excellent cover.  great value .  This is my second Diztronic cover and as expected, it is a quality product.  The cover is snug on the phone with no gaps or sags. It provides a good grip. (not too grippy, not too slick).  Adds barely no bulk to the phone and the matte color looks like the actual phone so there is no major change from the phone with the cover to the phone without.  There is a slight gloss to the tpu material on the face of the phone but it is subtle and barely noticeable.  That glossy edge provides good protection to the edges of the phone. (my biggest concern) it protects from drops and damage from laying the phone face down.  I chose the matte black over the transparent one because the my last Diztronic cover (galaxy note 2) was transparent and it was also an excellent cover but it did begin to turn yellowish after a few months of use.  Overall i am very satisfied with this cover.  There are other fancier and bulkier covers out there but if you are looking for minimal bulk added to your phone, a stealth look and great protection for a great price...this is the cover.

This may work well connected to the phone, but my S3's charge connector is hosed. This is not compatible with the Samsung standalone charger.

Gripes: 1. does not hold a charge for very long (maybe 3 hours of continuous use max), 2. proprietary charger so can't use an available usb - have to carry around the ac charger or purchase their car charger. 3. no on/off button so battery will run down while not in use, but will disconnect itself from bt when not used for a short time - 50% of the time will easily reconnect, the other half it will take many tries to get it reconnected (might be a phone issue - galaxy nexus).  has trouble connecting to phone sometimes, which is usually solved by turning off bt on phone, then back on then reconnecting (might also be phone issue). 4. doesn't hold charge in standy - Fully charged but unused for a day, then used for an hour before it died. But charge holding has improved a bit over time. (haven't had a repeat of the one hour issue)  5. starts to drop out if more than 10 ft from phone 6. rubber ear bud covers sometimes come off when you're not looking (have permanently lost one so far)Likes: Keeps connection well with only occasional hiccoughs. Ear pieces could be smaller, but they're ok, stays in ears well. they are nice and light to wear. sturdy - they've been banged around quite a bit and no problems. phone calls sound fine on my end and I've had few complaints from others. had issue with no indicator lights, called tech support and mostly resolved by resetting the headset, but still no on/off indication.

These are great screen protectors for my new phone.  They were easy to install and work great.  I couldn't have asked for a better product to protect my new Galaxy S5.  I would recommend these to anyone with this phone

I am very satisfied with this product. As an individual with many devices (Samsung Galaxy 2, iPad Mini, iPad Classic), I usually need to be charging multiple devices at one time - especially when I am in class/running from place to place. This dual port charger is the perfect solution for me - when outlets are limited, this charger saves space and frustration as it allows fast charging of two devices at once. I can't remember a time when I've only needed to charge one device at a time (after all, this is the gadget age...). This charger works on all of my devices thus fat, and it charges them fast! Faster is better!This charger is great for on-the-go charging - It's "charging prong" is easily collapsible into the charger - allowing for quick and safe storage in any sort of purse or bag. I've been frustrated with chargers that do not do this, as a prong is easily crushed under the weight of a computer or textbook - not so with this charger! The size of the body is also pretty compact and light for easy carrying.I would make three possible improvements to this product:- I always appreciate a smaller product - this one is pretty compact, but any space-saving improvements would be appreciated.- It should come with USB cables (at least a micro USB) for convenience.- **The output ports should be labeled - I'm pretty sure they aren't equal output, and clear labeling would be more consumer-friendly.Overall, I would definitely recommend this charger.

Purchased these for my Bluair 601 after my normal internet search and these were the least expensive out there.  Only one problem found and it is trivial: the visible portion that shows through the screening didn't match up to the screening and leaves a border around what is visible.  Hard to explain and no big deal but does annoy. Will purchase replacements from this vendor when it is time to replace current filters.

My brother has a Keurig and after seeing/trying it, my husband thought he might like one as well.  He usually has a pot of coffee on weekends, but during the week he'd like to be able to make one cup before going to work.  I was hesitant about the Keurig since it's expensive, and there are many negative reviews with the same issue - it stopped or never worked properly.  And I don't know, but the term "scaling" just turns me off as it's probably something I'd end up doing and I don't even drink the coffee! So, I shopped around and came across this Hamilton Beach one cupper.  It was less than $20 (as of this writing) and comes with an 18-count bag of the Senseo coffee that it takes. We decided to take a chance, and now I'm very glad we did.  We love this little pot.  The Senseo "pods" look like thick teabags, actually.  They are easy to load in the machine, and my husband found them flavorful.  One pod was enough for his cup, though the machine does allow for two pods at once if you like your coffee strong.  He uses one of my measuing cups to fill the machine when he makes coffee.Yes, the Keurig has features that this does not, but I'm not certain they are better.  Keurig has infinitely more flavor offerings with the K-Cups, whereas Sesnseo has about 8 flavors: with Hazelnut and Vanilla being the only true "flavors".  However, K-cups are expensive, and take up more storage space (no worries about the senseo pod being crushed).  Plus, my husband uses flavored creamers anyway.  The bigger Keurigs have reservoirs, which does make it more convenient.  However, if the water sits too long it becomes stale and I suspect this is also where the "scaling" comes in.  It's easy enough to pour the water in each time with the Hamilton Beach.  And, for many kicthens, counter space is a premium.  This HB has a very tiny footprint compared to any of the Keurigs.  One disadvantage of the HB is that it won't take a jumbo sized mug; a standard mug from the new Corelle dishes lines will fit but nothing bigger.  Still, if you want a jumbo sized cup, why not make a full pot of coffee?Overall, I highly recommend this reasonably priced one cupper; especially for those that want to keep their main coffee pot anyway.  Please see my pictures of the actual unit we got (though note that the pot comes with the regular Breakfast Blend Senseo, not the Hazelnut).UPDATE (9-19-12): Senseo pods have been going out of stock, and I never found them in any of my local stores.  Happily, there is a new line of pods that have more flavors and are available on Amazon.  Baronet Coffee Pods.  Check them out!Baronet Coffee Pods Chocolate Babka, Medium Roast, 16-Count Box (Pack of 3),Baronet Coffee Hazelnut Medium Roast (140 g), 18-Count Coffee Pods (Pack of 3)and many more.

We've been geocaching a long time, and while our trusty Garmin has taken us to many caches, but we're tired of keying in caches manually and taking printouts with us.  This seemed like an ideal solution.What we love:All the cache information being right there on the GPS - no more printouts!Excellent accuracy - not surprising that technology has improved, but this gets us quite near the caches.Color screen.What we don't love so much:Interface is not intuitive and enclosed manual is not helpful - must download full manual online.Included caches kept getting in the way of ones we'd downloaded - we finally deleted the "helpful" top caches file.Downloading caches from site requires multiple steps each time - doesn't remember your device type.Buttons are either hard to press and unresponsive or overly sensitive (center stick).I've seen some other people recommend this for beginning cachers - I can't say I agree.  As an experienced cacher and a technologically savvy user, I found this unit getting in our way more than it helped us.  It's also significantly more expensive than our original Garmin was years ago - we paid $100 for a basic unit and it took us to hundreds of caches just fine.

This was the third USB 3.0 hhub that I have bought, trying to find one that worked or didn't fall apart. I thought that I had found a good one as the flash drives and cables fit snugly into the ports, and my computer had no trouble recognizing what ever was plugged into the hub. It worked this way for about 3 months I guess, then one day, after having taken my notebook on a weekend away, upon returning home and hooking the notebook up and plugging the cable for the hub into the computer, The  error message flashed up onto the screen saying "The last device that you plugged into this computer failed, and the computer could not recognize it". I tried several things to get the hub to work, but nothing worked. I unplugged all items from the hub, and still upon plugging it into the computer again, I received the same message. This without a doubt told me that the problem was the hub and not something plugged into it.
