Pre-Intro: Seidio Inc was founded in 2002 in Houston Texas. They first started as a company that designed unique and quality products for PDAs, which slowly evolved into Smartphones. They has a passion for innovation and this lead them to become the leading manufacturer of Smartphone accessories.They are also very well known for their rubberized cases. Many online forums and review sites have reviewed and vouch for their products. Their products are known to be built for quality and endurance.Currently they are carrying a variety of cases for the Sprint HTC Evo 4G, Ultimate Screen Guard Protectors, Spare Batteries, Chargers, Cables, and many more. You can visit their site here for all the details and see a list of their products (Seidio - Your Single Source for Premium Smartphone Accessories).Specs of Innocase II for EVO:Quote:Innocases have quickly become one of the most popular products Seidio manufactures. Crafted from a hard yet flexible material, it provides an amazingly thin layer of protection without adding the excessive bulk of other cases. The Innocase II consists of interlocking top and bottom pieces that fit your phone snugly, and Seidio's signature soft touch finish provides a great feel and better grip without attracting lint. It is also available in a combo, which includes a matching holster.- 1mm thin case provides scratch protection without adding much bulk- Designed from a hard, yet flexible material with Seidio's signature soft touch coating- Unique slide-in design consists of top and bottom interlocking pieces that fit your device snugly- Precision cutouts for ports, controls, and kickstandFirst Impression:HOLY!!!, I GOT IT, I GOT IT!!!!! I GOT IT, I GOT IT!!!!!The little brown square/cubed box arrived at my door early this morning. I was like "wow", quality service from Seidio. It was very nice of them to send me a review unit early before shipping date. I must take my time to thank them once more.As I took the products outside of the packaging, I saw the plastic looking case for the Innocase II. The product showed very well through the plastic container. It was definitely screaming at me to put it one. But before I did that, I took my dSLR camera out and started to snap some shots with my camera.One thing that I thought I would do better is move Seidio's Logo a little high above the inside background. You can't really see the Seidio logo from the packaging.Build Quality:Definitely not cheap. The build quality of the thing was hard. I wouldn't say hard as a rock, but it's definitely hard plastic. It was harder than those cheap eBay cases that you can get for 10 bucks shipped to your door. The rubberized feel of the case was also very good on your hands. It felt smooth and prevented the phone from slipping out of your hand.There was no sharp edges around any corner. or on the inside of the case. The inside of the case was very well thought out. They put some felt cloth in the backing so that it will prevent the back cover of the Evo from being scratched up.I also tried to double check on their advertised specifications by using a mm gauge to measure the overall thickeness of the phone before and after installing the case. You can find those pictures below.Fitment:I slipped the case and the Evo and it clipped together nicely and firmly. The right side and the left side of the case fit snuggly together and it fits the Evo flush on both sides.The cutout for the LED, Camera, and Speaker is perfect. There's enough space for the all three hardware specs. The camera glass is now protected because the case brings up the level to a point where the camera is indented inside the cutout location. No more worrying of scratching the camera lens when you put it on the table.I've also took specific pictures of each cutout location around the case to show you how they go around the buttons, insert holes for the charger, HDMI, power button and volume button. All those edges fit without gap in between and have been well covered with the rubberized layer to protect the phone.Forgot to mention, it covers all screen leaks from the edges.Protection:With the pictures showing 1000 words. I don't need to say much abut protection. They thought out every aspect of the phone do the point where there's felt inserts on the backing to protect the back cover. I wish they did one thing with the inserts though. They should have put the felt cloth all the way wrapping all aspects of the inside including the curved edges of the phone.Conclusion:In conclusion, the case lives up to the standard of innovation. They really did make a case that protected the phone real well. The design is slick and they did not have to include a bunch of artwork to make it attractive. It also keeps the phone low profile and gives the Evo a high class look while your on the phone or when the phone is sitting on your table.People always complain that getting a case at a high price isn't worth it. Or they complain that they want their phone naked. I think the reason they say it is because they can't get themselves to click the buy button. Some people settle down with cheap cases from ebay. But if you want a cheap case, that's your decision. If you want a high quality case built with precision and design to innovate, this is definitely the case to buy. Buying a cheap case from ebay would make your $450 phone look cheap also.They offer many different colors, so go to their site to check it out!The only thing I wished I had the honor of reviewing was the accessory that came with the case, "The holster". I didn't get a chance to get the holster so I couldn't test if the holster made the case even better. I'm sure the holster would give the case additional points!

I absolutely love this battery. It is pretty, simple, to the point, just the right little things to make it useful, it charges an iPhone 4s to 100%, and it is inexpensive. At the end of the day, eleven bucks and it will double the capacity for my iPhone? Yeah, what's not to love about this?OK, yeah inexpensive is one thing, charging my phone is another - if this was ugly, or big that would kill it. But no, this thing is pretty. The case is deep metallic royal blue with a silver end cap. It is about the size of two wide lipstick cases end to end. And it feels great in my hand; the rounded corners are so soft.Sendy added a couple of good things to this particular battery. There are four blue LED's that indicate percent charged - handy to be certain the battery is charged up before taking it. The battery charges via microUSB. There is a power button on the top. Press the button and the LEDs light up telling you how much charge remains.And then the thing actually charges devices. This I couldn't believe, many much more expensive batteries don't do this - charge an iPad or iPhone at the highest, fastest charge rate. Now remember, this is only a 2600mAh battery, so it will do a full single charge of an iPhone 4s and a little more. I charged my iPad with this, from a fully charged battery; I got a boost of 20% in just over an hour. Now that is fast and a great boost. I honestly never expected this battery to work with an iPad. The battery got a little warm, not more than body temperature during the heaviest charging - that is totally expected. The battery pack is charged via microUSB.Charging is excellent with this. I was able to charge my iPhone 4s at the fastest rate from 25% left on my phone to a full 100% with three quarters of a full charge on the battery. I used up the last of the charge on my iPad Air, and got about a 10% boost with the remaining battery power (also charged at the high charge rate). Conclusion - this will easily fully charge an iPhone 4s from completely dead. Now how great is that? Seriously, eleven bucks to have a full backup battery of your phone.Included in the box is a stretch mesh pull string bag - that is really nice. They went the path of an odd interchangeable charging cable, I'm not sure I love these. There is a main USB cable - the one with the rectangular connector you plug into a computer or AC adapter. On the other end is a jack. There are two tips you can plug into that jack - a microUSB connector and a lightning connector (the type used in the most recent Apple devices). These are not designed to carry data, only charge the battery or your device. I like that Sendy tried, I think these are a little fiddlier than I like. I'll just use my Apple cable and one of the 5,000 microUSB cables I have laying around.The battery will work with any USB cable and can be charged with any microUSB cable - you don't have to use this funky universal cable included in the box.There is no AC adapter included. You will need to charge the battery via a computer USB port or an AC adapter like the kind used with iPhones. The battery takes some time to charge. Normal for these things is to just plug them in overnight.What the heck is this battery? An amazing deal if you need a small capacity battery that will give you an additional full charge on an iPhone 4s.I was provided a review sample.

First I would mention that I am a filtration designer for another company. Being familiar with filtration I am able to evaluate the claims and performance of a consumer air filtration device. I have bought and thrown out many air filtration devices due to inefficiency.  Let me simply outline why this air filter system is the real thing and not a gimmick.1.The multi-stage filter system is logical and based on proven science. The first layer of filtration is a washable coarse screen. This catches the largest dust particles. It is the protector of the replaceable paper filter behind it. The next layer is a paper filter covered with a spun synthetic fabric. This filter is non-washable. The spun layer captures the larger dust, allowing finer particles to pass through and be captured by the paper layer. You can gently vacuum the spun material if it gets dusty. The final stage is the activated carbon filter. This filter captures chemicals and odors that are in the air.2. The ionization unit works by sending out charged particles into the air. The charges attract dust and cause them to settle. In theory they get sucked into the filter and removed from the air.3. The filter uses are particle sensor to monitor the dust level in the air. The fan in the filter pulls air into the sensor for analysis. If dust is detected the sensor turns the filter on high(er) until the dust level drops.4. The "odor" sensor detects odors in the air. Honestly, it will detect bad breath and a fart. I am 100% serious and have tested it. When an odor is sensed, the fan ramps up until the odor level drops. The activated carbon filter is responsible for odor removal.5. When the paper filter gets clogged, the air flow rate slows down. The unit detects this and send a visual warning to change the filter. You can vacuum the paper filter and get up to a year out of it if you like. I replace mine once every 15 months.The unit is quiet and needs very little hand-holding. Just set the device to the level of filter activity you want and walk away. It is really very simple. Read the brief instructions and you will easily grasp what to do. This filter is the best I have ever used. I am completely satisfied.

To be honest with you, I have scoffed at speakers of this sort in the past due to their size and their limit to play audio at high volumes without a lot of distortion. I did not think speakers this small could provide decent or adequate sound quality at higher volumes for larger rooms/offices or outdoors.There seems to be a lot of these types of portable pop-up Bluetooth speakers on the market lately, made of various materials and having various features, battery life and price points. The most intriguing feature of the RoverBeats T3 by Etekcity was its 7 hour battery life.After all the preliminaries were done, it was time to see how the RoverBeats T3 by Etekcity sounded. To my surprise, it sounded pretty good. As I stated before, I was a very skeptical about what kind of sound this speaker could produce. I have had many sets of portable speakers and most have never live up to their hype, but the RoverBeats T3 by Etekcity provided an all-around solid, crisp sound. The bass is more than adequate for how small this thing is, the mids and highs are clear and sharp, and the volume range is very good and better than a few other smaller Bluetooth speakers I have tested. I listened to a variety of music through the RoverBeats T3 by Etekcity – Rap, Hip-Hop, R&B;, Country, Classical, Alternative and even some heavy metal – and the speaker sound was surprisingly better than expected with less distortion than I anticipated at higher volumes.SOUND: 5/5----------*Highs 5/5*Mids 5/5*Lows 5/5*Overall 5/5I am giving this a 5-star rating in sound because it was simply the best performing smaller Bluetooth speaker I have tested, and I have tested a dozen or small smaller/mini Bluetooth speakers. It was able to perform better at higher volumes than the BassBoomz and the OXA Mini speakers I tested. I was also surprised how much bass this thing has for being so small as well.BATTERY: 5/5------------I was able to achieve a little over 7 hours of battery life during three consecutive trial periods, so the touted feature of 7 hour battery life holds true. I'm sure over time this will decrease, but that is expected with any battery powered device.BUILD:------5/5Very sturdy, dense, compact and visually appealing design. What a lot of people do not understand is that a lot of small Bluetooth speakers focus on design over audio quality. To me, it's ALL about the audio quality over ANYTHING else but not to be confused, the design on this is visually appealing, simple, functional and very well built.CONCLUSION:-----------All in all a very good mini/small Bluetooth speaker and I can with easily recommend this, especially at the $20-$30 price range. The one gripe I do have is that it only has PLAY/PAUSE and VOLUME buttons on the physical speaker itself, a lot of others offer PREVIOUS/NEXT song buttons as well. I would like to see the second revision or generation of this speaker to have those buttons added.-------------DISCLOSURE-------------I was supplied a sample for test and evaluation, and I promised that my review is fair and honest with no bias.

I didn't get the rooCase protectors, but some CHEAP subsitution. PC Micro Store is **SLOW** to respond to my order (2 out of 2 times). My order took 4 days before it was shipped or acknowledged.Hard to believe I received the same product the other reviewers did. The lower right corner has a square notch cut out of it. ? The other 3 corners have a round radius but the curve is steeper than the inside radius of a Kindle Fire so they don't fit inside properly. The film is cut about 1/100th of an inch to narrow and about 1/100th of an inch too long. Thus one end (plus 3 corners) can never lay down properly. It has a VERY shiny surface that  reflects WORSE than the screen without a protector.This product is the epitome of CHEAP CHINESE junk. Don't go there. :-/Update:Well, I *will* say this. PC Micro Store is a VERY conscientious seller. I didn't complain directly to them. But they take the initiative to review product reviews on items that they provide. They read my unhappy review and they contacted ME. Apologized and explained that they had been out of the Roo/Protectors and a substitute was sent. I suspect it was a new item for them and they weren't yet aware of any quality problems (many Amazon items are drop shipped from a third location and a seller may not have the opportunity to "audition" each item prior to the first few sales). They also promised to inform me when the actual item was back in stock, and if I wished I could request a replacement at that time.So a week or so later I got an e-mail informing they were back in stock and provided an e-mail address and phone number I could use to request my replacement. Well, work got crazy and then I got a cold and I forgot to reply. Never the less, after another week and a half, a pair of shiny new Roo/Protectors arrived in the mail! These look much better, I still don't know if I will be able to get these applied to my satisfaction, but I'm giving them 4 STARS just on principal.PC Micro Store *ROCKS*.:-D

The Mohu Jolt is a USB-powered HDTV amplifier.  Why USB?  Well why not, but probably because many televisions now have USB ports on the back and those can be used to get the 5 Volts power supply this amplifier uses rather than having to run a cord to a wall outlet or power strip.  Pretty clever.  The Jolt also includes a normal AC wall plug with a USB port for those of us who don't have USB ports on our television.  If your television does have a USB port then you now have a spare AC to USB converter.The Mohu Jolt is packaged well, contains a small but clear instruction pamphlet, and the overall impression I get of the product is good.  When plugged into USB there is a very small blue light that lets you know it's getting power.However, although I wanted this product to work but I didn't get good results.  My setup is as follows.  I live between two cities, about 40 miles from both.  One city is SW, the other ESE.  I have a Tivo Premiere fed by two antennas.  The two HDTV antennas are a Winegard FL-5000 unamplified high on my wall and facing basically SouthWest, and a Winegard SS-3000 (a very good antenna) amplified antenna facing East.  I combine these two antennas with a Channel Plus 2512 splitter/combiner.  Two antennas allowed me to receive stations from both cities with mostly good results, but windy days would still cause signal degradation.  The combiner has 2 inputs and one output, the antennas plug into the inputs and the output goes to my Tivo.I tested 5 channels, without the Jolt, with the Jolt between the Channel Plus antenna combiner and the Tivo, and with the Jolt between the unamplified Winegard FL-5000 and the combiner.  Here are the numbers:Ch. - No Jolt - After combiner - Between Combiner and FL-5000 antenna6  - 55 - 43 - 55 - 528  - 23 - ** - ** - **23 - 54 - 56 - 43 - 4929 - 61 - 56 - 65 - 5435 - 27 - ** - ** - 24** = no signal.The numbers show that the signal got worse in some cases with the Jolt, a little better in a couple cases, and totally disappeared in a couple cases.Thinking conditions may have changed as I was testing I went back to my original configuration and got the same numbers that I had before.Perhaps by moving one or both antennas around I could make the Jolt more effective, but it took me quite a while to get things working as good as they are.  As it is I wouldn't say the Jolt will help in every situation, certainly not in mine.

I got this to replace my Cisco E3000 after I moved to a larger house and found signal strength to be 2 bars in my office with low throughput (7Mbps as opposed to ~35 when next to router). This newer access point did not improve signal strength or throughput, it was same as my older Cisco for the same distance. The connection also kept dropping every few minutes, which is not apparent when browsing, but if you use VPN to connect to your work then you will find out very quickly. I used the same channel number as my older router which never had problems, so I know its not interference from other devices. The final straw was when I accidentally nudged it off a two foot high table, the antenna connector on the device broke. I went back to my older Cisco.This device is probably good if you don't have too many walls that the signal has to go through. One thing I did not research before buying - this has to be plugged into an existing network, it will not work if you plug directly into your cable modem (unless your modem has an inbuilt dhcp, the one I have from Comcast does not).

I've had this ice-cream maker for nearly two years now and love it.  My only complaint is I wish it were a 2-quart capacity; the ice cream is so good but goes so fast!  The recipes in the accompanying booklet produce just the right texture in the finished product, and after a few tries you can easily modify them to make any flavor you desire.It's ideal for whipping up a premium dessert in a short amount of time when you have last-minute guests; just keep plenty of whole milk and heavy cream in the back of the fridge at all times!  Everyone who has tried my mocha-toffee-hazelnut homemade ice cream from this machine has loved it, and quite a few have proclaimed it the best ice cream they have ever eaten.  And you just can't beat how quick and easy it is to make.One tip -- make sure you have a 2-quart plastic container on hand to store the ice cream; you cannot store it in the mixing bowl because it is easily damaged.  In general, right after you take the ice cream out of the mixer, it can be served right away if you have kept your ingredients very cold, but its texture will benefit from spending at least an hour in the coldest part of your freezer.Some people have complained about the noise; it's loud enough to bother a cat but not so loud you can't talk over it.  Definitely not a reason to avoid purchase.

Non-Stick?  Not quite...unless by `non-stick' they mean `not as sticky as cast iron'...Ok, first a disclosure -  I'm a little peeved at the company anyway, because they didn't respond to an e-mail question regarding why some NS pans are dishwasher safe and some are not...I have the  Calphalon 8" omelet pan (commercial grade - 3-rivet handle) - I paid full retail, around $85.  This pan is incredibly sturdy, the heft and balance are fantastic - you could drop an attacker with it and go right back to the range.  But it's NOT a non-stick pan.  It's the stickiest `non-stick' I've ever used.  I realize that commercial grade non-stick pans are more durable and generally less slippery, but this is ridiculous.  I want to be able to cook my eggs w/o oil...not possible with this pan.It also seems to be getting worse with use.  I'm getting ready to go back to my Chantal non-stick.  The Chantal handle is crap but the `pouring' edge and super-slick non-stick is superior to the Calphalon and about  the price too.Bottom line - you'll be happy using the pan to pan-fry and sauté, but not as an omelet pan.Jay

I'm moving overseas in a few months and have been busy unloading all sorts of things, including 2 laptops, 1 netbook, 1 i pad device, desktop computers 3 and monitors - you get the picture. So I wasn't even looking to buy another laptop, but the more I thought about it I figured I'd peek at what's new in laptops. I had just gifted a like new green Dell 15" laptop to my sister-in-law last summer. It looked nice, but nothing special and the battery just didn't last that long - about 2.5 hours at best. After all, isn't that the whole purpose of having a portable computer?This thing has at least twice the battery life, plus it runs circles around the other's I had. Boots up (with Windows 7 Professional - no less, in 20 seconds. Perfect connectivity to my Cisco N wireless router. At first I didn't know if I'd really appreciate the screen, as it is a bit on the dull side for my taste. But this is the price one pays to have longer battery life. These LED screens use less power and since screens tend to use up much of the batter, it only makes sense to have such in a laptop. I've only had three days, and already I'm fine with it. Matter of fact I am running on a 10 brightness on a scale of 1-15. Below that is too dull for me.Love all the connections it has, including HDMI to power my HD TV. Perfect on playing YouTube vids without hiccups. Speakers are just ok - but that's laptops for you. The optical drive is a gem and will run just about any form of media. Also has slots for various cards. I just realized put this review under the wrong model. Oh well!  I got the E520 with i3 chip, 4 Gig memory and 320 Gig HD. The hard drive is a bit slim in todays world, but I have a 1.5 Terabyte external I will hook up if needed. Forces to me to keep an eye on bloating the drive. It is a very fast drive, excellent response.Heat - there are two fan vents on either side below the hinge and I've noticed this computer produces very minimal heat. Much better than most laptops which seem to second as heaters. I can hold my hand next to the heat vents and hardly feel any heat at all under normal usage. I have not stressed the system yet so I cannot comment on heat when the CPU is maxed out.I owned a Thinkpad back in the early 90's when the name was still IBM. That laptop was also far ahead of it's time and always made me feel good using it. There is something about Lenovos and how they are made. It seems they are better engineered than most laptops. I also have a Kindle and a Sony eReader and it's like the difference with how the Sony feels so much better in the hand than the Kindle does. Just better build quality.This has been an excellent purchase and I am happy to have such a well made laptop for such a reasonable price point.

I was skeptical about Bluetooth headphones, and have stayed away from even trying them mostly because of the price. But the Motorola S305's cost half what other Bluetooth headphones have in the past so I decided to give them a try. Mostly I've been very impressed with these, though I do have a few problems.First, the sound quality is very good - on par with a decent set of wired headphones of similar price. I haven't found there to be any static, though occasionally (and inexplicably) the sound will cut out for a half second, which can be annoying. It doesn't happen that often though. The range on these is remarkably good - I bought these specifically for jogging, so the range would only ever have to be about 2 feet, but I've also used them at my desk and I really appreciate the freedom to get up and walk around up to 20 feet away with several walls between my phone and me. When the headphones do get out of range, they just disconnect, and then reconnect when they're in range again. The volume is also very good, and although they do not cover the ears completely, the volume can be turned up to the point where it covers most outside noise. If I were choosing headphones just for work or travel, I might choose something with greater noise isolation, but since I chose these for general use and especially exercise (where size and weight are the main consideration) these work nicely. The headphones are quite comfortable, more so than other behind-the-head earphones I've tried. Wearing them for a couple hours at a time did not cause any numbness or pain. They are also pretty secure, and jogging with them hasn't been a problem. I appreciate the fact that these charge with a micro-USB cable, which means that I can charge with my PC and a standard cable when I'm on the road. Sadly, the included charge is a hard-wired micro-usb type, unlike the ones that take a powerplug and provide standard USB-out, so if you don't want to bring the power charger with you you'll have to buy a micro-USB cable.My biggest problem with the headphones so far is the buttons. There are a total of 7 buttons; one for power (with indicator LEDs) and volume up and down all located on the rim of the headphones, and then a D-pad layout for call, play/pause, >> and << on the outside of the right earphone. The buttons located on the rim are loose and feel cheap. Worst of all, they rattle when jogging. That clickety-clack is quite loud when it is right next to my ear, and it is distracting. The other buttons all work fine, though as other reviewers have noted the >> and << buttons don't work with the iPhone/iPod Touch because of Apple's software. I've also found that I have to be a little careful in handling the headphones because it is easy to accidentally press the "call" button, which automatically triggers a call to the last person you called, and that can be somewhat embarrassing.My final annoyance is with the auto-power-off feature, which does not seem to actually work. The manual states that if the headphones are unused for 5 minutes, they will power down, but they obviously just go in to "sleep" mode and will automatically wake if there's an incoming call. This can be frustrating since the headphones might be 20 feet away in another room but when someone calls, the headphones will take over the call and I've found myself on many occasions talking into my phone while the person on the other end is talking through the headphones. This is, I'm sure, an issue that my phone is just as likely to be the cause of, but it's worth noting because it does happen a lot. Just make sure to turn the headphones off when not in use and it won't be a problem.Reading that last bit of my review one might get the idea that I'm not happy with these headphones, but that's not true; I think they're good for the price, and I would certainly recommend them to anyone who is looking for an inexpensive pair of wireless headphones for office or exercise. But those looking for the best at any price are likely to find more expensive headphones better.

I have this connected to the analog audio-out jacks on our bedroom TV so as to not disturb the wife at night and it works great.  It is always powered and is attached to the back of the TV.  I use it in combination with aPlantronics M50 - Bluetooth Headset - Retail Packaging - Blackthat I also use with my cellphone - paired perfectly.  Volume via this setup is excellent and the audio lag is virtually imperceptible - not a problem for me at all.  It is likely that lag is more a factor of your headset than this device - an aging Bluetooth headset is likely to have more lag than something newer. Sound quality is very good, and drop-outs are very rare - only when the battery in my headset is running low. A minor niggle is that it puts out a fairly bright red glow that might be annoying to some in a very dark room.  But that is very easy to cover up - we just have the light facing the TV rather than away from it. To sum up:- This is a transmitter, not a receiver - so you use it to transmit audio from any non-Bluetooth analog source to a Bluetooth headset. So it plugs into your car|home stereo|TV etc and you need a Bluetooth headset/receiver on the other end.- Although it has a battery, it works fine when plugged into a power source.  I have not used it without a power source to evaluate battery life.- Paired easily and stays paired.  When I turn on the headset, it connects automatically.- Very decent sound quality, although I have not evaluated it with a high quality sound source.- Very little audio lag - not enough to be an irritant and most people would not notice it at all if they weren't carefully listening for it.- It has a 3.5mm stereo male plug input that is designed for a mini headphone jack - I just used an adapter (2 x RCA Male, 1 x 3.5mm Stereo Female, Y-Cable 6-Inch) to connect it to the 2 RCA jacks on the back of the TV.

Of the two toast ovens in service, one is larger than standard toaster oven, and another one is smaller than standard.  I was not able to find a silicone baking mat to fit, so I purchase these Cookie Sheet Liner. I read reviews about how thin they are, but still was a bit surprised because these are truly paper thin.However, once I cut them to size, laid them on the baking pans, and started using them, I like them immediately. When brand new, these bake with no odor unlike the silicone mats. These are also easier to clean than silicone mat I use for the large oven. I use these on the baking pans, with metal wire rack standing on them to reheat fried, rotisserie, BBQ meat. Or to bake hash browns, chicken steaks, pizza, etc. This way grease will drip down on the pan instead of into my stomach. I use the toaster oven all day long, and used to waste foil a few times a day. Since using these liner, I have not wasted one sheet of foil. Just wash gentle with warm soapy water and everything including burnt BBQ sauce and cheese come off in a breeze. I wash these at the same time I wash the wire rack, and hang the liner on the wire rack to dry together on my drying rack overnight. Next morning, they are as good as new. Great liners!

I have been juicing for years, always in search for the ultimate juicer. Anyone who juices a lot looks for one that is easy to clean. It also has a problem with hard foods like carrots and beats and back up into the feeder tube. Soft fruits were poor as well and I was not impressed when it comes to wheatgrass.  While the slow speed motor has lots of power,  NOT for the plastic and stainless insert basket.  Within 2 months the plastic starts to form hairline fractures. This diminishes the effectiveness of the unit. With 4 months things start to get worse and in 6 months it becomes dangerous and it might explode on you as it did me. The quality of the juice is sometimes good however it clogs up easily.  I was hoping not to have two juicers one for greens and one for carrots and alike.  However I with this one you need a blender, Breville (good juicer almost industrial grade) and the Hurom to do all my juicing. Not a deal from my point of view.  Replacement parts are frustrating because you will need one part within a week if you are not careful clean it. I have documented the complaints above extensively sending photo's and everything. While the Operations Manager 310.515.7170 in Torrance CA. is a very nice guy and has sent me several replacement baskets and stoppers. His corporate bosses are third world thinkers or should I say stinkers. DO NOT BUY THIS UNIT you will need another within the year not worth $350 I paid for it.

All those functions (particularly gps/navigation) drain the battery very fast and this product is just the right solution.- The lightning wire fits in my phone without needing to take the case off (some non-Apple wires have thicker plastic which prevents use with some cases)- The cigarette lighter charger is very small and slick- there's an extra USB slot that allows my fiancée to charge her iPhone 4s without interrupting the charging of my phone- the lightning wire can be removed which allows it to be used with a standard apple wall plug or with my laptop- charges my phone fast!Minus one star because it's expensive.... I have a Galaxy Nexus which is a total power hog.  This battery charger more than handles its power needs. It's a little big to carry around in your pocket, but well worth it if you're going to be burning through your phone's battery.

First - What I was looking forMy wife has been working on a series of juvenile fiction books over her summer breaks.  Much of that time has been spent at the municipal pool for kids' swim lessons or open swims.  So we were looking for a waterproof, functional cover for her iPad (I know, who writes books on an iPad, but she is an adept hunt and peck typer and it works well for her.)What we gotAs for protection and performance, we got exactly what we were looking for.  It is not waterproof, but it is secure enough for the potential poolside splashing and dripping.  The double zip-locks form strong secure seals.  The screen works well for her typing and allowed her to have all the functionality she was looking for.  At least for the poolside purposes.What wasn't quite up to our hopes.It is not exactly easy to slip the iPad in and out of this case.  Personally I think it borders on being enough hassle that it may make her decide not to use it in the long run.  Also the iPad needs to be removed for charging or access to the headphone port etc. Obviously this is needed for water-proofing, but this does make it less likely to be used.Also we had a small issue when we left the cover in the car at below freezing temperatures then brought it in. I did not let it warm before trying to open the seals and one of the seal rails cracked.  It still makes a good seal, but should not be opened or closed at freezing temperatures.SummaryWhile not perfect, it should be perfect for our needs.  Construction is good (see note on sub zero temperatures)and functionality is good. All in all happy with it.

Have to say upgrading from a stock cooler that came with my i5 2500k to this, made all the difference in the world. The cooler is just epic. 24 degrees celsius under normal conditions at 44 degrees during load (gaming), runs circles around my previous (stock) cooler 33 during normal conditions and 64 during load.The installation was super easy and the box came with everything that was required. Just follow the guide included, you can't go wrong.I'm extremely pleased with this and would recommend this to anyone that wants the best air cooler money can buy. Just make sure your motherboard is compatible as is your case. Also certain ram modules esp. those with larger heatsinks may have problems. The compatibility list is mentioned at the noctua website.I highly recommend this to anyone that wants to OC their processor and get the best possible air cooling solution.

This toaster is five star to me because you can't beat the price and the performance. I am not comparing it to the expensive convection ovens. For an very inexpensive toaster oven you just can't beat the features, the price and the performance. It toasts evenly. It bakes evenly. I tried to bake some Pillsbury Crescent rolls and it did a better job than my regular oven. It has a wonderful timer feature that you can set up to 20 minutes. You can also opt to leave it on in the stay on mode. There are toaster settings. Best to watch the item you are toasting the first few times and know the time for your specific bread. It holds three large bakery style loaf bread slices. It does so much more quickly than the last Black and Decker model I had. The pull our crumb tray feature is a real time saver. Whether I need to cook fish, meat, or heat a hot sandwich, toast, or reheat...I find it incredibly wonderful for the price. THere are small saftey protusions in the back of unit so you keep it the proper distance from any touching surface. What more can I say? If you want a toaster over that is not reinventing the wheel then this is the model!! How can you beat $26.99?! Besides all this it does look nice. It is compact at just 9" deep x 16" long x 8.5" tall. Amazon had the wrong size listed on their site. I bought it from SEARS because they had a much better price.UPDATE:Feb 2012  I have had this toaster oven for over two years and I still love it. There are no problems. It is wearing well.UPDATE: APril 2013: I have this toaster oven for over three years and there have been no problems. Still works great!

I've had this vacuum for a year now, and use it almost daily for the Beagle hair on my red linen couch. It gets the job done like no cordless ever could. The rotating brush is very powerful -- left me with bruises when I tried to get hair of my jeans! I keep it in a little basket beside the couch and pull it out nearly ever day. The long cord lets me zip over the couch, suede side stairs, and all the random throw pillows on in time flat.I do replace the filter religiously (available by Amazon Prime) and only had to refit the belt once when it slipped probably because the cup was overloaded. Which brings me to my only complaint. The dirt cup is small, and really icky to empty. I guess regular dust would fall right out, but we're dealing with dog hair so it just mats up and I have to reach in to pull the mess out. I know-- it's the same thing I'm touching when I pet Abby, but it's gross now that its coming out of a dirt cup. To keep the suction, I probably empty it every third or fourth use.A few reviewers have mentioned overheating but it seems they're using it as a household vacuum. This is for quick pickups of furniture and small areas of rugs. It gets warm, but I've never had it overheat in a year of use.The thing is, it's too good to justify replacing so I'll just deal with the nasty dirt cup until something better is so compelling that I might donate this one.

Like I said, so far, so good and looking forward with optimism.Update July 23So much for optimism.  I plugged the earbuds into the jack on the A380 and after 30 seconds, I heard a "POP".  They don't work anymore.  So now I am in Germany for a week without my noise cancelling earbuds.  I will purchase a basic pair for the rest of the trip (everything is more expensive here.  I am not happy at all.  So much for Audio-Technica.

I spent several months researching the current TV's, plasma, LCD, rear projection (LCOS, DLP, LCD), and even a front projector. And of course, checking out each brand from each type of TV, both online, and in stores. Basically, just trying to find the best price vs. performance and something that would suite my needs.I needed a BIG screen, over 50", so this ruled out LCD and Plasma since they are WAY too expensive above that size. Then I also needed something looked great for playing HD games (Xbox), watching HD movies (HD-DVD), and HD TV (Cable HD DVR). Which really means an awesome 1080p TV. After looking around at various stores, it became clear the Samsung and Sony models were going to be the best bet for rear projection under $3k.Ultimately, the Samsung did win hands down. Thanks to low price on amazon, and a picture that was just as good, if not better than the Sony in some cases! It has an excellent 10,000:1 contrast ratio for very bright white's and perfect black levels. There is also no ghosting at all, and the rainbow effect (which I have seen on previous DLP's) is virtually non-existent. Which makes this set perfect for gaming. Also no audio sync issues as with some of the older Samsung models.In the past month of owning this TV, I've only seen the rainbox effect twice, and it was in a B&W; movie. None of the serveral other people that have watched this TV have noticed it at all. They just comment on how awesome everything in HD looks on the TV - it's quite perfect really. Out of the box, the picture is almost perfect. I just had to slightly lower the contrast, and it's good to go. And thanks to easy to navigate menu's, this is very easily done. While they are a bit sluggish (takes 1-2 seconds to appear), they have plenty of options.When it comes to Inputs, it does appear to be lacking on the HDMI side. While 2 inputs is standard for most models, I could really use at least 3 or 4. 2 is OK for your DVD Player and DVR, but with computers and game console also starting to use HDMI, you can quickly run out of ports. Luckily, a cheap HDMI switch can solve that issue.There is also a PC (VGA) input on the TV that is *suppose* to accept a 1920x1080 resolution at 60hz, per the users guide. Unfortunately I haven't been able to get it to work with a computer at that resolution yet (tried 3). It'll take 720p, all the way up to 1920x1200, but since that's not a native resolution, there is some cropping. This really is a minor issue , since that high of a resolution from a PC via VGA port won't be all that clear anyways. You'd really need HDMI to truly appreciate 1080p. And there are now true HDCP compliant HDMI video cards out now. The xbox will output to this TV at 1920x1080 via the VGA port, but there is a 1-3" border, and the screen is tilted.Figures though, even though its got the VGA port, it's still a TV, not a PC Monitor. :) The TV won't send information to the source indicating what the correct resolution, refresh rate, and other settings it needs - as a PC monitor would. Future xbox updates may help address some output issues as well. For now, I'm waiting for MS to release HDMI output.And to wrap up this long review, here is a list of Pro's and Con's.Pros:* High Contrast Ratio* Fast color wheel to eliminate rainbows* No Ghosting at all* Perfect for gaming (no audio/video sync issues)* Good viewing angles (for rear projection)* Nice variety of inputs* Only about 17" Deep for the 61" model* Weighs only 80lbs!!* Easy setup out of the box* Relatively inexpensive* With it's thin black border and silver control panel, it physically looks GREAT.Cons:* VGA input has some limitations* Needs more HDMI inputs* Plastic bezel feels somewhat cheapAnd that's about it for this TV. Any other "cons" such as limited viewing angles and wobulation (google it) are going to affect ALL DLP's at this time; so I won't use that to effect my review. Otherwise, both myself, and many friends who come by to watch movies and play games, are very impressed with this TV. It's quickly converting them from their favorite brand over to Samsung. So this TV is highly recommended if you need a big HD 1080p TV for around $2k.

I bought this for a friend who had a stroke and lost the use of her right hand. We decided to try different models of can openers, but there aren't many choices out there for people with limited function in their hands. This one worked very well, but it uses AA batteries. It can get expensive if you use the can opener a lot, but it works with either disposable or rechargeable batteries. It opens cans of all sizes. It has an automatic cycle that shuts off when it's finished. It is easy to use, easy to clean and easy to store. It's not too noisy and it's kind of fun watching it work. If the can doesn't completely open, just push the button and let it run through the cycle again.

I'm hard on keyboards.  I'm a writer and tend to turn out novels at a steady rate. This keyboard is about the easiest to use there is; the key pressure is just right and the "feel"is less tiring than  the usual straight-line keyboard.  In my time as a writer, I've had THREE of these keyboards and therein lies the rub- the last MS 4000 KB had some sort of cheap lettering on it and after barely 1200 pages of draft, the letters started disappearing until a quarter of the keyboard was blank--the letters were gone.  Bad ink?  Poor printing?  I don't know.  I bought an different keyboard and after barely 2000 pages the keys started sticking.  The worst one was the E.  (Groan, new keyboard time)  Then I saw this. It is reconditioned to as-new condition.  It is easy to use.  If I can fix the other keyboard with the sticky keys, I may use it as a spare.  One thing I do know is that the Microsoft Natural Ergonomic Keyboard 4000 is about the most comfortable keyboard out there, and this vendor provides an Excellent product.I tried the others.  For 3$300.00 I could buy a really fancy keyboard (like the one I learned on) or for 10% of that price I can buy  one of these by this Vendor.  A writer, I am.  Stupid, I'm not.(Hint: Science Fiction>Adventure>Kindle library: "New World" is my latest release.  You'll see why I wear out keyboards.)

My HTC doesn't get constant use, but with that large screen the original battery is underpowered for my days off when I leave the phone on all day.  This extended battery has solved that problem.  Though it adds a noticeable bulge and heft to the phone, neither is an issue for me; and actually makes the phone easier to hold - less likely to slip from my hand as the original slim profile could.My only gripe is that one side of the extended cover doesn't snap into place as snugly as the other side, leaving it feeling like it might pop off; so I may return it for a replacement.  (Update: I should have returned this but it's too late now.  The poor-fitting battery cover seems to be loosening up over time and requires that I constantly check it.)Battery life seems excellent.  On one rare occasion when I had the phone on all day at work, the battery appeared down by about half at the end of the day.  Though I didn't use it much that day, I'm guessing that the battery drain was due to constant signal searching in a poor indoor reception area.  Otherwise, no 4G reception issues with the battery cover antenna that some have reported.Yes, it's worth getting, but hope your battery cover fits better than mine.  (Update: I no longer think this is worth buying from this seller.  I originally contacted them about the poor fitting cover and they wanted me to send them a photograph of the defect.  Never got around to that, but if they were more customer oriented, they should have immediately sent me a replacement and return label for the defective cover.)

I received this about 5 days ago and immediately charged it and started using it. The last couple of blue tooth units I have had found it hard to make it through the day. I am on my 4th day and still my initial charge. Granted, these have been a little lighter use than normal due to illness but I just shut if off and and restarted it to check the battery life and it reconnected immediately and said battery level is high still. I now am going to nor charge and see how far it will go. Sound volume is good. It really a little too loud for me. I have it turned as low as it will go and it is still too loud in quiet situations. When asking around, the callers on the other end say sound is loud and clear but a little muffled or canned but still good. With all that and the fact this sits on the outside of my ear, it is good enough to keep and recommend. The only reason it is not 5 star so far is the inability to get the volume low enough and the slightly muffed sound on the other end.Update 3.20.13 It has been about a week of light use for this headset. I was sick a couple of days and did not go to work and therefore did not turn it on. When I wear it, I do not turn it off between calls. A couple of the days were not so light use and I am still on the same initial charge. When I just closed and open the boom to restart it to hear the battery condition, it still says "battery high". I am going to not charge it just to see how far it will go.3.21.13 most of the way through another 14 hour work day and several calls on my headset, it lost its connection and I had to actually go through the pairing process again. When I did the battery level has finally dropped to medium. I am going to guess the battery voltage drop had something to do with the pairing connection and am finally charging it for the first time since the initial charge.

"The Qi Full QT10 is a universal practical Qi Standard wireless charger pad, which makes use of electromagnetic induction technology, and can be used to charge all Qi-enabled wireless charging devices or other devices with a Qi wireless receiver or an external Qi wireless power backcover receiver".The charger has an elegant, sleek design and measures 5.90" x 3.25" x 0.25" (about the size of a Samsung Galaxy Note 3). It would not look out of place on a office desk, side table or bed stand. Very intuitive to operate and simple to use. Just plug it in via wall plug adapter or USB 2.0 into computer/Laptop - a LED will turn green indicating that the pad is charging in "standby mode". Place the mobile phone on top of it - the LED turns blue indicating that it is charging properly. If the phone is placed incorrectly on the pad, a high pitched sound & green flashing LED will alert you.If you need to use or answer the phone, just pick it up; no attachments and no cables to unplug.The main caveat is that the device to be charged must be Qi (chee) or CE capable to charge wirelessly (as indicated by a logo that looks like a circle with an "i" in the middle).Wireless receiver modules are available to add to a non-compatible phone. I have added one to my Samsung Galaxy S4 and to Galaxy Note 3; the S4 took up to 3.4 hours and the Note 3 up to 4.2 hours - for a full charge.The battery capacity is 5V/1500mAh with an expected efficiency of 70-80% (as all chargers). The pad can charge through a phone case of 10mm maximum thickness.The box contains the Qi charger pad, a cable with USB/ microUSB and a bilingual Chinese/English manual. The battery capacity is 5V/1500mAh with an expected efficiency of 70-80% (as all chargers). The pad can charge through a phone case of 10mm maximum thickness.Cons: Low amperes capacity.Should include wall plug adapter in package.Sleek phone contact surface. Would benefit from non-slip rubber rubber ring or band.A complimentary sample provided for review.

I owned these for two weeks. The design is decent. Size, fit and sound are good; not excellent, but good. The problem is the battery life, after a week of use, all I could get out of them was 60-70 minutes of music listening. That's just unacceptable in this day and age, when most Bluetooth headsets, are getting a minimum of 4 hours talk time. Everything else works fine, but an hour of use - really?I exchanged these on Amazon for the Jaybird Bluebud X. I haven't used them for more than a day. But, I have talked on the phone on them for a solid hour, and the battery is still showing green, which supposedly means "Well Charged". So, I'm impressed. Yes, they're 50% more expensive; but the Plantronics unit with an hour of battery life is a joke. The other big plus on the Jaybird - if you use it during workouts, as I do, they have a wire management system that allows you to wear them over the ear and the wire can be fitted to be snug to the back of your head, which means no wire flopping around while you do your workout. Very nice.I'll update this on battery life and other impressions on a Jaybird item review once I've spent a few weeks with the Bluebud.

I bought this unit last week on sale at a big-box retail store.  I've not had any of the problems that others have mentioned regarding being prompted to update to maps that are larger than the device capacity.  In fact, I noticed that the TomTom Home software now automatically links you to a new compressed map set for units with 2 GB capacity like this, which rectifies the problems others have stated.  It's too bad others went through the pain needed to solve the former problem, but it's fixed now and my user experience hasn't been hampered one bit by it, so it isn't entering into the rating of my review.I updated the map and IQ routes with TomTom Home without any issues and also put on some of the user-created voices and icons for giggles.  My four year-old will enjoy being navigated by Darth Vader's voice while watching a tie-fighter icon on our cross-country trip next month.  Lots of eye-candy customization is available.  Bearing in mind the warnings about map size, though, I always make sure I have space on the unit before accepting any updates of significant size.The maps and routing is pretty good, but after testing it significantly through known routes I've found some missteps.  The recommended route from my home to the Pittsburgh International airport was 41 minutes, but I suspected another route was shorter.  I embarked on the shorter route, the GPS calculated the new route, and came up with a 34 minute travel time.  I'm not sure why it didn't find the route to begin with, but it didn't, whereas my older Garmin, MapQuest, and Google all identify the quicker, albeit "quirkier", route.  Also, an Embassy Suites in the Raleigh area was not found in the points-of-interest database, which is odd since the hotel is not new.At the same time, the IQ Routes feature is nice.  It relies on a large sample of actual speed and travel data taken from TomTom users to create a more realistic calculation of travel time at different times of the day.  This pointed me to an alternative route to a trip I take often that I had not considered, but turned out to be a very efficient route at certain times of the day.Also, when I come across a missed point-of-interest, like the previous Embassy Suites example in Raleigh, I can add it to the GPS with just a few touches and share that update with other users.  I find this crowd-sourcing approach very appealing.In general, the routes have been good (not fantastic) and the unit is packed with features.  My wife was impressed (and jealous), so we bought a second one to replace an aging Garmin in her car.
Nice job on the OTG cable.  It just works and provided one to my mother and sister in law so that they can use their USB drives for the Nexus, smart phone and tablet.

EVALUATIONDoes the job, but there are virtually no instructions on its use.  Most of the complaints about lack of power are probably from folks who installed the batteries wrong.COMPARISONI love my big powerful hand blenderHamilton Beach 59770 Turbo-Twister 2-Speed Hand Blenderbut sometimes, it is just too much, and I don't want to go to the hassle of cleaning it.  For simply mixing a package of powdered chocolate mix into a cup of milk for hot chocolate, etc. the  Norpro is perfect, and cleanup is just a few seconds.  I use it mostly to make home-made gelatin desertsKnox Original Gelatin (32-Count Envelopes), Unflavored, 8-Ounce Boxes (Pack of 2).  For example grape "Jello" made with real grape juice.INSTALLING BATTERIES> The gray switch "panel" is the door to the battery compartment.  With the pointy end to the left, slide the grey panel to the right.  Install the first (bottom) battery with the positive end (nipple end) to the right.  Install the second (top) battery with the positive end to the left.  Slide the panel back in place.> The batteries are hard to install and harder to remove.  This can be remedied by applying silicon grease to the contactsPermatex 22058 Dielectric Tune-up Grease - 3 oz..  The silicon grease also protects the contacts and improves the electrical connection.***> Alternative, it is easy to make a "battery extractor": Cut a ca 1/2" x 4" strip of paper (or thin ribbon); roll the end around a battery, fold up to make a tab, and glue the tab back on the paper strip or ribbon (in effect make a "doggie collar and leash" for a battery); slip the bottom battery in place, then the top battery, if you have more than 1/2" left at the top, trim it off.  To extract the bottom battery (after extracting the top battery, just pull up on the leash.INTALLING MIXING TOOLSNotice that the lower portion of the stems of the mixing tools is hollow.  This hole fits over the solid metal rod in the power unit.  You just push the tool in, and when you are done, pull it out.  Sometimes the fit is pretty tight, and you have to push hard to install, or pull hard to take the tool out.  Be sure to push it ALL THE WAY on, as far as it will go.  If the fit is too tight, a little silicon grease (apply with a toothpick) fixes the problem.**USE> Lower the tool into the liquid to be mixed and press the grey lever.  Immerse only the lower half of the tool in the solution.  Do not immerse as far as the pointy end of the power unit, or you may suck up water (etc) into the motor.> The mixer is one-speed.  You can adjust the "violence" of mixing by choosing the tools.  Obviously the stick-like tool is the gentlest mixer.  The plastic Y is the second gentlest.  The wire whisk is the second most intense mixer, and the "frother" is the most intense mixer.  Use the frother only in a water glass or other tall, narrow, container.Although the whisk is intense, it also slows to pretty slow if immersed to most of it's length.  I find the plastic Y to be the ideal mixer for mixing gelatin into 3-4 cups of water, without making froth.WASHING UPNEVER submerge the power unit in water---just wipe it off.  You may be able to clean the tools adequately by simply operating the mixer in a glass of clean water.  If not, then pull off the tool and hand wash.*** Why silicon grease? Vegetable oil may turn gummy after a while, making the situation worse.  Petroleum-based oil can attract dust and damage rubber and plastic.  Silicon grease does not attract dust, does not damage plastic, is actually good for rubber, and improves and protects electrical connections.

I would have given it 0 stars if possible because not only were the cards hard to get working, but Netgear support proved to be useless.Brought two cards. One for an XP system, one for a 2000 system.  Followed instructions, and neither installed.  On the XP system, after trying all the usual fixes (reinstalling software, switching PCI slots, etc), the card finally worked after downloading the driver via Microsoft's "Windows Update" page (via built-in ethernet).  On the 2000 system, I ended up calling Netgear support (sounded outsourced), and they informed me that this card is NOT supported in Athlon systems.  End of story.  Sure enough, says right on the box that a "Pentium-III Class PC" is a requirement.  All of us Pentium-4 and Athlon owners are out of luck.I suggest you look elsewhere (particularly if you like decent support).  I'm now VERY happy with Motorola products (they managed to solve one issue I had without asking what processor I was using)

This Le Creuset Buffet Casserole's (Braiser) low, wide shape, with its curved walls is ideal for braising in the centuries old coastal Western Mediterranean Sea style: France's Provence-Alpes-Cote d'Azur, Languedoc-Roussillon and Corsica, Spain and the Balearic Isles, the long Italian coast and Sicily, and Greece with its many, many islands. Don't forget to braise matching the regional dish you choose with its regional wine, or California equivalents.This Braiser is known around the Mediterranean as a "low marmite." The 3.5 quart size is Le Creuset's medium-sized low, round braiser, or Buffet Casserole, ideal for entertaining or for making a meal for a family, or for a side dish when entertaining. The 5 quart size is best for entertaining, for a main dish. The thick, even heat, with no hot spots makes it ideal for simmering and  braising. By the way, this is NOT a frying pan, and is NOT intended for fierce heat. It is a Braiser or Casserole. You already have frying pans and saute pans, I hope. The high domed lid allows you to use bulkier veggies or larger chunks of meat, poultry or seafood, and it self-bastes at the same time.Vegetables are added to seafood, or lamb, or chicken, or beef, or veal pieces and then simmered. You can do it like pizza, add anything you want, but you must have some cooking experience to venture out into non-recipe land. Or you can find cookbooks that outline the style. Make sure to use good olive oil, garlic, shallots, mushrooms, artichoke hearts, garlic and good fresh chopped herbs too, all to your taste, as desired, to your Mediterranean recipes. Of course, many, many other non-Med. dishes can be done in it. And what a presentation it makes...guest's eyes will pop.The low, wide shape allows everything to to cook evenly and slowly, in a thinner layer than a deep pot, in some liquid such as water, stock, broth, wine, or a combination of the aforesaid. Simmer down slowly, very slowly until all the liquid evaporates (I leave the lid slightly ajar), but make sure not to burn the bottom, so use gentle heat...a light simmer, be patient because if you do then all the flavors concentrate and explode...mmmmm. Turn every so often...keep an eye on it, dance in the kitchen to the radio or CD. Try it for cacciatore (hunter's stew), especially chicken cacciatore...to die for (See the Giada De Laurentiis' recipe online (pollo alla cacciatora). This is how it is done in those outdoor cafes and restaurants that line the Mediterranean fisherman's harbors overlooking the sea. This is the kind of pot they useImagine crab or lobster with veggies? Or chicken or good sausage with a veggie base, in red wine. It is expensive, but it will take you, simply by playing with it, to new culinary heights. This low Braiser is not a deep, round pot meant for smaller pieces of meat such a a stew, or a vessel for a large, oblong piece of meat (pot roast) that matches the ovoid shape of an oval French Oven.The lower profile is better for quick veggie braises because it cooks in a thinner layer than a deep pot (20-30 minutes). Or for longer veggie/meat braises (40-90 minutes). We use the Braiser/Buffet Casserole far more frequently than our deep braisers: 5 quart and 7 1/4 quart Round French oven, or our 5 quart and 7 1/4 quart Oval French ovens.You really want to get into this kind of fantastic Mediterranean cooking? Then check this out. The book series "The Beautiful," in coffee table size, with exquisite color plates, should be collected by those into fine cooking, e.g., Provence the Beautiful, Tuscany the Beautiful, Spain the Beautiful, France or or Italy the Beautiful, Mediterranean the Beautiful, you get the idea...even America the Beautiful. I buy mine used here on Amazon at amazing prices! The recipes are just out of this world, simple, wholesome, down-to-earth...what the people there cook and eat...lucky them. What a cuisine...makes me hungry typing this.--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------Pros:*Heavy gauge for even heat spread out over the entire base and up the sides., and from the lid down...360 degree radiation of heat, like an oven. No hot and cold spots, ever, always perfectly even heat, especially on the bottom. Retains heat...at table it keeps food hot longer. Can be placed in the freezer afterward.*Easy to clean, even burn-ons, so just soak, or boil with vinegar if really bad or use Bar Keeper's Friend. Usually just wipe clean or use a "safe for non-stick" scrub pad. Dishwasher safe.*Non-reactive chemically to wine, citrus, tomato, anything acidic. Being enameled, your Le Creuset Braiser is sealed from the bare cast iron so it will neither react chemically nor give off odd tastes or colors as would food cooked on a bare cast iron surface.*Drop-dead gorgeous presentation, oven-to-table. What a centerpiece when entertaining. It's a testament to your good taste, and honors your guests.*Super durable, will last generations if cared for and not abused, or left empty on the burner for a long period of time.*101 year warranty, pretty much no-fault too. You cannot beat that. NO COOKWARE COMPANY HAS THAT POLICY! NONE! I have replaced several pieces over many years--no questions asked, just tell them the the reason for return, get a return authorization number, pay the shipping to them, and they pay the shipping back to you. You can even select a different piece and different color than what you returned. Incredible.*Good, tight fitting lid keeps the heat in. Its weight prevents steam building up and wobbling the lid, leaking heat.Cons:*Expensive. Again, just another case of you get what you pay for. Each piece is made by hand, then the mold is broken...one-of-a-kind. Several coats of enamel are used. The enamel is baked at outrageously high temperatures for quite a bit of time. It's very expensive to do that. Each piece seems like a labor of love.Stove-top safe and oven-safe. Le Creuset is also induction-safe.This is something you will to the next generation, so the cost, spread over years, is actually negligible. Americans are learning to invest in high quality rather than cheap, poor performing throw-away junk. The Europeans have done so for many generations, they cannot afford to keep buying the same low durability junk over and over again.*There are always minor imperfections. That happens when each artisan handles his or her own pieces, like artwork. No robots are involved in making it, just highly skilled human beings.*It can be heavy. Especially when food or liquid is involved along with the weight of the vessel. It's the weight that makes it cook so well. A good trade off, unless you have health problems involving muscles.Summary: Extremely durable, perfect-cooking vessel. Highly attractive and satisfying to own and use. Will take daily use for decades and comes with a 101 year warranty. Highly recommended.I hope this was helpful to you. If you have questions, please reply in the Comment section below...I would be glad to help.__________________________________________________________________________________________________MY NEW BLOG: thegoodstuffreviews.blogspot.com

I bought my first TR2500BC about 3 years ago and loved it. The controls for loaf size and crust color allowed me to make bread that's "squishy" enough for my kids. I even bought a second one a year later for the cabin. They jump a little on the counter, but did their work well.Then, at some point in late 2007, one of the pans broke. The paddle shaft in the bottom stopped turning. I called Breadman (or whichever consumer product company owns the brand) and asked to buy a replacement. They said they were backordered. A few weeks later I got a paddle. I called them and explained I had plenty of paddles, I needed the pan. Again, I was told they were backordered, but that my order would be filled when they came in. Several months later I got ANOTHER paddle. So I called again, and was told that they weren't making replacement pans any more. I hunted on eBay and Craigslist and couldn't find anyone trying to sell the pan without the machine.After trying to remember to shuttle my one good pan between home and the cabin, I gave up and bought a new machine, the Panasonic YD250. Hated it. No crust control, and it overcooked everything. And I don't know what was weird about the rise cycle, but my breads always rose too much.I was up at the cabin and decided to bring the Breadman home and take the Panasonic up there since mostly what I do up there is dough, which the Panasonic manages not to screw up too badly. The next day, a box arrived in the mail. I didn't remember ordering anything, and didn't recognize the shipper. So imagine my astonishment when I opened the box and found -- the Breadman bread pan! Luckily I had kept the extra paddles they had sent. So now I can go back to having the Breadman each place and sell the Panasonic to someone who likes dark crust. This has to take a prize for late order fulfillment.Anyway, I'm glad to be back with the Breadman. If I was made of more money, I'd get the Zojirushi, but I have no complaints.Update December 2010:Well, the replacement pan broke. Whatever it is that holds the spindle in the bottom (the one you put the paddle on) lost something so the spindle falls out everytime I wash it. Machine bakes well, but the pan was trash; it worked if I held it together just right to get it back in the machine. I saw a lot of people complaining about not being able to get new pans, but I just found a website associated with Breadman where you can order them for about $12. I've ordered two. The site is [...]. They can't fix the stupid little clips on the sides of the machine that hold the pan in, but I've always had luck bending them back into shape so the pan quits doing jumping jacks.I was at Costco the other day and saw a machine with the Cuisinart brand on it. It seemed horribly flimsy, and cost just a few bucks less than this. I sure wouldn't have trusted it to last. I make pizza dough in this one at least once a week (using the recipe from the book) and it's the best. Also make a buttermilk bread that my kids adore, and a Swedish rye bread that takes my husband back to his childhood. Can't live without the machine, and if they finally have a parts source that works, I'm happy.

The Zebby 3.1 amp dual USB car charger is a great car charger to charge 2 devices at once at a fast speed.Pros:-3.1 amps of power shared between the USB ports-One USB port has a max output of 2.1 amps (labeled) and other USB port has a max output of 1.0 amps (labeled)-Very small in size for its very high output of 3.1 amps-Can charge an iPad Air (2.1 amp port) and an iPhone 5 (1 amp port) at a fast speed-Metal ring around the car charger adds a cool look to it (silver part of the car charger on both color combos)-High quality construction-Fairly priceCons:-Cannot charge 2 tablets at the same time (tablets require 2.1-2.4 amps [iPads] of power to charge at full speed)I recommend this car charger.I was supplied a sample for test and evaluation, for free to keep, and I promised that my review is fair and honest.

I have used Calphalon pans quite a lot and they really are some of the best cookware you can buy.  This pan didn't get much use in my kitchen, I tend to use the frying pan and pots more than this.  Like the other Calphalon pots and pans, this one is made of heavy-gauge aluminum which I absolutely love.  It heats up quickly and evenly.  It also has stay-cool handles, which are great for me when I'm having a moment and forget the pot holders!I have used this particular pan for making omelettes and browning meat for tacos, so while its not the most commonly used pan in my kitchen it has seen a decent amount of use.  I've never had a problem with the stick-resistant surface, its always been super easy to clean after each use.  If something is particularly sticky, I just soak the pan in water for 20 minutes and then scrub it down.  I also only use nylon Calphalon utensils, using metal will probably scratch up the pan.The only bad thing about Calphanlon pans is that you are not supposed to put them in the dishwasher.  You "can" but it tends to damage the surface and it also voids the lifetime warranty (which is one of the main reasons I tried Calphalon in the first place!).

I wanted a gamer oriented motherboard that could handle the latest intel cpu's. I bought two: one for my son's computer and one for mine.  The board fit nicely in both cases.  The bios is very well laid out and has all the controls and flexibility you would want to over clock your computer.  I installed intel 3820 processors in both systems along with 16gb of ram from corsair.  This system has worked very well for both me and my son and we have fun playing our reloaded games on a very fast system.  The instructions and web site were well laid out, detailed and easy to use.  All of the drivers I needed were readily available.  Upgrading the bios to the latest version was easy.  Keep in mine if you are over clocking your equipment that you have a good cooling system and that what you purchase will fit on the motherboard within the case.  I did have problems with the corsair ram heat sinks as they are right uo against the cpu and interfered with the CPU heat sink I had purchased.  I had to remove the ram heat sink to make the CPU heat sink work.  Since I am not over clocking the ram or the CPU this worked out ok.  In general I am very pleased with my purchase and how it worked out.

I ordered these for my wife as we were about to go on a trip and she couldn't find her older model Audio-Technicas. Well, the new ones arrived promptly, just as we were leaving, and just as she found her old ones. No time to return the new ATs, and by the time we came home, the return window was almost closed. #1 Source said I could return the ATs without a restocking fee since we hadn't opened the box of the new ones. Good customer service, I'd say.For the record, I've had ATs before (the older model, of which this one is an upgrade), and I liked them, except that the ear cups were too small, so I couldn't wear them for multiple hours at a time. And the band connecting the ear cups pressed uncomfortably on my head. New model has larger ear cups, and I might have bought it (when my old ones broke inexplicably) if a friend hadn't given me some reconditioned Bose QC15s. The Bose are fine, and certainly more comfortable than the old ATs. However, I don't like having to have noise cancellation switched on in order to listen to music. Why would Bose do that? If you're not trying to counter ambient noise, turning on noise cancellation detracts from the music, is annoying, and wears down the batteries.All in all, if I were buying new ones, I'd try these ATs. And although I am often leery of buying electronics from Amazon affiliates rather than Amazon itself, I would not hesitate to do business with #1 Source. They did the right thing with no fuss.

The quick-guide helps with layering the ingredients in the right order to keep ingredients blending rather than getting stuck.And, the recipe book has simple, fast recipes for soups, dips, and smoothies--which can be modified--like cheesy potato soup, kale and spinach soup with asiago cheese.I haven't had any problems with this powerful and fast blender. Advice: wash the pitcher immediately in warm soapy water to avoid having to scrub any sticky ingredients off later.No regrets about this investment as it has made it more convenient to eat healthy meals and breakfast smoothies on-the-go for people like me who are too lazy to make and sit down for breakfast before work.

I will be the first to admit that I like OXO products and have quite a number of them around.  I have yet to be disappointed in any of them.  I will also be the first to admit that I do not like change all that much and have fought the good fight in trying to keep my old bristle brushes I have been so fond of.  Well I finally broke down and bought one of these things for pastry.Now I have been using a silicone brush on our outside gas grill and must admit to liking it.  Why I did not want to use it on indoor cooking I do not know.  Anyway....This is an excellent brush.  As the OXO company tells you, the way these things are designed it holds the liquid longer on the brush than other brands.  This particular brush feels perfect in the hand and has a very nice balance to it.  Since I do all the dishwashing in our family, I like something that I can stick into the dishwasher and not have to worry about it.  This brush cleans up very nice.I have used this for about a year now.  In using the natural bristle brushes I would go through several in a year's time as they washed poorly, retained odors, lost bristles and were constantly clumping up.  No problems with this brush in any of those areas.  I figure I am getting a better job done and saving money at the same time.Love this brush. The price here is about two dollars less expensive that what I paid for it at a local retail story.Don BlankenshipThe Ozarks

The Mountek nGroove is innovative in it's approach, and reading other reviews, I expected magic.  However, I found the following shortcomings with this mount:1.  Vibration - Others have written about the mount being rock solid.  However, holding a Galaxy Note 2 is a stretch for any holder,  The Note 2 is a big, heavy phone and it really stresses the nGroove's mounting technique.2.  My Note 2 is inside a Urban Armor Gear case.  The nGroove's side arms are lined with high density foam in the shape of rectangles without centers.  I found it necessary to remove the top and bottom of the foam "O's" to get a secure fit with the UAG case.3.  The tightening screw with winged head is long enough for the nGroove to be used in dash cracks that must be 1/2" wide.  Used in a CD player slot, I found it too long and it extended over the face of the head unit.  I promptly replaced it with a M6X20mm socket head screw.  This is the proper length if you intend to mount the unit in a CD player slot.  You can get them a Lowe's or Home Depot for about a buck for a pack of three.What the mount needs to be better is 1) a stabilizing strut to some other part of the vehicle to stop the vibration, and 2) deeper arms to grab the device inside "all known cases".I give the nGroove 4 out of 5 stars.  It's not magic, only manageable.
