Seriously, this is the best purchase that I've ever made on Amazon.  It's been hot in Southern California, and it's important to keep hydrated.  And a 24 ounce tall glass of water or diluted juice is what we all need.  And having the bottom of the tumbler shaped to fit perfectly into the drink holder of my new Outback enables me to have it close by at all times.  Of course, I had to purchase the lid to keep from spilling in my new car.  When the lid is closed up, the system is completely air tight, and doesn't drip even when I hold it upside down, and shake it.  Having the double wall construction really keeps the liquid insulated so that it stays cold for a long time-- and it doesn't sweat or feel cold to the touch.  I fill it every night, and leave it in the refrigerator so that the contents will be cold in the morning.  I was concerned about BPA because the product description on Amazon didn't say anything about it.  But when I checked the website, it was clear that Tervis products are BPA free.  I strongly recommend buying the lid for this tumbler if you're going to travel with it.  They are a little difficult to attach and detach, but that's the price we pay for having a very secure fit.  Opening the drinking hole cover allows the air to escape, rather than pushing against the lid, so that helps.  Also, there is a review that complains about the drinking hole cover spraying liquid everywhere when it's closed.  I found that to be true until I found a way to close it slowly rather than snapping it shut.  But that and being difficult to attach to the tumbler become non issues after getting a little experience.  I like the fact that these tumblers are unbreakable and guaranteed forever.  It's a very high quality product that won't disappoint you.  I just read that one of the easiest and most effective things we can do to benefit our health is to drink an extra glass of water per day.  And this tumbler makes that much easier.  I can't say enough about it, and I feel strange that a tumbler has made me so happy.  I even enjoy trying different juices and teas to mix, to make the best combinations.  Thank you Tervis for creating an amazing product line!

I switched to this case from a Griffin Survivor light case (and before that, an Otterbox Defender).  The Thule gauntlet case is much lighter and easy to hold. I do agree with the other reviewer who said the camera cutout is a bit too big; you see both the black glossy area and the aluminum. Some iPhone cases limit the type of after market lightning cords you can use (for example, the Lifeproof case).  No issues here as the entire bottom headphone port and charge port are accessible, as well as the volume and mute buttons.  For a little heavier duty for scratch protection on the front, you can combine with a screen protector.  I would definitely recommend this if the item will be carried in a purse or in your pocket with items such as keys.  Overall, a great and sleek case worthy of the iPhone ;)

My wife and I both started using this case a couple days ago, for our 4th- and  5th-generation iPads, respectively (this review will pertain mostly to the iPad Air model). My wife had already warned me against getting Apple's Smart Cover. It managed to allow a couple scratches through, and is now starting to fall apart after only a year. Also its two options for stand height aren't enough and the taller option never felt all that stable to me. Finally the Apple cover starts to look quite dirty over time--possibly due in part to the material.All that is presented to offer points of comparison to this Moko cover. I wanted a non-keyboard case/cover that would offer suitable protection, was fairly durable, and offered a variety of stand height options. So far, this cover appears to offer all that and more. The reinforced front and back cover seem quite sturdy, but aren't so much as to feel bulky; this cover lets the iPad keep its sleek profile while actually adding a touch of elegance. The six-point clasp system holds the iPad securely in place; it takes some effort to wrest the tablet free--this is mostly a good thing (I'll address the potentially concerning part of this in the stand height paragraph below). Since the retention system doesn't go around the entire perimeter (as does the Apple cover), three of the outer edges of the iPad remain exposed. This is a bit concerning, especially when you see that a few millimeters of the actual pack of the tablet are also exposed at both the top and bottom. I plan to install a full-body screen protector for the back of my Air to minimize potential scratches from this exposure. The plan for now, though, is to proceed with a dash of caution. Overall, however, the case promises to do its primary job well. An added bonus is the strength of the front cover's closed-position clasp. It attaches very securely and is a reliable physical barrier to the case swinging open by accident. This puts me eat ease more than would a case relying only on magnets to keep the front cover closed.The stand height options is one of the great strengths of this case. The four angles seem to suit a variety of needs. I have mine taller when using a Bluetooth keyboard or just browsing at the table, and more shallow when I need to use the screen keyboard. I find it really does help to have two different "talls" and another two "shorts." Further, the slots designed for stand use are deep enough that the iPad doesn't shake or feel like it will buckle, even as I type this review with fairly heavy fingers on the screen keyboard. The only thing that I'm a bit worried about is that you have to remove two of the iPad corners from their retention clasps to use the case in stand mode; look closely at the Amazon images and you'll see what I mean. I don't like the idea of potentially forgetting to re-clasp those corners when finished, and the fact that you need to exert effort to release the corners from the clasp (as mentioned above) makes me fearful about causing a bit of early wear-and-tear damage. I'll update this review after a few months to report on how that goes.As I mentioned before, the cover meets my expectations, and then some. This stand as "smart" capable---activating the iPad's sleep function when closed---beware that not all budget-priced covers will do this! The included stylus holder is also great, but doesn't seem to get in the way if you have no intention of using it. In flat open position, the included hand grip is useful for freeing up the other hand to use the tablet when roaming around, though I find  the placement of the hand grip more awkward in landscape mode. Maybe a diagonal design for this would have been more accommodating. Still, none of the nit-picks I've addressed in this review keep this cover from being a great five-star choice, and wonderful value. I will happily update this review as needed once I've had more time with the product.

I ordered two of these babies. One I gave to my roommate and one for myself. We both have black iPhones and the contrast is great. Really sets the look off. I bought this on a whim and I loved it so much I bought it twice. Definitely worth it.

comfortable to use, and really great at keeping hands protected from hot pans!  I used them when we had cast iron pans on an open flame while camping, and didn't feel the heat through them at all!  They are a simple pattern and design and would go with any decor!

After very mixed results with Jabra bluetooth headsets in the past, this is easily the best Jabra product I've used at this price range (currently $128).  It's easy to set up and very versatile which makes this a worthwhile endeavor for the user who plans to take advantage of the headset with a phone and a computer.  It's also very comfortable which is a major plus, and if it weren't for the slightly inferior sound compared to some alternatives at this price point, it would be a solid five star product.Out of the box, it works with minimal hassle and setup.  I'm using this with an iPhone 4, and I've had no problems getting it up and running, even with the latest cumbersome iOS.  Connecting the bluetooth to the phone has been no problem, and with the 360 feature, you can connect to your computer or other bluetooth enabled devices.  I have connected this to my Mac Mini with very clear and concise results.  I have experienced no lags or drops in sound quality whilst using the product, and the performance stays consistent with heavy usage.  Other callers report no trouble hearing me loud and clear.The headset is also very comfortable which has been an issue in my experience with past Jabra products.  It's very unobtrusive, and fits nicely without sliding or shifting around.The one slight negative is the sound quality just isn't as good as it could be.  It seems a bit tinny to my ears, especially when making phone calls on the go.  This perhaps could be a product of my aging iPhone, but I doubt it given other newer bluetooth sets sounding just fine with it.  An upgrade in sound would be a nice thing here, especially since the MSRP is $200.  It definitely should contain a broader range of sound with a price point this high.Given the positives, though, and the overall quality for the current price, I would highly recommend this set to someone who plans on using this moderately, with both their phone and computer.  It's comfortable and versatile, which in these times gives any bluetooth set an edge.

I am not a professional just a mom and grammy. I love that this camera gives me all the benefits of my old Vivitar manual camera and my Cannon camcorder in one. I can zoom not only from the top button on your right looking out, but there is a manual zoom tab on your left of the lense (this little option is great for the left handed).I have always wanted to do panoramic pics and this makes it so easy. You set the dial and choose to pan left to right or right to left. Then all you do is take the first photo, and move camera to the other 2 positions. There is a white circle with empty plus in middle on screen.  You move the camera until you see a yellow plus sign. When you match up the plus sign to the empty circle this camera automatically takes the 2nd and 3rd photos. There is no way to make a mistake on the panoramic photo.The video recorder is awesome. I was impressed, that I was able to video tape my new grandson (I was next to him) in a resturant and it picked up his ahs and coos.  I purchased to get the most out of my camera.SanDisk Extreme 32 GB SDHC Class 10 UHS-1 Flash Memory Card 45MB/s (SDSDX-032G-X46)The 3 inch screen on the back is nice and clear and since digital you can review and delete as wanted.The lithium ion rechargeable battery is specialized, you can recharge it.Love that it comes with a strap to attache the lense cap to the camera (no more lost lense caps).Comfortable shoulder / neck strap has a texture on the underside to keep it from sliping around.Push the top button on your left looking out to make the flash pop up ( so easy).You can change the photo to be black and white (many more functions to discover).Box includes instructions, Camera, battery, a compact wall charger, cables to hook up to computer (for downloard), lense cap, short lense strap to attach, and shoulder strap.

I'm amazed at how handy this little blender is!I love my big Kitchenaid stand mixer, but love the ease and convenience of this handheld.Smoothies for two are as easy as dumping a few ingredients (bananas, yogurt, splash of OJ, wheat germ) in the cup, blending very quickly, and pouring into cups for a super-fast breakfast on the way to work.For desserts, pour 1/2 cup heavy cream, 1 1/2 Tbsp powdered sugar, and 1 tsp of vanilla into the cup and zap with the whisk attachment for delicious fresh whipped cream in under 2 minutes.On less decadent days, we also use it for black bean vegetable soup; blending some of the beans makes a thick, creamy soup without adding calories.Clean up is a breeze and storage is, to - this little unit stacks and has a very small "footprint" in the cabinet.Ours has been getting regular use for quite awhile now and we've had no trouble with stripped gears or any other issues. We've never used the chopper on very hard foods like nuts or carrots, and we don't use this unit to blend ice cubes.  I wonder if it is best suited for softer foods, like we typically use it for?  For our purposes, it is perfect!

It makes the screen too dark during the daytime and doesn't work well as a privacy screen. I also couldn't get all the bubbles out and the ones that I could get out would just come back so this is a waste of money, time, and effort....

The Asus O'Play Air does the job for me. I've read some reviews around the Internet complaining about what this unit doesn't do. I'm not sure what those individuals expect for this price. This is exactly what I wanted to stream audio and video from my laptop to my home theater/audio system.Being an IT professional, I thought the setup was easy. The manual is pretty good, so if you can set up a wireless router, you should be able to set up the O'Play Air. If you want to stream video I'd recommend a 100 mbps wired or 802.11n wireless connection. Composite video cables are included, but I used a spare HDMI cable I had instead. If you're primarily going to stream audio and lower quality video, then the composite cables should suffice. The included remote is full-size, and well made. The setup menus are fairly easy to navigate - similar to a cable or satellite box. If your unit isn't loaded with the latest firmware, you should update it before you go through the setup process. (This might seem like a lot to ask for a brand-new product, but it's a fairly easy process and will give you a better feature set. It's also nice to be able to upgrade the feature set, rather than simply living with what's in the box.)Using the O'Play Air is easy, although it does require drilling down through menus to get to the actual playable media. I'd like to see Asus make it easier to navigate right to my music or videos. That said, music and video playback is excellent, with no degradation or streaming issues. I mostly use it for music because I don't rip videos to hard drive. I have HD cable and a Blu-ray player that provide most of the video I want to see. The latest firmware includes the ability to stream media from the Internet. This includes a pre-loaded list of Internet TV and radio stations, which I rarely use. You can also view Picassa and Flickr images directly from the Net. (A YouTube link would be nice.) The local storage options are excellent, but I haven't found a need to use them - other than using the SD card slot to upgrade the firmware. Honestly, I bought this box so I *didn't* need another storage device. But the option to let a friend bring over an external HDD, thumb drive, or SD card is pretty cool.For less than the price of an iPod, I got a media player that handles DRM-free media on full-size screen and real speakers. I can play the many hours worth of media, in a wide range of file formats. That's what I wanted, and that's what I got.

I have been using Galaxy Note II for several months and recently used for a comparison at a friend's place with S3. Both are running similar software and except for the pen capabilities, S3 is identical from a user's point of view. Pictures are awesome Rear: 8 Mega pixel Front: 1.9 Mega pixel. 64 MB microSD card makes storage abundant with all of the Google Drive/Music/Amazon mp3/box/dropbox and all of these.In essence, phone is super speed and is nice. With iphone's 3.5 or 4" is looking too small compared to awesome phones Samsung is releasing. I wish they continue the upward trend and lose focus on design/weight/features/updates.

I wanted this head set to use in the gym; one that I could use without rattling around my iPod or iPhone and most of all so that it wouldn't be sweaty.  For that job, it works fine.It connected easily. FM does not work well. It has a short awkward necklace and as a female, it's not the most attractive to wear. This is not a book, it is a cheap pamphlet (see photos I posted).  The printing is crooked, the cover is poorly cut, and the binding is nothing but a couple of regular staples.  The entire thing looks like someone printed the cover at home on photo paper, and used cheap paper for the pages.  The pages are crooked, and the cover doesn't even reach the end of the pages - it's cut too short!  This pamphlet should come free with the Nordic Ware Bundt Cake Pans.  Also, the cover of the "book" I received does not match the picture on Amazon.  I would't pay more than the cost of shipping for this "book."  I rated it with two stars simply because it does have several interesting recipes.About the material: This book contains recipes for cakes (50% of the book), sauces, breads (quick and yeast), entrees (ex. burger in a bundt), salads (ex. "strawberry sour cream mold"), special desserts, glazes, and includes a notes section. It's more than a dessert book, though I don't feel compelled to make much more than dessert bundt cakes. Something about "Dill Sauced Salmon Ring," "Turkey in the Bundt," or "Lazy Days Meat Ring" don't sound appetizing to me.Updates:3/14/2012 -  The main photo of the "book" has changed since I bought it.  Now, the photo shown on Amazon is a photo of the same "book" that I received.8/15/2012 - The main photo of the "book" has been changed back to the cover of the "book" shown when I ordered the it.  However, the pamphlet I received did not match the picture.  This makes me wonder, why did they change the main picture, again?  Will a new shopper receive the currently shown cover or a different cover?  Are there two versions of this "book"?4/19/2014 - It appears that my photos of this "book" have been removed.  Judging by other reviews, there are a few different versions of this item floating around, some better than others.  Two years have past since my original review, so perhaps the book has improved. I just can't help but laugh every time I see the version I received (and I'm not alone according to other reviews). Mine is not even cut straight (as my pictures showed) - literally the spine of the book is shorter than the length of the opening of the book. The cover doesnt even reach the end of the pages, it's cut too short. On a bookshelf it sits crooked because the bottom is crooked. And, it gets lost in a bookshelf because it's no bigger than a Blu-Ray manual (109 pages).  Some comments say this book is "dated."  That seems apt. I still find humorous all the fancy ways to say "meatloaf" (a.k.a. "meat ring" "log" "mold").

I purchased the "PUR CR-6000C 2-Stage Oval Water Pitcher with LED Indicator" to replace the Brita pitcher we had used as an auxiliary to our larger dispenser.  (This model of PUR was being phased out by the big box hardware store and was marked down by 50%.)Water in our area contains a significant amount of minerals and often has a chlorine odor; if you want good tasting water, using a filtration product is very necessary. All our drinking water now tastes very clean, crisp, and fresh.  The double filter may be the reason for this "purity" of taste. While the PUR filters are more expensive than Brita replacement filters, the cleaner tasting water is worth it. Further, we no longer have charcoal specks floating anywhere in the water - hurray!The slim, oval shape makes the "PUR CR-6000C 2-Stage Oval Water Pitcher with LED Indicator" easy to fit onto your refrigerator shelf.  It is not bulky, nor is it hard to fit other items in around the pitcher. However, the LED indicator light on the PUR is worthless.  It worked for the first few months and has since indicated "red" meaning the filter needs to be changed.  Even when we replace the filter, the red light remains lighted.If you are considering the "PUR CR-6000C 2-Stage Oval Water Pitcher with LED Indicator", I recommend purchasing the PUR 7-cup Pitcher CR-6000 instead.  It costs a bit less and provides the same filtered, clean-tasting water.

AUGUST 2012 UPDATE: I now notice that Orka or the seller has removed the heat-resistant information from the Amazon website. I have contacted Amazon to find out if it is still 400. If so, then I would recommend going with theProgressive 9-inch Silicone Gripper Tongs in Green. They are a shorter tong, but they are heat-resistant to 600 F.APRIL 2012 UPDATE: Since my original review, Orka has altered the heat resistant abilities of these tongs. These used to be good to 570 F and are now good to only 400 F. I will leave my original review as is. Mine are good to 570F.ORIGINAL REVIEW:Originally I thought 12-inch tongs would be too long, so I ordered both these and theProgressive 9-inch Silicone Gripper Tongs in Green.When I received both sets, I preferred the 12-inch tongs. That extra length is useful when you are turning something in popping grease and you want some distance between the range's heat and your hands.In addition, the silicone grips are large and flexible, making it easy to grasp and turn just about anything, even when food is sticking.  I also like that they are heat-resistant to 570 Fahrenheit. (This has now changed. Please see update at top of review.)The Progressive 9-inch tongs are still a nice set of tongs if you want smaller tongs. They also have the advantage of being heat resistant to 600 Fahrenheit.Coming from a background of using the more traditional scissor-like tongs, I was a bit apprehensive as to whether I could adjust to this style of tong, but I did so with no problem.Silicone utensils are recommended by some manufacturers of stainless cookware, like All-Clad and Cuisinart, to keep from scratching stainless steel cookware. They also work great with hard anodized and nonstick cookware.With regard to what other reviewers have mentioned: I have not had a problem with a tight spring in these tongs. They do not feel tight or uncomfortable to use. I pull on the loop and I open them. These are only 12 inches so may be too small to turn very large cuts of meat.Regarding the actual size of these tongs: These are NOT 10-inch tongs as claimed by another reviewer! The actual tongs, not counting the loop, are 1/8" shy of 11 inches. The actual tongs are almost two inches longer than the Progressive 9-inch tongs. The 12-inch measurement includes the loop.

I've owned a small Hoover canister vacuum since the 80's, that I loved, and it finally wore out.  I've been looking for a replacement vac for my hardwood and tile floors. I purchased a Sanyo canister vac at a local vacuum store and hated it. I knew I didn't want another bagless vacuum. I spent a long time reading reviews for canister vacs for hard surfaces, and the Electrolux kept popping up as a vacuum people really liked.  After the Sanyo fiasco, I was leery about ordering a vacuum without trying it out, but I was getting desperate to get a replacement.  I ordered the Electrolux from Amazon (it was $299.00 at Lowes) and absolutely LOVE IT!!  It's quiet, which means I don't have to wear hearing protectors, which I do with my other vacs because I have tinnitus, and loud noises really bother my ears.  I also love the flexible hose, and the suction is fantastic.  I tried it on my area rugs, and it really works.  My old canister vac didn't pick up dirt on my area rugs, and my upright sucks them right into the machine.  The suction was perfect with the Electrolux.  It is a little heavier than my old canister, but, unlike my old vac, the weight keeps it from tipping over when I pull it around corners.  Like other reviewers, my only complaint is that it doesn't have anywhere to carry the tools onboard, but that's a small price to pay for all of the other features.I hate to admit it, but when I saw the combination tool that comes with the vac, I thought from the picture in the instructions that I was missing a part.  A very patient customer service rep kept insisting that there were no other parts for that tool.  He finally got one of the tools and saw what I was trying to explain.  When the brush end of the tool is placed over the hose, the clips on the other end  of the tool (for fabric, curtains, etc) actually fold down to form a new nozzle.  Now I know they have good customer service, even though I felt like a moron.Anyway, I highly recommend this vacuum for anyone who has lots of hard surface floors and area rugs.

Just got this today for my Canon 60D and it works great. I read reviews around the web after i bought it saying it doesnt work for DSLR's, but this works fine and its a great flash for the money. Feels pretty sturdy to be all plastic. When first turning on the flash it takes about 20 seconds to get a charge and 30 seconds for a full charge after that its about a 4-6 second recycle time depending on how high or low the flash output is. Its a simple flash unit but works great so far, im happy with it.***update***When i was timing the time it took to charge the flash i was using the batteries that came with the flash. I replaced them with rechargeable duracells @1700mAh and the charge/recycle time is waaay faster. about 9 seconds for a usable charge and 11-13 seconds for the flash to be fully charged and the recycle is about 3 seconds and 4-5 seconds to be fully charged again. I get 3 flashes before it drains.I also used rechargeable duracells @2450mAh and the charge/recycle time went down about 1 second. When you get the flash use better batteries to get better performance.

Its neither slate nor metal.  Which you probably kind of guessed.The Spigen case is from a Korean company called SGP. Korea is really doing well producing consumer products.  Hyundai is a common car these days, Samsung is doing good things in several industries (including being Apple's main rival for the iPhone), and China looks to Korea for many new products and fashion cues.So I was curious to see how this case stacked up.The case is made to precision specs.  It fits the iPhone 5 like a glove, with razor thin tolerances that fit just right.The main case is a soft TPE material, and the so-called "metal slate" is a piece of plastic that clips onto the soft rubbery TPE to shore it up and keep it on the case.I've seen cases like this before, and this Spigen is probably the best example of design.The packaging is over the top, very nice.  Probably overengineered.There are no instruction on installation, only removal.  I guess they figured you can puzzle it out on your own.Also included are three "jelly bean" home buttons.  Right now, your iphone has a recessed home button which is easy to find. Plus, since its recessed you can't press it by accident.  SPG figures these are superfluous details, and gives you some stick-on Home button covers so that your home button sticks out all the time.  I'm trying that part out still, I'm not sure I like it.  But you don't have to use the jelly bean home buttons, you can just throw them out.Overall I think its a good case at a good price.
Works well with at least two and appears acceptable with three at a time (although all three not near empty to start if that matters).

I thought I had a great thermal mug before I bought this, and I did.  But this ridiculously named mug is superb.  My tea stays hot from morning well into the afternoon.  It has a high plastic rim so you never have to press hot metal against your lips, and there is a push button inside that opens and closes to start the flow of liquid.  The high rim screws off, and you can wash all parts in the top rack of the dishwasher.  It's wide at the bottom, and you'd really have to work to at it to knock it over.  If there is any small disadvantage, it's that you really have to tip up the bottom to drink to the bottom of the 26.5-ounce container.

My husband is an avid hunter and you also know that I'm super cheap. These two things combined means that when he gets a deer, we always process our own meat. I've been perfecting challah and was using a lot of foil to keep it -- and the egg baste -- from sticking to my baking sheets.These mats are fantastic!I didn't love the idea of fiberglass woven into the baking mats but from my research it looks like this is the normal/safe way to achieve this kind of mat.... I have thick fingers and use the stylus/ S pen to text and take notes. The size of the stylus has always been an issue for me. This product solves that issue and more. My accuracy while texting has improved greatly. No more writers cramp from sweezing that teeny tiny stylus. Now my handwriting looks like handwriting, not monkey scratch.  The only negative, and this is more a critisism of myself really, is that I have tried to use it to write with on several occassions mistaking it for my ballpoint pen.For those complaining about price, it will cost you anywhere from $20 to $30 to replace the S pen. I have been playing with my Nuvi 360 for about a day and I really like this device. The software is superb and it does everything that all the reviews are raving about. The housing is small and sleek and the screen is sharp and definitely large enough. I was able to pair my Treo 650 (cingular) with some difficulty but now it is working great and you can dial any POI on the Nuvi while the devices are paired. The suction disk seems surprisingly secure and  I would refrain from buying another holder until you see how nicely it mounts with the included bracket. This devise makes the magellan look like a 1974 paper atlas and the software (and general coolness factor) simply are better than the Tomtom. I am really happy I spent the extra bucks for a Garmin.Addendum: Feb 4, 2007; Still love this device.  Some have commented that the volume was too low when used as a bluetooth. I just upgraded the firmware to version 3.4 off the Garmin website. This markedly increases the volume of the bluetooth/phone feature of the device which is helpful. It will not increase the volume of the GPS voice when recieving directions. The upgrade is very easy- just follow the instructions on the website.

This carrier is amazing! Not only can it carry a cake or cupcakes (with enough clearance for both cake and cupcakes to have taller decorations), the lid is also collapsible so that it doesn't take up as much room when stored. The stand for the cupcakes can slide when the lid isn't on but once the lid is snapped on, the cupcakes won't be going anywhere.Even though I gave this 5 stars, there are 2 things I don't like about this carrier. First, you should almost NEVER use the handle of this carrier. If you aren't carrying many cupcakes or your cake isn't very heavy it's not a big deal but if you look at the handle, you can tell that it can easily break or come out of the lid. Second, you should always make sure the lid is snapped in place before picking it up, even if you just set it down momentarily. Once I was transporting a small cake to a cake decorating class and was in a hurry. I set the carrier down on the ground then grabbed it by the handle on my way out the door only to find that one of the snaps must have gotten loose when I set it down and my cake slid sideways. Luckily it was a small cake and none of the sides touched the lid and was damaged. However, I found out that when got to class, my top layer of cake had shifted a little to the right so now I had a slightly lopsided cake. We've cut out sweets and carbs from our diet. Jerky has become one of my staple snacks. Store bought jerky is expensive and often loaded with all sorts of stuff. So I did some research and selected this dehydrator.We've made a few batches of jerky so far. One was a couple of pounds of lean ground chuck doctored with some cure and seasoning I found on my own. Following the directions, I mixed the meat with the fixings, loaded the jerky gun and plugged it in. About every half hour or so, I checked and patted down the strips with a paper towel as recommended. Good idea. About 4 1/2 hours later, I had some good jerky.My local supermarket had a deal on shoulder so I bought a bunch. Sliced it, marinated it overnight, and set it in the dehydrator. Again, I checked on it about every half hour but that was pretty much being a mother hen. I had some great jerky in about 5 hours. I layered it in paper towels and let any oils absorb for an hour and then packed it. I had a lot, so I vacuum sealed a bunch and have it in the freezer.My wife is planning on drying out some fruit and herbs this weekend.It's easy to use, pretty easy to clean, and - when you wrap the cord around the top - fairly easy to store.Looking forward to getting a lot of use from this appliance.

Like the previous Motorola phones that I've had, this one has the easy intutive system for navigating its numerous menus --- I'm sold on using Motorola for this reason,  compared to the Noxxxx, Motorola is great!PROS:Almost everythingAwesome designGSMI found the volume to be excellent compared to my last phone (motorola v60).Good antenna, extremely low rate of dropped signals --- also, since the antenna does not protrude, I anticipate it will not require replacement (I had to replace the antenna twice on my old v60 --- broke off from carrying in pocket and/or dropping)Excellent color screen.Good camera for a phone.Great size, easily fits in a shirt pocket.Great battery life (with Bluetooth off)Easily customizable but items that I never use (media mall, media net, etc.) remain in a prominent place and can not be put in some obscure location.CONS:The most significant negative feature is the screen --- it is extremely hard to see in sunlight.Display on front (when closed) is hard to read.Price (if you are upgrading your phone with Cingular --- they wanted $199) However, since I was changing from AT&T; to Cingular I was able to buy the phone at BestBuy for $70.The manual that comes with it should be better writen.Screen gets oily from normal use and must be wiped off frequently.Alarm Clock interface could be better.Date Book Alert is not very loud.Accessory support at Cingular Stores rather poor --- two Cingular Stores did not have the USB cable (had to buy one at Wal-Mart).Of the four or five phones that I have owned, this one is by far the best in all respects.  Even with these cons it is still eons ahead of other phones.EDIT: Change from 5 stars to 1 star...  I loved the features on the phone and the flip lid was great.  PROBLEM:  After about 3 months the screen would not light up --- took the phone back to AT&T; and they sent me a reconditioned phone which lasted about a month --- took that one back, they sent another reconditioned phone,  Long story > short:  I had 5 phones in one year, the 5th phone finally worked and kept working for a couple of years.  Because of my experience with this phone, I will not buy another Motorola.

I researched disk cleaners and repair thoroughly and compared 3 units head to head -- Skip Dr., Aleratec and Maxell DSR-1. Aleratec, and Skip Dr in particular, were total junk by comparison. Skip Dr. is poorly constructed and they don't have a combo unit so you need to buy a repair unit and a cleaning unit separately. The repair unit severely scratched my test disk, creating huge starburst type scratches from the center to the edges of the entire surface of the disk. Plus, their repair unit doesn't even buff. You have to do that manually with a cloth!! So I'm supposed to strain and waste my time buffing out all those pretty scratches their unit created with a cloth? Doesn't that defeat the purpose of an "automated" product?? And the buffing cloth was useless anyway cuz the scratches the unit created were far too deep. Not to mention, if you use this unit on a rental disk, the video store will probably charge you for mangling their disk. Their cleaning unit is equally a piece of junk. It's supposed to eject the disk after cleaning but it doesn't. The disk just keeps spinning and spinning forever. Both Skip Dr. units run on batteries, so be prepared to spend extra money on that too. And you know as batteries die down, the cleaning and repair units will be less and less effective, so you'll have to replace batteries often. Don't bother with Skip Dr. unless you want to destroy disks, waste time, waste money on batteries and waste space on 2 inadequate units. Buy a Maxell DSR-1 instead. See my review for the Maxell for more details on how it works.

Pros: simplicity, build quality, reliability, flexibility, PandoraCons: still expensive, Pandora extra costI have been searching for a wireless audio system for my home for years. There have been virtually no choices at the $1000 level. The Sonos founders must have noticed. I am amazed that they got everything right the first time around. I ordered the BU130 bundle plus the Sonos speakers. The setup was simple. Connected one of the two units (either one will do) to my router (using wired connection). The router recognized it immediately and I was in business.The bundle comes with the 100 (amplifier included) and the 80 (no amplifier). The speakers turned out to be weak - would not get those again. If I had a choice, I would not get the 100 again because I don't need the amplifier. But, the package includes it and the package is cheaper than buying two 80s and a controller. You might find the four 10/100 etherports on the 100 useful (I didn't).I connected my media system to the 80's line input and I now can pipe that sound all over the house (no wires).  I just bought another bundle to add more rooms.This system is just crazy rich. Besides the obvious flexibility of line-in from any of the units, Sonos includes 30-day trials of Pandora and Rhapsody. If you've ever tried Rhapsody, you may find it hard to go back to radio (broadcast or Internet). Unfortunately, you'll have to pay for it after the 30 day trial. Pandora is basically like satellite radio. I'm not hooked, yet. Sonos also delivers a broad selection of Internet radio genres. All of this is selected from the fantastic remote. Be sure to spend the extra $40 for the cradle.One last comment. When I first set the system up, I was concerned that the range would be limited. I've had it all over my house, in every room and closet and upstairs far, from the server. No problem.

Let me start off saying that for price on Amazon, the Klipsch headphones are a great buy. I have owned them for about 6 months before they started shorting out. Im not sure if it was the headphones themselves but I tend to run through earbuds rather quickly. That let start the direct comparison.Comfort:I would have to give a slight edge to the Klipsch headphones over the Shures here. The Klipsch soft ear sleeves just slide in but there is a pro and con to everything. The Shure's use a foam tip that expands in your ear. It creates a much better seal and therefore has better noise-isolation then the S4's. My ear sometimes feels a little discomfort after wearing them for a while but i may just need to switch the smaller sleeves they provide.Secure Fit:The SE215 has the clear advantage hear. The headphones are tricky to learn to get in but once you do the very nature of how the sit in the ear makes them much more secure. The headphone cord comes forward then loops over and behind the ear. Tugging on the cord rarely budges these things. Meanwhile, I have had problems with the S4's coming out, especially if I'm working out.Ease of use:The S4's have the edge here. You simply push them in. The SE215's require to roll the foam tips before you put them in to get the best seal and to loop the cord over your ear. that can be annoying if they fall out while running. That said they much less likely to fall out. The S4's have alternate of putting them in that involves looping the cord as well but i found it to be uncomfortable.Noise-isolation:Advantage goes the SE215s for the reasons mentioned before, the foam tips.Construction:Both seem well constructed. The SE215 feels more substantial and the cord is much thicker. It seems less prone to tangling.Performance:As i wrote this I listened to the same song once with each pair with the same EQ and volume settings. The SE215 were louder and sounded cleaner for the same songs and settings. The bass was deep and the highs were clear. The S4's are no slouch either. They outperform most other sub-$100 earbuds out there.Bottom line:Both are a great set of the headphones. The SE215's are $100 and the S4's are $60. Do the Shure's justify the extra $40? yes. but its all personal preference. The S4's are a steal for $60. That said I really appreciate the superior performance, fit, and noise-isolation.

The point of a case is to protect your phone.  This case does not do that well.  I had been using it for a couple of weeks and it worked fine.  I didn't like that I could not dock it with the case on but that is just nit picky.One day I was sitting on a curb waiting for a taxi to take me to the football game when my phone slid off my lap.  It fell a total of 6-12 inches max.  It landed on the bottom corner and the case just split open.  Either side of it fell off and my phone then landed on the front glass CRACKING THE SCREEN!  After much reading I found that these screens are pretty durable so I assume it was a fluke and must have hit a small pebble just right.  Either way if the case would have done its job right and not fell off my phone would not now be cracked.Luckily the phone works fine just has a large crack which is more of an annoyance and weak spot if any.  Thanks to Apple and AT&T; to fix this costs ~$200 so that will not be happening.  I ended up getting the griffin easy dock case and it works great and is much more of a sturdy design.

The Bodum stainless steel thermal press makes rich coffee.  As others have noted, those "12 cups" are 4-ounce cups, a measuring cup is 8 and typical drip coffee pots count 6 ounces as a cup.  The box says it is 50 ounces capacity and the directions say to leave at least an inch at the top.  We used 48 ounces of hot water and still had an inch clear at the top.We buy dark coffee beans to begin with, and using the recommended 1 rounded table spoon of coarsely ground coffee beans per 4 ounces delivers a very dark, rich, yet smooth and not bitter coffee.  This is not the kind of coffee one would drink quickly ;-)  The directions say that a finer grind will tend to clog the filter (metal screen like some drip coffeemaker filters), we have not tried that yet.The pot appears to be well-made, solid yet not too heavy.  The lid is fairly thin sheet metal with a plastic rim inside for pouring (or not).

I picked this up after seeing videos on how much speed this can add to just about any system.  My macbook A1342 is the best computer I've owned but it is aging.  Rather than go out and get a replacement, I started looking at upgrade options that I could perform myself (besides just ram of which I have 8GB currently.)Using this SSD as my primary drive has brought this macbook to "better than new" status.  It boots in about a fifth of the time it used to, programs load nearly instantaneously. Even brand new, this was not the case. I use the most recent photoshop (CS6) and I used to get stuck with that spinning color wheel for up to two minutes while it loaded.  Not only do I no longer see the color wheel, I can start using CS6 almost immediately after it opens (which takes about 2 seconds.)Here's what I did:I installed my old HDD in a drive caddy and replaced my optical drive.  At the same time, I installed the new SSD in the HDD's old tray.I booted my mac from the old hdd and then used Carbon Copy to clone the HDD to the SDD.  That part took about two hours.I then changed the primary boot drive to the SSD in the macbook's settings and rebooted it.DONE!There are lots of vids on line on how to do this but there is one thing that I ran into that I didn't expect.  The SSD seemed to be running very warm/hot.  Make sure your reset the mac's SMC when you do the drive swap.  It's simple and details can be found on Apple's website and eslewhere.Bottom line: do this upgrade!  I don't think I will ever go back to using a standard HDD at this point.  I've been spoiled!
Took to camping trip. Was able to charge my Samsung S3 quickly.Minus one star: Tried to use it to charge ipad, didn't work.

I'm a bread baking enthusiast a yr now and just used this item for the first time and am satisfied, but I still have many questions to find answers for.  Like knowing how to bake other bread recipes that make NO mention of La Cloche. Do I use the same cooking temp and time or not?  Also, many reviews state using this in a cold oven, others preheat this in the oven, others presoak it and others do not.  The directions say nothing about putting this in a preheated oven. It only says to preseason it and then wash it without soap. Period.  I used it for the first time by preheating the top and bottom of La Cloche in the oven only b/c the recipe I used from Breadtopia.com had a Sicilian bread recipe (no-knead) and that's what was directed.  But in general, I still have more questions than answers as to whether or not there is one best way to use this. I have another bread book that states she preheats the bottom of La Cloche and presoaks the lid 15 to 30 mins while oven is preheating and uses a 450 temp.  My first La Cloche Sicilian bread came out beautiful and dark and crusty, but no matter which method you use, with or w/o La Cloche, while sitting out to cool, the crust loses some crunch. I find to restore a bread's crunchy crust (and even popovers), I put it in a hot oven for a few minutes b/4 eating it, and it is crunchy again.

01/20/2014 Update This weekend my daughter asked me for a HDMI Cable. I asked why and she said that she wanted to play some web content on our 55" Widescreen T.V. She hooked it up and the movies played and looked georgous. I'm going to upgrade to 6 stars.+++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++I thoroughly researched Chromebooks before making this purchase but was skeptical of the rave reviews. Seeing is believing.You can go to the HP Spec Page and read all the specs, so my review is about my impressions.I bought this as a Christmas gift for my daughter and I'm absolutely blown away. I just set it up for her, so on Christmas Morning she'll open it up and be up and running. This was the quickest easiest setup ever. The new Logitech Wireless MouseLogitech Wireless Mouse M325 with Designed-for-Web Scrolling - White Paisleywas instantly recognized.The 64gb Sdxram card B00E83XHXM instantly recognized but would glitch when I tried to name a file. Simply reformatted on chrome machine and the problem went away.Speed:Every low end windows laptop that I've ever tried has been laggy.Not the HP 14 Chromebook, the Intel CPU and the 4gb Ram make the difference. This Laptop is zippy. I tested out Netflix and Hulu and they both ran flawlessly. Had plenty of tabs open without performance degradationErgonomics:The feel. Everything is rubber coated including the keys, even though this Laptop is low price it doesn't feel like it. The Keyboard just felt nice. The Trackpad is amazing, once you learn the Chrome Commands. 2 finger scrolls up and down. Hold 2 fingers down for right click. It is so darned intelligent. The Pearl color is elegant and the laptop looks far more expensive then it's price. I asked a coworker to play with it and she concurred. This definitely doesn't look or feel cheap.Monitor, while not stellar is certainly adequate, I did not feel like I was looking at a low resolution monitor. It was regular. Speakers yes Tinny but hey what do you expect for$340.00Neutral: Having a 14" Monitor and all the extras like Intel CPU, HDMI Port, USB Ports, SD Card Port and solid construction adds weight. For example the hinge for the screen is one piece going along 3/4 of the width of the screen, if it was not manufactured with weight in mind then they could have made it flimsier and lighter. More components, quality construction will add extra weight. So it is 4 pounds, plenty light for a teenager or me, If you are not looking for a full blown system and desire more of a tablet like device, then this might not be for you. I'm not taking off a star because realistically life is a series of tradeoffs.Turns out that Microsoft's scorch campaign about the Laptop being a brick when not online is BXXXXshxxxtGoogle office runs, the native movie and music player runs, and with a 64gb Sd Ram card with 9 hours of life you can watch movies, listen to music, take notes, write a paper and make a spread sheet. All the basics. OFFLINEThe equivalent performance in a Windows Machine would cost me at least double and Apple Triple.If I was Microsoft I'd be quaking in my boots.

This pan is well worth the price that I paid for it. Cooks as expected and easy to clean.But, I had to give it only four stars because the non-stick surface is starting to lift off a bit after only eight months of gentle 2-3 times a week use. I had expected a longer life for the "Triple-coated" nonstick surface.UPDATE:  December 17, 2009...  ONE STAR for this cheap junk.Within a few weeks after I wrote the first review the non-stick surface of the pan was peeling so badly that my wife stopped using it and hid it in the back of the cabinet. I found it there this November, and remembering that it had a "Lifetime Warranty" I shipped it back, via UPS per their instructions, at a cost that equaled 1/3 of what I paid for the pan.Calphalon received my pan, which had been manufactured in Toledo, Ohio, on November 13, and today, December 17, I received a replacement pan back. Big problem though. The replacement pan I received back weighs about 30% less than the one I sent in, and the quality of workmanship is less than half the quality of the pan that I sent to them. So, the pan I received back is only worth about half the value of the pan that I sent in.It was no big surprise to me to read on the carton that the new pan came in, "Made In China." Do not buy the "new" Calphalon pots and pans because you will NOT get what you pay for.

We've had this on an ipod for the last 1.5 years and we love it! THe only complaint is that it's a little hard to get off (as it should be) and that once the screen protector gets dirty it's hard to use the ipod and a little difficult to clean. Worth what you pay for it though.

First off, I've owned/installed literally hundreds of screen protectors(SP) so I'm not a newbie at installing them.  Installation was quick and easy.  This is my 5th glass screen protector but first privacy-glass SP; that’s what led me to buy it.  I've tried two of the major name brands' SP's on Amazon and although they are nice, you can't beat the oleophobic feel of Amzdeal's.  I'm not really a fan of matte finish SP's cause my fingers tend to be dry so the matte finish feels weird.  The previous matte privacy SP's I had only lasted like 2 months cause of the feel-then they were yanked off for a clear oleophobic SP.  Keep in mind they are all thick so you might as well go with a glass privacy SP.Back to Amzdeal's SP, this is a 2-WAY PRIVACY SCREEN PROTECTOR, don't get it confused with Tech Armor's 4-way SP.  If you're viewing your iPhone normally, people to the left and right of you can't really make out what your viewing.  It's looks blurry to them.  But if someone is viewing your phone from the top or bottom, it's pretty clear.  This is truly oleophobic-it's very slick to the touch and almost no fingerprints stay on the phone.  I haven't tried a scratch test cause I feel it may damage the phone but it feels pretty solid.  Normal "flexible" privacy screen protectors tend to be thick anyways so the thickness of this one doesn't bother me.  I'm using it with a Otterbox Symmetry case - no problems.The only "con" which isn't really a con because it's expected from privacy sp's is the brightness problem.  You do have to turn your brightness up but as a person which normally keeps it low, this doesn't really bother me.  Also, Amzdeal should advise the buyer that the adhesive sort of has to dry.  Similar to wet screen protectors, the adhesive has that hazy look when first installed but it went away a couple hours later.If you want a privacy SP, and are undecided whether to try Spigens glass $40 SP or Amzdeal's <$10 glass SP, hit that buy button and be happy!  Don't go for a expensive privacy sp just cause the name brand makes good cases, Amzdeal's products are legit and I'm very happy with my purchase!

I have an ipod touch(1st generation) and waited for close to a year for  Apple to update it to work with flash.  No luck, so I decided to look around for another portable media player that could play flash, LIVE tv and had a camera.  The 1st player I considered was the Archos5, it had everything I was looking for except it did not come with a camera and I was not sure it could play live tv, PLUS it is impossible to buy from a non-online store.  I also considered the Nokia N810 Portable Internet Tablet it comes with an attatched keyboard  BUT its not available in stores only online.(UPDATE after 1st away from home trip:  Took both to use in the waiting room at the hospital with free Wi-Fi connection.  Both have different positives and negatives.  I was able to take pictures with my netbook camera BUt both the ipod touch and netbook were blocked from accessing certain websites.  I wanted to listen to sirius radio but couldnt on both devices.  THe netbook is a definite NO NO for car use(if you are driving).  The ipod touch is a little easier to use in the car BUT the best device to use in the car is my ipod nano.  I am still satisfied with my purchase of the netbook BUT I'm still waiting to see if Apple adds a camera to the ipod touch or Microsoft adds a camera to the Zune.)I have come to the conclusion that you cant compare the ipod touch and the netbook because they are both useful for different situations.LAST UPDATE: the battery life became a little bit of a concern so I bought the Tekkeon MP3450 myPower ALL Rechargeable Battery.  I can use the netbook for 2 hours normally, plug up the Takkeon MP3450 rechargeable battery and get another 3 hours of use, Once the rechargeable battery runs out I get another 2 hours of use from my normal battery because the Rechargeable battery recharged my netbook.  For a total of 7 hours of continuous use.

Have you noticed? Most plug-in electronics and wall power supplies these days work with power voltages and frequencies around the world, e.g. 100V to 250V, 50Hz & 60Hz - but not DVD and BluRay players. That's probably because the discs and their players are regional, and US discs won't work in Europe and Asia and vice versa. The manufacturers might save a few pennies by making power supplies that work on only one voltage range.If you want to go overseas and watch US discs, you generally need to bring your own player. If you do that, you need a power converter such as this, for the player, even if all your other electronics plug right in.This converter is about as small and light as you'll find while being suitable for this purpose. It uses a transformer, which is what you want, not an electronic converter, which could create waveforms that might damage your player.And yes, I've taken this overseas with a BluRay player and everything worked fine.

Everyone has such different expectations for earphones that I never know whether I'm going to like them until I'm actually wearing them.  I used these earphones with mySandisk Sansa Clip 4 GB MP3 Player (Black).I usually find earbuds to be uncomfortable and find that they have trouble staying in my ears.  These came with 3 different sizes of tips, so I was able to get a pretty good fit.  I LOVED the cords, which appear to be covered in a "fabric"-type material, instead of the traditional rubber/plastic coating.  I felt this will hold up better.  Sound clarity is fine for my use--these replaced the factory-issue earphones that came with my little mp3 player, I've never owned truly high-quality earphones, and I don't listen to anything where sound quality is of the utmost importance.The little case the earphones come with is nice, and actually holds my Sansa Clip as well.  These came packaged in the Amazon "Frustration-Free" packaging, which is nice for personal use, but I do not feel it would make them good as a gift.  (It looks like you opened them and re-wrapped them or something...spring for the "real" package if you think the recipient might care.)My only qualms with this product are the little covers for the earphones (what do I do if I accidentally lose the ones that fit me best) and the way the cords seem to sometimes rub against my ears (I know, just keep still...but tough to do at the gym!).

I received this product from UNU as a FREE SAMPLE as part of their UTRY Reviewer Program. I currently own two of their other charging packs, as well as charging packs from other companies, but this is my first wireless charging pad product to review. I like how the case comes apart in the faceplate. It's very easy to remove and replace when needed. The mat is awesome. I've never wanted to get a wireless charger mat before because it would just make the phone bulky and would only benefit when I wasn't using the phone, namely when it was charging. SInce this is also a built in battery pack, and slimmer than some other battery packs that don't have have the wireless charging function, it's a no brainer as to why it's worth it. It's nice at night when in bed to not have to reconnect and disconnect the lighting cable everytime I pick up the phone. just set it on the mat and it's good to go. While traveling, I love to have the extra juice to keep my phone going without having to plug it in. The only downsides I've noticed were getting used to the shape of the case was kind of awkward to hold at first, but once I got used to it it wasn't a big deal. Also while charging, the phone and pack tend to heat up a little bit and won't cooldown until I've turned off the charging function. All in all I'd say it's a step above a simple battery pack. The price is comparable to similar battery pack cases with similar mAh settings and those don't even have the charging function. I just wish I had a charging plate at work also!

I bought this TV to put next to my computer.  It replaced an old 13" CRT TV that was on its last legs.Overall, I'm pleased with it.  It's light, nice-looking, and easy to set up.  The default settings work well, and if you want to change them, it's pretty easy to figure out how.  There's a "game" button that lets you switch to other inputs - not just game systems, but a VCR, DVR, camcorder, etc. - whatever you want to hook up.  The built-in DVD player works well and is easy to use.With flatscreen prices dropping every day, the cost might be a bit high.  But the main drawback is the slowness mentioned in other reviews.  It is very slow to turn on, and very slow when you change channels.  I can't blame it on my cable box, because I don't use one.  (I plug the coax cable directly into the TV.)  I can live with it, but you're the type who loves to channel-surf, the slowness will drive you bonkers.

OK, I'm running an Apple shop: 2 iPads, 2 iPhones, a Macbook Pro and a Mac Mini. I only have one Windows box left but it still works fines, so it's safe for now. When my then girlfriend (now my wife) and I got this Linksys router, we only had two devices using it on the networks, a Tivo and a Laptop. After being married for a year, now we have 10 devices :0 )It was time to get a new router, because it started dropping in and out, here and there. I found this router used on Amazon, the latest one, for only 70 bucks in "like new" condition. The only problem I had setting it up was the fact that I didn't reset my cable modem long enough to assign it a new IP address, so don't make this mistake. Now that it's up,  it's great. I know that because I can stream HDX movies from VUDU, something I couldn't do on my Linksys, as HDX is bluray quality and requires a faster bandwidth connection. My internet connection could handle it but the Linksys could not.  My advice, always go Apple. I never owned an Apple product that stopped workings...seriously...I have five and six year old iPods that still work like a champ!My printers are both already wirelessly networked but I did put my 1.5 terabyte drive on the AEBS. I also formatted it first, so that it would support Apple's Extended-Journalling file system. Then I put my backed up files back on it. I'm preparing myself for an all Mac shop, once the Windows box is gone.

i like the 6 mdh.  it's very well made, doesn't scream "camera gear!" and breaks in pretty nicely.  you can customize the compartments inside the bag to suit your needs.  for many people this will be an excellent camera bag.  now, the reasons i've docked it two stars:first, two construction issues that are annoying:  the pockets in the front of the bag are kind of worthless.  you can throw a few extra CF cards, a cord, maybe a pen in there, but when your bag is filled with gear the front pockets are compressed so much you can barely access it.  i realize the design cuts down on bulk as you don't have a big pocket hanging out there, but it's not terribly useful as a result.  second, the strap does not have clips where it joins the bag that allow it to rotate.  in other words, every time i pick this bag up the strap is twisted and i have to untwist it before putting it over my shoulder.  this is annoying.those problems i can live with.  this problem, not so much:  the padding on the bottom of the bag, after about 8 months of use in my case, is pretty compressed, thin and, quite frankly, insufficient if you have a long lens mounted to your body.  if your bag is full, meaning that your lens goes all the way to the bottom and your camera is snugly held in place when you close the flap on top, there is very little room for the bag to give in order to absorb impact, and the padding on the bottom is not good enough to handle the job.  i learned this the hard way.  now i keep cushioned material on the bottom of the bag to avoid further problems, but to be honest it's kind of destroyed my faith in the bag.  if you can live with this (or if you shoot mostly primes or shorter lenses... or if you don't mind taking longer lenses off your body before putting everything in the bag) then this bag may be for you.  but i'd have a tough time recommending it simply because of this issue alone.this would be a pretty easy fix for crumpler - replace the padding on the bottom with a material that resists compression over time and is better suited to absorb impact.  i'm not saying you should be able to drop this thing off the roof of your house.  but your gear should be safe from the bump and grind of every day carry.

The Plantronics Voyager 510 is a great headset for the right situation.  I've owned a 510 for three years, this latest one to replace the last one the dog chewed up (literally).The 510 is a comfortable headset with good battery life.  The quality of the audio I hear is very good, and the quality of the audio my callers hear is also very good.  However, the unit just returned would continuously send a low level static to callers, which wasn't the case with my prior 510.  I assume this was just a defective unit.The big strength of this headset is dealing with noise.  In comparison to the other two headsets I gave serious consideration, the Motorola H710 and the Jabra BT8040, the 510 had the best audio in all conditions.  One test was driving down the highway with all 4 windows down, the radio on at a moderate volume and the headset in the right ear.  Cabin noise was so loud that I felt the need to raise my voice level to hear myself.  I was able to hear callers over the noise just barely, and they could hear me, but did hear a lot of background noise as one would expect.  With the H710, I could hear callers well, but I was completely unintelligible to them.  The BT8040 was almost as good as the 510; callers heard me almost as well as with the 510, but I had trouble hearing callers because the audio was cutting in and out, as if echo cancellation was getting confused by all the noise.  All three worked quite well in quieter environments.  The H710 fails to cancel echo (of my voice back to me) when paired with a Uniden cordless land-line phone (with BT support).  The BT8040 fails to pair with the Uniden cordless phone at all.I ultimately kept the BT8040 and returned the 510 because of some shortcomings of the 510.  First, it is tricky to get on and off.  It took me a long time to figure out how to put it on using one hand, and it takes some fumbling.  It's far easier and faster to use 2 hands to put it on, but this is a hassle, and frankly looks stupid when you're doing it.  I don't like wearing it all the time because it is big and the darn blue light flashes all the time, grabbing the attention of others around you.  I feel like I'm on exhibit when I try to wear the 510 around others.  Finally, the 510 is not a good shape for keeping it in your pocket, and it's large enough that the phone and the headset in the same pocket isn't usually workable.  Which brings us back to the dog and the chewed up headset.  Because of its poor pocketability, I would tend to leave it on the desk or table when not in use.  Doggie finally figured this out.For use exclusively as a car or office headset, I'd pick the 510 over all three.  But for versatility in a wider range of uses, the BT8040 works best for me.

After a lot of research, I got one of these for my wife and immediately ordered an additional one after the first one arrived. Good stuff disappears fast after word gets out, even if it is from a company nobody ever heard of. I think the sound is excellent for speakers of this size, especially if you try a few different equalizer pre-sets in your music player.  We want to leave the unit plugged in most of the time, but I had a question about damaging the battery by doing this- simultaneous charge and discharge (while playing), which is discouraged for many devices. I e-mailed the manufacturer and they quickly responded assuring me that this device is designed to do this without battery damage.PROSI think the sound compares somewhat favorably to the comparable Bose product with a variety of music. At 1/5 the price!!Simple- 4 switches right on the top- power, volume up and down, and bluetooth. None on the back.Perfect fit and finish with elegant piano black finish, well protected with release plastic all around.Class D amplifier with 5W per channel (my Sony boombox has 3W per channel).Auxiliary input as well as bluetooth. It's on the back where it belongs, and comes with a cable.Very tight housing for buzz-free operation and acoustic resonance.Loud, no matter what any other reviewer is saying (unless someone got a bad one).Power switch blinks when a charge is needed, and also while charging, then goes out when fully charged. No glaring front panel spotlights.Nice smooth look, no pseudo-techie heat sink look. No klugey handle when you don't really need one- use 2 hands.Responsive and courteous tech support by emailFull year warranty.CONS (minor)Speaker cover is fabric. I prefer metal grille for slightly better acoustic transparency, and so I can SEE the drivers.I cannot see any way to get this apart to replace the battery when/if needed, but it will probably run plugged in with a depleted battery anyway.I cannot vouch for battery play duration, but other reviewers seem happy. I plan to leave it plugged in a lot, so we will see if the manufacturer's claim of no negative impact to the battery is accurate.I have to give this 5 stars for the sound, the overall design, and the price. If I encounter quality/reliability issues, I'll come back and update this.

So I must admit, that I actually don't use this adapter any more. I discovered Bitfenix's products, and they were too beautiful to miss (they use paracord sleeving)! But that doesn't mean that the NZXT cables are not totally great! Apart from one caveat I will address below, they suited my needs perfectly!BUILD/QUALITYThe adapters are well sleeved, with not a great deal of extra sleeving (they are pretty tight). The heatshrink was cut very evenly, something which seems obvious but I have seen other companies skip over entirely! The plastic used for the connectors does not seem flimsy in the slightest. The ONLY issue that I have to mention is the quality of the sleeving. I have been sleeving cables for many years, and not just in computers. I have come across all kinds of sleeving and I have to say that the sleeving used here is really mediocre. It doesn't look bad (especially with the white cables they use underneath) but it has absolutely NO heat resistance. I re-sleeved two of the wires on my CPU cable in a grey from Bitspower to go with the MDPC look, and when using my HEAT-SHRINK GUN (specifically designed for this application), the NZXT sleeving actually melted. The bitspower sleeving that I was aiming it was totally unscathed. I decided to not proceed with sleeving this cable and the VGA PCIE power cable...Oh well, lesson learned.AESTHETICSApart from the one point I made above, I have no complaints at all! The sleeving looks great, the shrink is done well and the wire used is malleable enough to put it where you want without being too flimsy!WHY 4 STARS?- Looks wonderful (++)- Mostly a quality product (++)- Sleeving could be betterOVERALLI really enjoyed this cable (and the others from the same line), however I would have paid an extra $2-3 for an adapter with higher-quality sleeving. Not at all bad, but I would suggest a quick search for Bitfenix on FrozenCPU!

This is a great device for adding an AUX input to an older car stereo.There are a lot of devices on the market that will modulate your iPod, MP3 player, etc., into an FM signal you can hear on your radio. Unfortunately, the transmitter output of such devices can't compete with the stronger signals from commercial radio stations.  This device also transmits on FM, but it electronically cuts off your car's FM antenna, so the only signal going into the radio is your AUX source. There's still a bit of hiss in the signal, but it's certainly tolerable.A person of average mechanical skills can install this device in about 45 minutes.

At my office we all (no matter what carrier) get about 1-2 bars of 1X/Edge. We decided to try this out. We ordered it, took it up to the roof (about 25ft up), found the latitude and longitude of the nearest Verizon towers, and pointed our GPS and compass at them. We directed the antenna towards the towers. No change whatsoever in the signal strength on Verizon but a noticeable increase for some other services. Called tech support and that didn't help, but tech support verified that we set it up correctly.I would love to give this higher stars, but I can't justify doing so since it doesn't work here with one of the most predominant carriers in the United States.

There are some things that I like about this GPS and there are some things I don't like. We'll go through the pros first. The design is great, the mount to the window is very easy to use, and the screen is very bright and vivid. The turn by turn directions are great and the voice is easy to hear and understand. When it gets to two miles prior on any highway, you get the next directions spoken to you, which definitely is a plus on long and winding road trips. The main con I have with the product is how annoying it is to find destinations. In this reviewer's opinion, you shouldn't have to change the location to be able to search destinations, which unfortunately is what needs to be done with this machine unless I am missing some magic secret. I tried getting directions to a well known theme park an hour away from my home and it was unable to find it in the destination folder because it was outside the local area that was pre-selected. This problem aside, this is a solid GPS that is worth the reasonable price Amazon is currently asking for it.

No glue, no residue, perfect fit, no interference with touch screen feel or function. Follow the directions to the letter and you won't even know it is there. As a helpful hint, dust is your enemy here. If you find a bubble you cannot work out, look closely and you'll likely see a spec of dust smack in the middle. Get some Scotch or other quality brand regular tape and shipping tape. Apply a piece of the shipping tape to the screen guard over the affected area, leaving a bit to use as a tab. Use the tab to pull up the affected section of the guard and use a piece of the regular tape to dab the screen and back of the guard in the affected area. Reseal the guard to the to screen and work out any air bubbles per the instructions. Take your time working out bubbles slowly to the edges of the screen. Most if not all bubbles can be avoided by slow careful guard application per instructions. I also used this brand on my previous phone and it kept on looking like it came with the phone. Update adding last ditch effort to rid bubbles and dust and save the screen protector. Apply "quality 3M" shipping tape to the screen side of the protector and the phone screen. Use two strips each to completely cover the area of both and long enough to extend about an inch beyond the top edges. Now fold over that extra inch so you can join them and begin to peel back the tape from both surfaces so you can line up the protector to the top of your phone screen. Now press down gently to apply the screen protector and "very slowly" pull the tape back on both surfaces and press the protector down following the tape. Do this slowly pressing out bubbles ahead of the protector and behind the tape. The result is a bubble and dust free surface. Using the quality 3M tape assures no tape residue will be left on the surfaces.

This is OK for desktop use. This said, it is NOT good for lap use. It actually makes lap use with the keyboard darn near impossible to use.I bought a pointer to use with this as well. When you have the iPad sitting in the case, the iPad is unstable for pointer use in this case. Big disappointment.The "On/Off" button is very cheap feeling. Not impressed and can see it failing pretty quickly. Also it's kind of difficult to activate unless you have fingernails. As a guy I don't typically have very long ones, so it's a bit hard to activate.The little leather holder for the pointer doesn't afford enough room for the iPad to sit properly with pointer in place unless you are VERY careful on where to put it.The "protection" this affords you is minimal.Finally one of the worst (and thus far most annoying) thing about this keyboard is that when your iPad is turned off a few minutes and the keyboard is not turned off as well, the iPad does not reconnect until the keyboard power is cycled. I unfortunately don't know if this iPad or keyboard specific, but if I'm placing blame I'd probably go with the Keyboard.
