The Instapark Solar Charger came to me all folded up in a box.  My very first impression is that it looked like a case for a Kindle Fire.  Open it up and it has three panels.  On the back is a pocket which stores two USB ports with enough room to keep a phone or other small device while charging.The first thing I tried to charge was my Kindle 3G . . . inside the car in the front seat on a sunny day.  I know instructions say direct sunlight, but I didn't want something screaming "electronic device" staring out the front window of my car.  That didn't work.Next, I tried putting it in the window of my home office.  This is a south facing window which gets very bright, though not direct, sunlight.  On this morning, it happened to be overcast.  I tried charging my children's spy watch.  No go.The sun came out and I decided I needed to go all out and put it in direct sunlight with the spy watch.  Voila!  A very quick charge.  It works!Now, since it was so bright outside, I tried charging both my Kindle 3G and myIncredicharge I-5000 External Battery Packthrough the front office window at the same time.  The Incredicharge charged extremely slowly.  The Kindle 3G still would not charge.Again, I brought the Instapark outside to charge the Incredicharge Battery in direct sunlight.  Whoa!  Another quick charge.One more time.  This time, I attempt to charge my Kindle 3G in direct sunlight.  It was outside all day.  I'd come out to check it every now and then.  Yes, it did charge, a little, very slowly.  After a full day, my Kindle 3G went from critically low battery levels to half charged.So, this Instapark Solar Charger does work very well for some devices.  It really does need direct sunlight.  It doesn't work so well in shaded or cloudy conditions.  I think I need something with a little more oomph to charge my Kindle 3G.  I like the portability, the pocket for storing small devices while charging, the "green" aspect, and the aesthetically appealing case.  It's a good idea for camping, but I would recommend trying it out before leaving.This product was provided for review by Instapark.

While the screen protector is nice once you can finally get it on, the application is a pain. I've used many different brands of screen protectors, wet and dry application, and this had to have been my most annoying experience to date. The screen protector is cut way too close to the edge of the screen, so the smallest mistake in lining it up results in it lifting at one corner or another, resulting in repeated lifting and reapplying. I went through all three before finally getting it perfect and without bubbles, which is something I can usually do first try, without much effort. This was a frustrating experience, and I will most likely not repurchase when I need to replace the one I have on.

This is the 3rd steam cleaner that I have owned.  The first was the best (Euro-Pro) but finally quit after many years of great service and it is no longer available.  Next I got the McCullough ... it was OK, but seemed rather cumbersome to use and move around.  Based on the reviews here I wanted to try the Sienna Eco .... big mistake!  First of all the trigger that releases (and stops) the steam sticks.  I have contacted customer service at Sienna and am arguing with them ... they tried to tell me this was normal!  I don't think so.  I have to tug at the trigger to get it to stop.  Then it's not totally stopped ... small amounts of water will seep out.  I've even sent them a short video to illustrate the problem.  Today I tried to use the floor attachment to clean the kitchen floor.  I've loved that in the past .. knowing the floor was as clean as it possibly could be.  The cloths they send with the unit are virtually worthless. They stretch as they get wet and since the unit is very hot, it's almost impossible to reposition them on the cleaning head.  I really think the Swiffer does almost as good as this did.  I'll try using a bar towel next time and hopefully it will work better than what is included.  The squeegee is adequate, but hard to reach the bottom of the shower door.  Could have a better design.  The instructions leave a lot to be desired.  The big thing is there are no instructions as to how many containers of water should be added .. it just says 'to fill water tank completely.'  A quick calculation determined that it takes 3 of the measuring cups to fill the tank (40.6 oz tank / 400 ml measuring cup).  I'd love to return this to Amazon, but it's not their problem ... it's on Sienna.  Hopefully they will finally admit there is a problem with the trigger and replace it as they should.Update:  11/21/13.  My complaint was finally kicked up to someone who recognized that there was a problem with the unit I had and asked me to return it to them.  I got the replacement today, but the elapsed time has been 3 weeks ... 1 week for original unit to get to them; 1 week to 'check it out.' and then 1 week to ship the replacement to me.  I still haven't tried using a decent cloth for the floor attachment, but if nothing else, I have a hand steamer now that I can use.  I still won't change my original rating and wouldn't recommend it to anyone.

I work in an office for two, in the back of my facility; which was designed for two people, two computers one fax, and one printer... Over two decades ago! Needless to say our electronic needs have increased over the years. We have surge protectors, plugged into surge protectors, trying to have enough outlets to keep up with modern times. Recently I got smart, and bought an Ankor 4 port USB power converter. Which helped but it still took up over two places on the power strip, as it plugs in parallel to the strip rather than perpendicular. Talking to my friends at ISHARK, they informed me of the 5 port power converter that they had released for sale at Amazon. Not only does it have 5 ports instead of 4 (which I actually needed believe it or not!) but even better, it has a short cord and a standard plug that only takes up one spot on the power strip! It is absolutely perfect for my needs! So once again the Folks at ISHARKK provide the best bang for your buck value to go along with their outstanding customer service!

I absolutely LOVE this. Let me tell you, this thing is amazing. It looks pretty intense and durable out of the package. The quality is outstanding and it looks tough. The product boasts "Water proof", "Dirt Proof" and "Shock Proof" right on the package. In fact, it says that it can be submerged for up to 3 feet for up to an hour! Hah, I don't think I'll actually try that, however, I BET it would. This is just perfect because it has such an amazing capacity to charge a smartphone and a tablet (the two main things I use everyday). And this is great because I'm also a graduate student and sometimes, when I'm on campus--my charges get low on my devices. This would be perfect to have as a back up in lab, or at home (or when the weather is bad and electricity it out -- it'd be good to have an emergency battery backup), I think it's just really reassuring to have that battery pack that can charge your devices. I'll be honest, I wish it were a little smaller and more chic but I think because of it's "XTREME" nature, it makes sense to have the design the way it is. I can see me being comfortable having this along with me on a camping trip or something. It took a LONG time to charge the actual battery pack though though (6+ hours), but the package says 10 hours (which makes sense for fully charged but I was a bit impatient ). I charged it up to 3-LED lights (there are 4 total) and it managed to charge my phone in about an hour (it was less than 50%) and it charged my iPad 4 from 45% to full. It actually didn't take long to charge my phone because that's typically how long it takes for it to charge using my wall outlet (it was actually faster since usually at around 60% battery, charging can take more than an hour. I know since that's how long it takes to charge my phone in the morning).  It was also really reassuring to see that it charged my iPad 4. I was a bit worried it wasn't going to work with the lightning connector (it's happened to me so many times where I buy a different non-Apple charger and it doesn't work). This worked perfectly!I did charge both devices at the same time, but of course my galaxy s3 charged a bit faster. The Xtreme Powerpak still has a charge in the battery (it used one one little LED light if that makes sense. Basically, it has the ability for a couple of charges of the devices).  Overall, great product. Great to have for emergencies, or traveling, or to just have to be able to charge anywhere!

The 10" pan is perfectly weighted and flat.  Having a glass top electric stove, a truly flat pan is an issue with me. The last three PTFE/PFOA-free pans I've gotten were not flat, or a weighty handle pulled down on a too light pan, or it lost its non stick surface fairly quickly.  This Ozeri pan feels like quality. I have used it for only two weeks thus far, but that daily use has been a pleasure of quick, even cooking on a nonstick surface that is a breeze to wash. I am following the instructions of not preheating for too long, and not using high heat. I appreciate the vendor's comments on the reviews about how to properly wash if the pan begins to stick.  I apply a very thin coating of olive oil with each use.PTFE and PFOA-free nonstick pans have been notoriously disappointing because they lose their nonstick typically within three months use.  I will continue to love my stainless cookware (All-Clad and other high quality being favorites), but there are certain foods that I prefer cooked on a nonstick surface. I'm now getting a second Ozeri, despite my real dislike of the unappetizing green color.  I'll put up with the color in order to keep the air in the house safe for the parrot and the people.UPDATE:  About a month and a half using this pan daily, and it's still perfect.  I can't speak to the problems others are having, but my pan is still nonstick and still flat.  I do wipe on a coating of olive oil before each use and after washing, a THIN coating you can't really even feel.  Meats, fried eggs, pancakes, no sticking.  I will update again if the pan loses its nonstick.3rd Month UPDATE:  This pan continues to be wonderful. I highly recommend it, but the pan apparently requires care since some people have problems. However, the care I use with this pan is no different from how I use any other pan, except one CANNOT cook on high heat with this Ozeri pan.  I have not had anything at all stick and washing is quick and simple. Again, use a THIN coating of olive oil...I'm talking about a couple drops smeared around the pan. Do not cook above medium, if you are watching/stirring. Otherwise, use heat lower than medium. Do not put water in the hot pan, but after it cools, I may add water and dish soap and let it sit a couple minutes while I do the dishes  Then I complete washing it, with no scrubbing of any kind required.6th Month UPDATE:  I have two sizes of this pan, and they are used daily.  They are both still EXCELLENT.  They heat well, cook evenly, and are non-stick.  Nothing peels. Washing is a cinch.  These are super pans, and are far superior to the vastly more expensive non-stick pans that sit in my regret pile.

These speakers are one of a kind!They are perfect for their price!The quality is a solid 9/10The sound (Loudness without distortion) 9/10The price is exceptional 10/10The durability is a strong 10/10 (The speakers have held up for over a year and sound as new)Now, of course you can purchase a much higher quality set of speakers at a much inflated price, but for $40 these are by far superior to any otherThere is little to no distortion when music is loud.My favorite part about these speakers is the ability to toggle bass input at a simple movement!A definite must have for many individuals!

. . .even if the in-laws didn't want it to happen.When I bought a new iMac to replace an old Dell, I joined a growing family of computer owners.  Our laser and inkjet printers are too young to toss out but our new computers don't have a parallel port for a printer.  Parallel and serial ports are disappearing, even on Intel/Windows machines.The computer biz's solution:  Come on, buy a snazzy new USB printer for that snazzy new computer!  Apple doesn't even list my HP LaserJet 6L among compatible printers for the iMac. Could they be in cahoots?  Hmmm . . .The LaserJet still prints out a razor-sharp manuscript.  With a little maintenance now and then, I can get more value out of it than I would for the price of a new printer.  Off I went in search of a solution, but at all the usual brick-n-mortars, a simple USB-to-parallel cable was half a C-note!Back here on Amazon.com, I found this Sabrent cable.  Though most of the reviews extoll its virtues on Windows machines, I figured I could gamble the cost.I hit the jackpot.  With the price of shipping, the cable is less than half the lowest price I found.  Even better, the iMac already had (or found on the Internet) a driver for the cable, and then hunted up a public domain driver for the LaserJet that works like a charm.So, a 21st century computer has found the perfect mate in a printer from the late 1990s.  And they lived happily ever after.

I bought these headphones for my girlfriend to use with her 4G ipod, and for the money these can't be beat!  IMHO they sound better then my 3 year old Sennheiser HD497's.  The only downside I can think of for PX100's is that the carrying case is kind of hard to use and easy to break in a backpack. Other then that they are great! I am currently dehydrating slices of kiwi, apples, banana, grape and strawberries.They have been drying for last 12 hours but most of the fruits are nowhere near done.Maybe I am suppose to slice them thiner?  I sliced them about 1/4 inches thick as instruction suggested.I don't know but I think this is just a big waste of energy to run the machine for more than 12 hours to get a few ounces of dried fruits.I am going to try to make some beef jerky and maybe I will like the machine more but for now, I don't think I will ever use it to dry fruits. I mean, I can go to Trader Joe's and get a whole bag of dried mango slices for around $2-$3.Another concern is plastic.  I am sure these plastics are not BPA free and with the high heat setting for a prolong time, I don't know if this is actually safe for my baby daughter.

As far as shower curtains go, this polyester one from InterDesign is a step up, aesthetically and functionally, from your average vinyl or plastic curtain. The leaf print design (I bought the grey version) is tasteful and muted, and the curtain has an appealing look and feel to it. It is machine-washable and features steel-reinforced grommets so that the holes don't rip from use. The curtain is slightly translucent -- it lets sunlight through, but, otherwise is pretty opaque. I'm happy with it.

The documentation states that running "setup" from the CD will begin a Windows install process.  It will not.  Programs run from the CD only. You could probably copy them manually to your hard drive if you wanted. The link to the documentation in PDF format on the CD is invalid.  Users must go to the file directly by locating it on the CD.Installation instructions advise users to copy the IP address which the software initially displays, and then close and reopen the program to display the "Discovered" IP address delivered by the DHCP server. The "discovered" address is supposed to be used in the print driver setup.  This discovered address does not exist.  It will not respond to a ping, and IP discovery software classifies it as "dead."This address is displayed as the IP address of the server in the web admin software also; however attempting to invoke the web admin software by using the discovered address fails, and the admin software can only be opened by using the very first IP address displayed--which is never displayed again.  If you don't write this address down initially, you can not install the software or succeed with a printer setup. Working with a static IP address that you assign may produce different results, but I did not try this, and the documentation did not refer to this process.The web admin software requires a user name and password.  The password is the one set when originally running the software from the CD.  The documentation fails to give the required user name, and I found it only by reading reviews (after my purchase) on other web sites.  The user name which must be entered for the web admin software to work is "root".My HP P2015d printer was connected to the Buffalo server, the server to a wireless LinkSys router, and the desktop computer connected to the router via Ethernet cable.  My goal was to use my laptop wireless connection to print without needing to have the desktop always on to act as a print server.Installation on the desktop which is directly connected to my wireless router via Ethernet cable went relatively well. I was able to print test pages to the printer via the print server after five minutes.Installation on the laptop was a nightmare.  After 11 hours of trying everything I could think of, including connecting directly to the router via cable, nothing worked.  I then tried setting up internet printing on the laptop.  After configuring my router port to forward to the correct IP address, this internet printing worked on the first try.  After that, for some unknown reason, I was able to install the printer and connect over the router. I have no idea what changed or why. I suspect it had something to do with the successful installation of the internet printing protocol, but that's a guess.At this point, I was happy, test pages printed from the laptop and the desktop.  The next day I attempted to print a 16 page document from the desktop to the print server. After two pages printed, there was a 3 minute pause, and then blank pages, or pages with garbage began printing.  I tried this several times, with the exact same results.  When I took the print server offline and connected directly via USB cable to the desktop, the print job finished without a hitch.  I don't often print 16 page documents, but that is a tremendous limitation to have to worry about.  I even tried printing just pages 1 to five through the server, but once again only two pages printed.  I am returning this unit and purchasing a different product, hoping to have better results.I would not recommend this to a friend, but would joyfully present it to an enemy!

I had read many of the reviews for this product and almost did not buy it.  I did research on other grinders, some quite a bit more expensive but ultimately came back to this one.  That said, this if the third one of this brand and model that I have purchased.  The first one lasted many years, maybe 10 or more. The second one I purchased for our RV and it broke the second time I used it.  Nevertheless, when my first one gave up the ghost (I do believe it was tired) I decided to buy it again.  For me, it does exactly what it needs to do, grind coffee beans.Is the grind (whichever is selected) perfectly uniform?  Absolutely not.  Does the grind match the selection?  Absolutely yes.  Whichever grind is selected, does the coffee taste good? Yes, yes it does. To me, that's all that counts.  It is a relatively inexpensive machine and I do believe you will get your monies worth.
What can you say about a cable? Either it gives you great video quality, or it doesn't.  This one definitely does.  I bought another.

I'll guess with the "king" Norwalk you might get 5% more juice out of the same stock, but the Norwalk was such a chore for small daily batches I didn't use it more than once a month.  This juicer I use about every other day.

I bought this coffee maker to replace a Mr. Coffee that developed a leak. I'm glad I switched brands, because this is a much nicer  product.The carafe is well designed, rarely a drip after pouring a cup. It has a nice shape, and I like the handle which is ergonomic and fits the hand very well and comfortably. The lid is permanent, unlike the Mr Coffee which had a lid that was loose and had to be put back on before you could make a pot of coffee.The removable filter basket is better designed and holds paper filters snugly. If you use a Mr Coffee permanent filter it sits exactly inside, like it was designed for this coffee maker. It was loose in the Mr Coffee, not that it mattered much.I make a whole pot every morning and it takes about 10 minutes or so, maybe longer, I haven't actually timed it. It isn't any faster than the Mr Coffee though. The hot plate definitely keeps the coffee hotter, but not too hot to drink. The water level is very easy to see because the water reservoir window is quite large.The clock is a green LED and it's a nice easy to read device, and again the Mr Coffee  had a clock that was impossible to read without a flashlight. I haven't used the timer to brew a pot before I wake up, but it can do that.As far as drawbacks, there aren't any. There isn't a beeper to tell you when the coffee is finished brewing, which the Mr Coffee had, but I don't think that's very important.This is a really good coffee maker and a good buy at this price.

Initially, I had some hesitation in buying a non-OEM product for my note 3, but after installing this receiver, all my fears vanished.  I chose this receiver over all the others because this one was NFC supported.  I read reviews and watched youtube videos on all the different receivers and found that a majority of them interfered with NFC.  I did not like the fact that in order to use one capability of the phone, I had to sacrifice another.  This receiver works like a champ.  Now I have wireless charging capability without any side affects to NFC.  This receiver is also very thin compared to others.  After installing the receiver, I cannot see any bumps to the back cover like other receivers tend to leave.  I have a Spigen Neo Hybrid case for the note 3 and it completes the look and functions I want.  Now when I want to charge my phone, I just place it on a Qi enabled charging pad (TYLT Vu Charging Stand).  No micro usb cord to plug in every time to charge.  Convenient and cool.  I would recommend this receiver to anyone.***UPDATE***Looks like the receiver stopped working.  I took it off to try out the OEM receiver.  When I applied it back on, it wouldn't charge.  I was really careful when I initially took it off but it seems to not have mattered.  I made sure all the contacts were touching and it still won't charge.  I guess it's not a good idea taking it off once it is put on.  It was good while it lasted.

More expensive older one went on the fritz after about 4 years.  This one is just as large and can handle full size needs (9x13s, even 12 piece muffin pans), while not heating up the house, especially in summer, which is why I always like to have a countertop oven; and with a back problem, arms length accessibility is a great convenience.  This one is faster and more powerful than my older more expensive one.  Love it!  Only downsides are that there's no interior light (took suggestion of another reviewer and keep a small flashlight near oven - does the trick), and would prefer digital settings like my older one, for more exact settings/time (impossible to find these days unless too big).  I set a kitchen timer just to be sure.  Bakes very evenly, but read enclosed instructions.  As it recommends, found that for convection baking, temp better 25 degrees less.  Would love toasting function, but found that on a high convection baking temp, it covers that function well too.  Overall a good oven that bakes evenly, with good capacity, for a very good/reasonable price!  Looks good too! Almost bought another commercially rated one by Waring, but this one got better reviews, so glad I tried this first, as has all the same functions and significant savings.  Very happy with my new kitchen toy. (Only gave 4 stars for lack of digital settings and interior light; otherwise would give 5 stars!)

I would say this earphone is alright for the $40, but not the $100 MSRP. I have owned countless earphones from $10 to $500 ranging from CX300, ue 4vi, ue500, klipsch x10 and the mighty sennheiser ie8.To start off, this earphone has a very nice braided cable that is soft and very thin, but feels durable. The packaging is also nicer than the most price at this range.First impression: it really depends on the eartips you use. If using the standard silicon eartips, the mid is really lushy, but treble might sound harsh in certain songs and bass loses some definition and punch. With the foam/olive eartips, you get a much better seal and a HUGE bass boost. Personally I do not like a lot of bass, but most importantly, the mid suffers a lot by using the foam tip.After about 10 hours of burn in, the massive bass hump has settled down and mid and treble start to slowly coming out, however nowhere enough for a long fatigue free listening. Don't get me wrong, those earphones are great and I can hear a lot of potentials. Or maybe im expecting too much as I have been using the ie8 for 3 years.For the price, I feel like you could get a more accurate sounding earphones from the ue 4vi (don't know why so many bad reviews) or a better mid with the UE350 or UE500.

I must say for a pair of headphones under 20 bucks they really fit the bill rather nicely.  It would have been nice to have the top padded but they are still fairly comfortable to wear due to the ear cups having soft padding on them.  They fit good on my head and the rotating cup feature is good to get that perfect fine-tuned fit. A big plus for me is that the cord is really long, I hate ear phones with short cords and these give you plenty of roaming room! Overall, I would say comfort-wise they are probably a solid 6 out of 10.Now about the sound.Just to give you an example of what music I am judging the sound by, I mainly listen to Jazz in the bebop/hardbop vein with some fusion mixed in for good measure.  I like the way these sound except they are kind of mid/bass heavy to me and in my opinion, kinda miss some of the higher end. I guess to me they are a tad bit muddy. Not extreme, just a little lacking on the crispness of the high end. I prefer more emphysis on the treble of the cymbals and hi-hats, but thats my opinion.  That being said, the bass is pretty good and full, what you would expect in a full size headphone. The mids are a little strong for me but not at all weak sounding. I give the sound a 6 out of 10 as well.In conclusion, I say these are an above average headphone for this price range although being JVC, I kinda expected them to sound better, as I have had JVC products in the past and they have always impressed me. Anyway, I don't think you'll be kicking yourself for getting these phones, they sound pretty good, especially for low dough!A side note...I have recently fell in love with some earbuds by Sony (MDREX58V/RED). They have AMAZING sound and nice tight bass(as long as they fit in your ear properly) and crisp treble. And they cost about the same as the JVC's, just be aware that they vary their prices according to color which I think is NUTS!  The JVC headphones may have to play second best to these, you might want to consider looking at these first!

I just got back from my first shoot with the Apollo 28" soft box and i must say I am very impressed with its performance outside, this thing provides BEAUTIFUL soft light! I love it over all my umbrellas. I also own the ORB and this box is built much better. It has double ribs and thicker steel rods than the ORB. I built a DIY bracket and now can fire my dual 580 EXII's  with Pocket Wizards for a killer wireless soft box capable of HSS. I will probably keep the ORB for in-studio work and use the 28" apollo for my on-location stuff. Feathering the light is very easy because of the recessed design and setup is a snap. A lot of folks cry about not being able to tilt the unit much, to me it's only a minor flaw, I prefer to have the stand feed trough the front as it allows better tilt and super quick removal to shoot in bare flash mode and if I need to tilt more I can simply buy an L bracket. This definitely gets my recommendation for you to try, I will be bringing this softbox with me on every future shoot.

This is a nice, inexpensive collection of non-stick cookware.  The aluminum is thin, hence the four star rating, but the ceramic coating easily releases cooked food.  Even incinerating (aka. frying a bit too long...) food in the frying pan only resulted in some slight discoloration.This set, with silicone handles, is good in the oven to 350 degrees.  Don't tune on your broiler :-)  The silicone is molded around a metal stem, which in turn is riveted to the base.  It's a solid connection.The manufacturer claims that this is dishwasher safe.  It may be for a while, but I wouldn't recommend it.  The bases are raw exposed aluminum and aluminum is attacked by most dishwasher soaps.Is it pro-level?  No, but for this price, you're not going to get a set of AllClad.

I'm not crazy about the square keys, and another minor problem is that "<RightShift>-I-D" does not work when you type it fast.  Specifically, if you hold down RightShift, then hold down "I", then hold down "D", the "D" will not show up.  Most users will probably not notice this, but if you are a programmer who types fast, then it may bother you.  In my case, I have some database queries that involve column names such as "EXPID" and "JSID", and when I typed them fast using RightShift, I kept wondering why the "D" was always omitted.  Now I know.

I buy many products based upon reviews from other users.  That is also the case with this lens.  I bought my Nikon D40 about 3 months ago (after 10 years of using point and shoots, including my treasured Canon S2is super zoom), with the kit 18-55mm lens, plus I bought a Nikon 55-200mm VR lens.  I love the VR lens, but traveling through Europe for 2 1/2 weeks proved to me that I HATE changing lenses.  It's inconvenient and potentially contaminates the inside of the camera body.  So, I started looking around at something that I could use as a single walk-around lens.  I love the wide angle perspective of the 18mm end, plus needed at least 200mm for the telephoto end.  I liked the idea of Tamron's 18-250mm, but it's not image stabilized, which is really essential at the telephoto end.  So, back to the Sigma.  I read Camera Labs review of the non-stabilized Sigma 18-200mm, and it was favorable.  So, I figured that the new stabilized version would be at least as good, and probably better.I have not used this lens much over the past couple of weeks, but have found that (with a couple of exceptions), it focuses at least as quickly as the 18-55mm Nikkor kit lens, if not faster.  The OS works great, making hand-held photos rock solid at 200mm.  The only thing that I haven't spent a lot of time doing is taking indoor photos with it.  I have taken indoor photos with the onboard flash, and they seem to work fine.  I have not had much luck getting sharp photos at the telephoto end indoors, but admittedly have not tried very hard.  I'll update this later, as I get more experience.  Based upon what I know now, I am NOT AT ALL SORRY that I bought this lens.  I was really having a hard time choosing between this and the [MUCH more expensive] Nikkor 18-200mm, but this lens seems like it is well worth the money spent.  The focus/OS noise does not bother me at all (In fact, it's reassuring to hear that the camera is trying to focus, etc...).  The only thing I wish Sigma had done was to make the lens Auto/Manual focus without having to move the switch from Auto to Manual, which the Nikkor can do.  However, if it auto-focuses reliably, I guess it won't be a big deal.  The zoom ring is a little tight right now, but I imagine that it will loosen up over time (hopefully not to the point of requiring the lock to be engaged).  I also like the large manual focus ring; the Nikkor's is much smaller.  Don't be put off by the 72mm diameter (which I was initially).  It's not a big deal.  Yes, it is quite a bit heavier and larger than the kit lens (or even my 55-200mm Nikkor), but not overly so.  In fact, it makes the camera easier to hold, since I think the D40/40x/60 grip is just a little too small.  I was concerned that the onboard flash will be blocked by the lens, but it seems to have no effect.  I'm not seeing any shadowing.  I'll keep you advised on how my ongoing review is going.Happy shooting!Update:  Sept 19, 2008 - I continue to love this lens.  The ONLY thing that bothers me about it is (as someone mentioned in another review) that the zoom and focus rings are too close together.  When I hand this to my wife or son, I have to remind them which one is the zoom ring, and not to touch the focus (BTW - It makes a gears-are-grinding noise when you move the focus ring without moving it to manual - Not good, although it does not seem to have hurt the lens).  There is a slight amount of shadowing the on board flash when you are at any zoom over the 18mm.  However, I rarely if ever use the on board flash.  I have a Nikon SB-400 flash that is absolutely essential inside, where you can bounce it (read Ken Rockwell's review at [...]  I have not put the 18-55mm kit lens back on this camera since I have had the 18-200, and probably won't.  I have a very old Nikon G model 50mm f1.8 that I can use (no metering or autofocus) on those low-light non-flash indoor shots, where the Sigma is not at it's best (although it's still very good).

I am very impressed with the quality of pictures taken by the two Fuji digital cameras I own.  I think they take better pictures than cameras costing significantly more so I am locked into the XD format.  Yes, it costs a little more than other formats but just wait for a sale / rebate as I did with each of the eight XD cards I have purchased.They do offer fast speed and help battery life.  I have also bought a couple of XD accessories (USB readers that I have been impressed with).  I prefer the 128 MB size cards to the larger size memory cards since they allow me to keep track of what pictures I take when on vacation.  For example, I use one card one day in so and so place and another card in a different location...just my system that works for me.  A 128 MB card lets me take plenty of pictures at a 4 MP setting. I also won't lose a lot of pictures if one card would fail for some reason or be lost.  I bought a small case that is custom made to accommodate the XD cards.  I can fit 16 cards in a small metal case about 2.25" x 4" x .5" thanks to their small size.If it matters to you, some of these Fuji cards are made in Japan by Toshiba and some are made in Korea by Samsung.  You can tell the difference since the Japan / Toshiba cards come in a slightly larger plastic case contained within the clamshell packaging.

Bought a high end Lenovo W520 directly from Lenovo.It is the best performing computer I've ever had.  The graphics and power are great.One reason for purchase was the famous reputation for build quality and the delightful to use "solid" keypad on Thinkpads.  The keypad was NOT solidly supported and had flex at one end.Had hopes that maybe "some" of that famous IBM Thinkpad build quality might have been inherited.At the very first use, a key popped off the keypad when typing normally. Exam under magnification showed key had been damaged / glued.Now another key has broken on the replacement keypad. The plastic moldings, for attaching the keys, is extremely fragile and weak.A piece of the air vent grill has broken out and the "J" mouse cover came off.Even had a USB plug guide break (plastic part again). Normal use.Abusive user, eh? As an engineer, I've used different brands of computers all my life...Have never experienced this before with any brand or model except....  an early ProStar laptop with cheap plastic that had some of these same problems.The build quality of this generation of Thinkpads is so much lower than the original IBM Thinkpad, that, to me, it severs the heritage.My 8 year old Dell Inspiron 7200 was a "tank" compared to this Lenovo Thinkpad.This machine dual boots into Windows 7 Pro 64 and Linx operating systems (primary).  It runs both without problems.The "J" mouse, and buttons, have proven very useful to me...especially when doing pixel level editing.First time I ever got an extended warranty.  Glad I did.Lenovo's contract service has been good and contacts with Lenovo directly have been satisfactory.Were it not for build quality, I would rate this laptop an easy 5.  Very disappointed and would definitely shop before buying again.These Thinkpads are NOT IBM Thinkpads.
Wires are thin but for the amount of power i am using it works good.  All in all great product

2012-01-04I bought one of these things at the local Fry's (not the greatest retailer, but better than buying EVERYTHING from Amazon.com). It's a nice case, tons of fans, lovely clear side panel. People have finally gotten a little smart about putting the heavy power supply at the bottom; nice to see.Not so nice to see: while the specs list this case as "microATX" compatible, there is a BIG BIG difference between "compatible" and "support." As in "has threaded supports for every mounting hole on a microATX motherboard." I put my Intel thunder-humper motherboard in, only to find there are four, not EIGHT places to bolt the puppy down. As a result one must exercise a LOT of care when attaching cables: the connectors are almost all out at the edges, and too much flexing of the motherboard will cause a failure if you're not careful. "Failure," as in "pitch your motherboard and get another." STUPID STUPID STUPID way to save $0.50 in manufacturing costs, Antec!I'll add to this when I hook up them gazillion cables this weekend: working on a black-on-black system after dark is something my eyes can't cope with...

I bought this lens from Amazon Warehouse and it's in great shape. What a sharp lens! Using it with an Olympus E-P3, it functions very well. I don't think its size or weight are a problem for the PEN E-P3. I had tried the OM 9-18m; although it is smaller and lighter than this lens, I found it too soft at some of the focal lengths. That is not the case here and the extra 2mm really makes a difference. Shooting at 7mm [=14mm]  is a dream, especially for landscapes. Distortion can be somewhat corrected in PhotoShop as can the chromatic aberration. There really is less CA than I anticipated [since Olympus does not correct Panasonic CA like the Panasonic cameras do]. Distortion can also be controlled in how the lens is held in relation the to subject.  My only concern is that expressed by others; you can't put a filter on this so you have to be cautious, especially when hiking around brush. The extended sides of the lens creates a kind of hood which seems to be pretty good for resisting sun spots and flare but not always. Because of the wide angle, its sometimes difficult not to have some sun bleed in if you are facing the sun in any way. Because there is no filter, you have to be careful with dust as well, and because there is no filter, I feel I need to carry the lens cap which is kind of large and not as easily pocketable as a normal lens cap [but it is doable]. On the whole, this is a wonderful lens and inspires me to take a lot of photos.

I was able to usually keep a 700k to 1.5M connection, which was enough to look for gas stops, food stops or a hotel. But keep in mind the fact that it only supports voice and 3G.  It is a more basic model so it won't help your 4G connection (LTE, etc) but it's better than not having it!For us it was a bit pricey but worth it.  But if they ever reduce the price of one that works with 4G I'll probably upgrade, since now I use my Blackberry as a 4G hotspot!

Ok, here is the thing. I never owned Apple's ipod before. As some one that has played with mp3 players way before apple launched the first ipod I have always found a good reason to stay out of shopping one.So let me start by explaining where I am coming from on this review: I was not looking for a good music player. If you are, then this is not for you. Go and look for a more economic player that will do the job as a music player better than this ( and that is not an ipod nano by the way). I wanted a large display device that could connect to my wifi network to navigate the net and check email, without firing up a laptop. Up to today, There are no devices out there yet that can do this as well and as portable as the ipod touch.The device connected to the wifi network easily without fuss. the Browser is great. with finger gestures to zoom in and out on areas of interest, makes this unit may favorite to check quicly my emails and browse the internet before I decide that I need to fire my laptop. Playing my music, audible books, and podcast is a plus for an all in one device that fits in my hand.Good:- Wifi connectivity. No other mp3 player is as compact as this and can browse the interned with an excellent browser for a portable device.- Apple Store: I love it. It is well populated with applications and some are free. I got a dictionary, and a game. but there are other things that pick my interest. No need to connect to a computer for this. Just connect to wifi network.- keeping up with my podcasts without connecting to the laptop is another great thing. Well done.Average:- Music player: not a great sounding music player. Apple continues its philosphy that adding a good equalizer will confuse the poor apple sheep. This is not a big deal for podcasts and audible books. But again, if your first need is for a good music player then stay out of this one and do some reasearch on non-apple players. They do it better and you get more features for usually less money.- Cover flow is just a gimmick. Looks nice in the adds but I ended prefering the old index listing way.- Itunes: It has become a bloated software. My machine fires up quickly the windows media player when I want to sync my Sansa Fuze. iTunes will not give me an hourglass for a few seconds to let me know that I have double clicked the software to launch it. I have given it the avarage rating because after the program is loaded makes syncing as easy as windows media player (that is right: people that tell you itunes is easier to use than anything else are fanboys and a statement like that should dimiss anything else they have to say)Bad:- Gaming machine: I have loaded a game that is a really good time killer, and there are many games in the apple store that I might be tempted to try out. But here is the problem: The battery does not last as much as you would like for a gaming machine. The Playstation Portable has been hammered for its poor battery life compared to nintendo DS, but the Touch runs through the battery much faster. This might be because it has a much lower capacity battery in that slim body than the nintendo DS.So I am not throwing away my other portable gaming machine.- iTunes Store: My opinion on this one is basically my preference of opennes. I buy my music from DRM free sites like Amazon ( I should get discount coupon for this plug). Seriously, you want a library that can be moved across non-apple devices as I do, and pay less while you build it up, you should look elsewhere. iTunes is too expensive and that music is locked on an apple device only, unless you go through the trouble of re-ripping your music. I think not, I rather extend the life of my laptop cd player/bruner, I bet it is cheapy anyways so I dont want to stress it out.

Before reading further please realize two things:  1) that Hamilton Beach makes several models of their BrewStation and that they are not all the same basic design or for that matter, even a similar design to other BrewStations; and 2) that the BrewStation concept is significantly different that the stereotypical Mr. Coffee style of coffeemaker.  Different doesn't necessarily mean better (or worse) so please read all of the details of this specific model before deciding whether it is the right model for you OR if the entire BrewStation concept is something you would be happy with.THE BREWSTATION COMPARED WITH THE TYPICAL CARAFE-TYPE COFFEE MAKER:The single most obvious difference is that the typical coffee maker has a top lid that lifts, a drawer of some sort that pulls out to hold the ground coffee, and a pot (usually glass) that sits under a spout.  You put a filter and the required amount of coffee into the drawer and slide it into place, you open a door on top and pour in the required amount of coffee into one place and water into another, and turn it on.  The coffee dribbles through the spout into the waiting carafe, which usually sits on a warming element to keep it warm for a while.  The advantages are familiarity; everyone knows how to make coffee in such a coffee maker.  The disadvantages are that the part that holds the water is usually molded in as part of the unit and is difficult to clean thoroughly.  The tray that holds the coffee is sometimes a challenge to line up and slide into place.  But the biggest drawback is the link between the spout and the carafe is often complicated and prone to leaking, the carafe is definitely prone to breakage and expensive to replace, and often the prepared coffee boils away causing the bottom of the carafe to burn and sometimes shatter.The BrewStation idea is to simplify the process.  There is no carafe, the coffee brews (in a very similar way) inside the coffee maker and you "pour" as much as you need by pressing your cup to a dispensing switch, much like you dispense soda pop at a fast food restaurant.  In most of the BrewStation designs the parts all come apart to wash in the sink or dishwasher and there is less tendency to spill as you are pouring.  Some BrewStations do this task well, some not so well.  But on the plus side there is nothing to break or replace, unless or course the heating element burns out.  Expect that to happen with any and every coffee maker you can buy regardless of brand, model or design ... a coffee maker bought today can be expected to have a useful life of less than 5 years, sometimes much less.THE 47701 COMPARED WITH SOME OF THE DESIGN ELEMENTS OF OTHER HAMILTON BEACH BREWSTATIONS:As previously noted, some of the designs of different models of BrewStations from Hamilton Beach differ so much it is a safe bet they are made in entirely different factories.  Some look cheap.  Some have many fussy parts and pieces and are complicated to use.  Some (as noted in numerous reviews here) have design flaws, like the nozzle that pours as much coffee out of the cup than in it (not the case with this model).I almost didn't buy a Hamilton Beach at all for two reasons ... valid negative reviews and a baffling array of models, none of which seemed to be ideal.  But it seems most people love the basic design concept of being carafe-less.As far as I can tell the 47701 is either a very new model or a limited edition.  The well-known "W" superstore wasn't going to start selling it for a few weeks after I bought it from Amazon, but even here it had zero reviews and only a few words of description.  The same was true of several other major retailers ... information was scarce and sketchy.Here's what I can tell you now that I own one of the first:1. It is simple looking and attractive, far less gaudy than so many other coffee makers.  It comes in basic black with a better than average metal stainless steel trim around the control panel.2. The buttons are basic, and you don't need a PhD to use them.  They are real buttons not something pressed into a plastic sheet.  There are 4 user button plus a large, tactile and prominent on/off button.  If you don't choose any options the entire process of making a pot of coffee is to add water and coffee and press one button once.  That's how a coffee maker should be made.3. The LCD display is large, bright and LIGHTED.  All of the controls are easy to read for those with less than perfect eyesight.4. There are an absolute minimum number of parts and no breakable hinges or snaps or fasteners.  The major parts consist of the unit itself and the water reservoir which can be lifted off to fill and serves as a pitcher to pour the water into the brewing cavity and then the place the coffee ends up at the end of the brewing cycle (sounds complicated but is ultra simple).  Once you fill the brewing cavity you lay the water reservoir back onto the base, put a filter into the lift-off holder and measure the right amount of coffee into it, and lay it on top, and finally lay a plastic lid on top of that.  None of those parts have any moving or breakable parts and all of them can be put on the top tray of your dishwasher.5. The controls don't try to launch a rocket, they let you simply select whether you want your coffee to be bolder or you want to brew a very small amount.  Another button determines what time the coffee with start brewing and another is dedicated to using the BrewStation to make iced coffee (something Hamilton Beach seems to have a lock on)6. The display will let you know when it's time to deep clean the unit, which is a simple process of running 2 cups of white vinegar through it using an auto-cleaning cycle every so-often (depending on how often you make coffee).7. Hamilton Beach does not force you to use a tap water filter if you already have a source of good water in your house.  If you don't you have the optional choice of plugging in a charcoal water filter that should last several months each.  I used the same filters on a previous Hamilton Beach and they work fine and are not overly priced.  PS: while they make a big point about the tap water filter being optional, my coffee maker from Amazon included one free in the box.8. As far as the misplaced spout on some other models, this one doesn't have the problem.  They redesigned the pour switch to be more rounded to cradle your cup into the right position and the spout pours much more into the center of any cup.NEGATIVES?I have seen a similar situation with other products in which a manufacturer wants so much to push a unique feature that they sacrifice convenience to overdo its importance.  The iced coffee feature on Hamilton Beach coffee makers is nice, but in reality how many people will use it daily?  My suggestion to them is rather than dedicate one of the precious few of the 4 function buttons (other than on/off) I would rather have seen them make it a feature of the Brew Option button and dedicate the iced coffee button to either choosing brew strength or keep-warm time.BOTTOM LINE:I can't think of seeing any coffee maker with more of a simple design.  I just can't imagine anything breaking or being fussy to use.  It makes a good tasting cup of coffee and it keeps it warm for a reasonable amount of time.  It's easy to use, easy to clean and doesn't take up a huge amount of counter space.

This has AMAZING capacity and I really love that about it.The only thing that kept me from a five-star rating is the way the lid fits: it's too loose! On my smaller Brita, I could fill the entire filter reservoir and still pour already-filtered water from the pitcher; on this model, if you fill the filter resevoir and then try to pour a glass before the water has worked its' way down, the lid comes off and the water spills everywhere.Not a deal breaker, but not great, either.

I bought this scale about 9 months ago to weigh my backpacks for hiking / backpacking trips, it is a big help for trip planning.  Also gets used for a lot of other small tasks - the fact you can hang it and hang a bag with stuff in it from the hook makes this a very useful scale.This scale has been a welcome surprise for accuracy.  I did not have high expectations going in, and would have been happy if this were a tad outside of the tolerances for lighter objects under 2lbs and heavier ones over, say, 90lbs... but as usual with American Weigh scales, this one exceeded my expectations.I own many scales, and have a myriad of different calibration weights.  Even with as litlle as 1-2 lbs, this scale reads right where it should.  The error is small enough, and definitely withing the .05 tolerance it is sold at, that I typically feel confident in what I am reading whether it is 2 lbs or 40 lbs on the hook.For the price paid, I would recommend this scale to anyone looking for a versatile, cheap and durable scale for their needs.  As always, those seeking accuracy to the gram level would need to look elsewhere.  This is my fifth AWS scale, and I have been happy with every one of them.  Plus, AWS has a ten year warranty, and if you ever call the company, you get a person (I got Kevin twice) who is friendly, knowledgeable and will take whatever time you need.Would buy this again.

Tylt seems to be putting quite a bit of effort into designing mobile accessories that have a style and flair that is quite different from the usual generic black utilitarian look.For educational purposes, keep in mind that all USB chargers always provide 5 Volts to the device.  This is part of the USB specification.  The other relevant metric is how much current a charger can provide.  Don't worry about a charger providing "too much" current and damaging your device.  The device will draw as much current  as it needs and no more, limited by the current capacity of the device.  Let's use a water analogy: the voltage could be compared to the temperature of the water.  So a USB charger is like a hose that always puts out water at the same temperature: 50 degrees (or 5V from the charger).  The current is how much water you can get out of the hose.  Let's say a hose can provide 1 gallon/second consistently.  If you try to suck more than that out of the hose, it will lose water pressure and be unable to provide more water.  That's like the current capacity of the charger.From a functional standpoint, this charger stands out because of its high current charging capacity.  4.2A is double the usual 2.1A car charger.  I was able to test it with a variety of devices with these results:- iPhone 5 charged ok with generic Lightning cable.  This phone is notoriously picky about what it will charge from.- iPad 3 charged ok with Apple OEM cable.  This tablet is even more picky about which chargers and cables it will work with.  It was also able to charge two iPad 3's at the same time.- Samsung Galaxy S4 charged ok with either of two cables I tried.  However, it was weird that it charged at 1200mA when using a general micro-USB cable but only 820mA with the Samsung OEM cable.  I believe the maximum charging current for the S4 is 1220mA.Unfortunately, I was not able to measure charge times because I did this while running my car in my garage and I didn't want to waste the gas required to charge the iPads for several hours.  I did use a charge current measuring app with the Samsung S4, but as far as I know, there is no way to monitor charge current on iOS devices.  Overall, I am happy with the charging current provided by this charger.Now, as for the physical design, the blue color and unique shape is visually striking and built-solidly.  The rubberized coating is nice to touch and provides good grip for pulling it out of its socket.  However, I did find that the spread of the Y shape made it awkward to plug things into.  In my car, when two or even one device was plugged in, it was hard to find a position were the two legs of the Y and the cable ends weren't bumping or in the way of something.  I think perhaps a more conventional straight design might work better.

The product is based on a fallback design used by a couple thousand different companies for a rather long time, a simple tried-and-true aluminum box and hinge with plastic reinforcement, and rubber inserts. Overall, it hasn't failed me, and serves the purpose it is designed to serve just fine.I would have preferred to see the inserts formed to fit standard SD and MicroSD, which is a wide industry standard, as opposed to the MiniSD format that it is sized to fit that I've yet to run into a device that uses. But alas, it works. And that's about as much as it needs to do.

You dont really need a SATA III cable since a SATA II will work in its place.It works, price was good.Other thoughts....Would have been better to get a FLAT cable but for the price and application... no matter.

This product is perfect!  Fits in the CD slot in both our 2011 Ford Fusion and Escape. Takes seconds to install and holds our iPhones securely. We can still use the CD player. It does block a couple of buttons and the view of the car's info display, but you can rotate the mount to a horizontal and that alleviates the view issue. I highly recommend this product!  No sticking things to the dashboard or windshield -- it's great!

The Vinturi is an attractive, maybe even elegant, wine accessory. As its name implies, it's function is based on a simple and elegant physical principle (known as the venturi effect).I've used the Vinturi for several weeks now. It definitely improves some wines, particularly those that are "big", or those that are young. It seems to dramatically improve cheap tannic wines. I haven't done blind taste tests, but I suspect I'd find a verifiable improvement in taste among those groups of wines. I can't imagine that a well-blended wine meant to be drunk right away (as opposed to one meant to be cellared) would be improved much at all. My unscientific survey seems to confirm that, though perhaps another principle is at play here - the placebo effect. That's not necessarily a bad thing. If my enjoyment of a seven dollar bottle of wine is significantly enhanced, the money I save purchasing cheap wine will pay for the Vinturi, and then some.Although the Vinturi seems to be a simple device, it's not clear whether its function is consistent. It's a bit difficult to pour the wine down the edge of its bowl, as indicated in the instructions. Sometimes I consistently get the satisfying sound of the wine gurgling into my glass, but when the bowl gets too full and/or the wine drips from the two aerating holes, or the wine pours straight down the middle, is my wine still getting enough air? Is the Vinturi still working?The most elegant device would be one designed to eliminate the ambiguity of its function (the ambiguity of its effect is yet another issue). I could imagine a  Vinturi 2.0 whose mouth directs the wine down the edge of the bowl and eliminates the need for a practiced hand. A video review may have been more instructive; video instructions from the maker might have eliminated my doubts.

I have been making wedding cakes from home for 30 years and I don't know what I did before I had these strips.  I guess I made a lot more cakes than I have to now as I had to cut off so much excess cake to level it.  With these strips I get a full 2" cake in my 2" pan and it's level and flat on top every time.  I never have a problem with them not working.  Other reviews that had problems with it working properly have me concerned they didn't wet them as directed on the box.  You have to wet the strip with water and then run it through your fingers to remove any excess water then secure around the outside of the cake pan with the included clips.  In the past 10 years that I've been using them I have never had a cake not turn out precisely as it was supposed to.  Make sure you are using the correct amount of batter for the size pan you use as well.  I usually use boxed cake mixes and add 1 small package of coordinating pudding mix along with the other ingredients on the cake box.  This makes a firmer cake that won't fall apart or crumble as easily.  My largest Wedding cake was a 8" x 10" x 12" x 14" and every layer worked great.  I only had 1 package of 2 Magic-cake strips in 36" lengths but you can use 2 strips on the larger pans connecting them together with the clips to get them all the way around or buy this bigger package of multiple sizes for all those larger pans.  They work on all shape pans - round, square, octagon, etc...  I only use the 2" tall Wilton or Fat Daddio pans with the straight sides.  The old cake pans I grew up with flared out at the top making it close to impossible to get a perfectly straight, lined up side on your cakes.  Hope this helps.

New telescope owners--new amateur astronomers--often give up the hobby rather quickly because they cannot align their scopes. Usually that is because they do not have the foggiest where the star named Cocab, or Tejat, or whatever obscure star their scope wants to point to actually is. Well with this instrument, they can find that star. Maybe amateur astronomy will become more popular and accessible with instruments like the SkyScout.Things I liked:1. Easy and fast software update (I have a satellite internet connection, and the update time was less than 1').2. Quick GPS location of observing site3. Amazingly accurate "Identify" functionThings I liked less:1. I find the screen a real pain to read. Black on deep red--what's with that? At night my 65 year old eyes dilate and even +3.0 diopter glasses fail to make the screen clear, but they blur the sky, thank you. I know the "night vision" paranoia runs deep, but even gray on red would work much better!2. I have had mostly good "Locate" experiences, although not always. Sometimes it says "go up" then "go right" then "go down" then "go left." You can spend a long evening on this merry-go-round. BTW, the object was nearly centered the whole time.3. There's no way I can find to practice indoors. My unit complains of too much magnetic interference and shuts down. This is really a minor irritation, however.All together--I'd buy one again, without a second though!

The great:  these sound AWESOME.  The bass is so loud I had to turn down the low end in my player's equalizer.  At moderate volume they pick up the quality of the source (in my case, 128-360Kbps MP3, so not the best quality source).  Basically, on lower quality tracks (especially the 128K MP3 streams I listen to), a noticeable hiss is there.  This may mean I'll start ripping CDs in FLAC or something similar.  If you're an audiophile I suspect you'll consider this to be a downside, but I do believe it's because of the source track being inferior (something I couldn't pick up with inferior headphones).  So don't expect flawless execution from your standard MP3 collection.(More of) the great:  The main selling points of these for me were the audio quality (mentioned above) and the Bluetooth feature.  The Bluetooth is great, I can walk nearly eight yards away before the audio cuts out.  Mind you, this is with many (as many as five or six) office building walls in between.  As long as I don't stay away for too long the headphones reconnect to the audio, and the audio picks up as if nothing happened.  Your mileage may vary depending on your player.  But the untethered aspect was the selling point (and why I didn't go for more "audiophile" approved headphones).(Some more of)the great:  Charging seems alright, I just charge it overnight and haven't had these die on me.  I am currently doing a full drain test on these batteries.  I listened to these Thursday, Friday, and now Monday on a single charge.  Even though I've used them maybe five or six hours a weekday, they haven't died on me yet.  I will try to push them until they do.  Actually, I wrote most of this review a couple of days ago (but forgot to submit).  These have lasted through right now (a week from last Thursday), and they're only just now starting to give me what I assume is an "out of battery" beep.  A full five-day work week on one charge.  Not too shabby!The neutral:  I haven't used the audio cable with these, since I only care about the untethered features of these headphones.  The carrying bag is a nice touch (looks similar to a high end felt liquor bottle bag), but not really necessary.  I also haven't used the mic on here, but that's because either Apple or my softphone manufacturer dropped the ball--no Bluetooth headset works well with that combination.The bad:  With extended use, these things hurt my ears after only a couple of hours.  Every day.  This has nothing to do with my volume levels, it's the rim of my ears that feel pressed too much against my skull.  I don't even have very big ears, so it's a little surprising.  At first I thought it would take some getting used to, but I've had these for over two weeks, and I wear them every day at work.  The problem is something I can ignore, but still the soreness is felt when I think about it.  When I go home, I'm going to find the support card for these to see if they can be adjusted.  I'm mostly the village idiot when it comes to mechanical objects, so I may just be missing something.  EDIT:  After a few more weeks of use, it seems my ears are getting used to the headphones.  I haven't had time to contact BassEffect support, I keep meaning to do that.

I just got my iSlice yesterday and am already confident this will be a very frequent use item for me. Because I had a little difficulty assessing exactly what this little thing was before I bought it I wanted to write a more comprehensive review of what this thing is and what it can do.To best understand what it's capable of I will tell you how it works. What you can't see in the picture is a tiny piece of ceramic held in the gray end. Call it a blade, call it a nub, there's not a great word for it but basically it's a pointy bump. The opener is used by gripping (between thumb and forefinger) the blue part and sliding the gray end along what you want to cut with the entire flat bottom edge (gray and blue) flat against the surface to be sliced. As that tiny piece of ceramic, which only protrudes about as much as a layer of cardstock, drags along the surface it slices it very neatly. The amazing bit is that the ceramic really doesn't feel all that sharp. You can even feel it with your finger without fear of cutting skin.There are a lot of reasons this design / material choice is very elegant.If you're familiar with ceramic skip this paragraph. If you're not familiar with ceramic blades they offer a lot of advantages over steel. For one they don't rust, and I don't mean they don't rust like stainless steel (which is really just stain resistant steel, to varying degrees) I mean that since rust is formed from iron, an "ingredient" of steel but not present in a piece of ceramic, this thing is no more able to rust than a ceramic coffee mug. Another advantage to ceramic is edge life. All steel, no matter how hard, still has some capacity to be "bent" and that ability to deform means it will eventually lose its edge. Ceramic, by contrast, behaves more like a stone. That doesn't mean it can NEVER lose its edge, but you're not going to find the cutting depth and sharpness of this ceramic nub/slicer suffers from casual use, or accidentally being pressed against a hard surface, the way steel would. The DISadvantage to ceramic is that, being hard and inflexible, it can chip and shatter. This is a huge drawback for a large, thin ceramic kitchen knife, but for the tiny nub of a blade on this thing I don't see this being an issue. So long as you aren't banging it against hard surfaces you'll be fine. Think of it a bit like a dinner plate, although in practice I expect this to hold up better since a dinner plate will shatter under its own weight when dropped but I don't expect this to.There IS a very small magnet inside the gray part. It is a very weak magnet. You will not be using this to hang a painting on your fridge, but it's more than adequate to hold the opener. This may be a blessing if you would otherwise end up having 8 coupons someone stuck to the fridge with this fall on the floor every time you wanted to quickly grab this, not that that's what happens with our magnetic bottle opener.... ;)Finally, the $64,000 issue: What can this thing cut? In terms of material hardness, the ceramic blade will easily slice through blister pack plastic, so don't worry about what it's capable of material wise. What you need to be aware of instead is how DEEP you want to cut, and that's where this thing really starts to seem magical, at least for "thin" jobs.If you want to clip coupons, cut out pictures, packing tape, maybe UPC / "box-tops" or anything else that's on a paper-thick layer, then use light pressure and trace the edges of what you want to remove. More pressure will work but you are liable to cut what's underneath as well. With light pressure you can actually cut out a coupon, picture, or recipe from a magazine without affecting the page beneath it.With firm to heavy pressure it will neatly cut open plastic packaging, even the stuff that's about as thick as a business card. Just run a couple cuts down each long side and a cut across the short side at the top or bottom that overlaps the side cuts and the new "flap" will open like a box lid without you having to touch those frustratingly tough edges. The firmer requisite pressure is not because plastic packaging is hard to cut, the ceramic slicer is doing all that work. The firmer pressure is because it's thicker and you need a deeper cut. My slicer arrived in the same box as a nylabone in molded plastic so of course I immediately tried it on that and was even able to cut the molded plastic. It may be designed to be used against a flat piece of material but it is not too difficult to cut molded plastic. Just try and hold it so the blue "handle" is over a convex part of the shape as much as possible, and cut from the center toward the each edge of any indentations, not straight across.At either end of the thickness spectrum just remember to slide the slicer with the flat edge against what is being cut. When you first use it your tendency will probably be to hold it at an angle as if the gray plastic is a knife blade. Remember that ceramic nub on the bottom edge does the cutting, and by varying the pressure you control the depth of the cut. I should also mention that if you ONLY want to make very shallow cuts for pictures, recipes, etc, say you're looking for a scrapbooking gadget and don't need something to deal with packaging, you may be better off with theSlice Safety Cutter, Greenthat has a shallower blade.So what can't this thing do? Cut anything deeper than one or two layers of cardstock. The ceramic nub simply doesn't extend that far. If you want to cut something thicker, such as corrugated cardboard, this is not the right tool for the job. That doesn't mean this can't help open any boxes, as many are secured with very thin tape, but just be aware this NOT a replacement for a utility knife.The iSlice is a new (to me) class of tool, elegant in simplicity, and seems to me to be a simply "correct" design. Leave me a comment if there's anything you wanted to know I *haven't* covered, otherwise I hope this is all the help you need to decide if it's what you're looking for.

Before this, I was using my "Hoover F7411-900 SteamVac Dual V Widepath Carpet Cleaner" and gladly suffered the headache because it cleaned the carpets to like-new condition.  It would leak water like crazy over the spots that were just vacuumed.  However, it spectacularly cleaned and it was only an ounce every time you moved the cleaner backwards.  So, this bissel doesn't look fancy, but sleek and it cleans just as good.  I haven't seen more than a couple drops of water at a time.  Nicer improvement to the puddle-maker.  I also use the tools from the old one and the new one...they seem to be interchangeable.  The only negative is that its a little too black, needs some color...maybe some more red?.  But for this type of product for the price you pay, the manufacturer will either make it pretty or higher quality.

This dock includes a adapter that allows you to charge your naked phone in the cradle/dock, or with a "slim" case on your phone.  It's a very nice feature.  It also includes a "piggyback" charger for your spare battery to plug into the cradle/dock in the back.  There were two cables included which both "appear" to be microUSB connections, but one (white) does NOT fit the cradle/dock, nor ANY other microUSB device that I have.However, everything you need to charge your phone and a spare battery is included with this kit.  The only recommendation that I suggest is finding a charger that supplies MORE power to the cradle/dock.  There are plenty of 2 amp (2000 mA) and greater chargers on Amazon and other websites.  But this cradle/dock comes with a 1.5 amp (1,500 mA) charging block, which will charge the phone or a spare battery quickly, but takes longer when attempting to charge your phone AND a spare battery.

This is a great meatloaf pan! The lifter allows the juices to run to the bottom which I then use as sauce for the meatloaf. Washing it was also a breeze! You forget that it's two pieces, so when you try to pick it up from the oven you instinctively grab the handles from the lifter (instead of grabbing the pan from the sides). Anyway, great pan and I highly recommend it!

PROS: The original controller feels too light. Now this one has some weight, but not too heavy. I'd still like a bigger size for my large hands. But this is all about the rumble baby! Now if Fifa 09 can have Rumble - oh that would be awesome!CONS: the joysticks are springy. if there is no sound on, you can hear it - subtle, but it's there. Everything else feels sturdy. I got this spindle specifically for an additional backup of my photos. From my previous experience with LightScribe DVDs I `ve gotten an impression that they are some of the most reliable recordable optical media that I've used, good enough for most of my archiving purposes. I chose these DVDs based on their Amazon reviews and ratings, and so far I have not been disappointed.Since I got them I've gone through about a dozen DVDs and have not had a single corrupt disk. I've burnt all of the DVDs on two of my Mac computers - one with an internal superdrive and one with an external USB-attached superdrive. I've tested the burned DVDs on both my Mac and Windows computers, and they have all worked just fine.I've used the LightScribe burner on my Windows HP laptop with a few of them, and it had worked OK - the performance of the LightScribe burner on this laptop has never been all that good, and over the years the final image has only gotten worse. I have never been a big fan of LightScribe. It has a very pale look (worse than the e-paper), and inscribing the image on a CD or a DVD tends to be very finicky. From what I've seen so far these Verbatim DVDs will perform on par with all the other high-end LightScribe DVDs that I've used, but this has not been one of the main considerations for me when choosing the archival quality DVDs.Overall, these are very solid archival quality DVDs. No optical media will hold your precious data forever, but these DVDs will probably keep it safe for as long as there are DVD drives out there, and quite possibly much longer.

After trying most of the higher-ranking, still cost-efficient brands(Netgear, D-link, Zyxel), I was getting very tired of constant signal-loss, connection problems, communication problems with Comcast modem etc etc. Reading a lot of reviews, it seemed like Cisco/linksys is still the only one who can create a properly functioning router.My network is pretty extreme for a normal house, and I utilize most aspects of it that you would see in a modern high-tech household (centralized data server, media streaming all-over etc). This router is the first one ever for me to meet all my expectations and more.- Set-up is easy; would be easy for non-tech users too- All connections are stable- set-up at central point in house; connectivity in almost every corner and right outside with wireless-N (3400 sqft house)- Standard additional guest-access with separate (short) password (compare to hotel access)- Many functions on top of all standard advanced functionality, like blocking websites, blocking 1 MAC address at certain times- etcI couldn't be happier with this, and wish I wasn't so cheap in the first place; spent over $200 on cheaper ones before spending the appropriate $100 on this one.

I really like these Lodge Logic skillets, and this one is no exception. It is heavy, durable, and pretty much perfect for a variety of uses. If you dont know, iron skillets distribute heat evenly along its surface. This makes cooking far more easy to control. There arent hot spots like with aluminum and non stick skillets. Additionally, they are very easy to clean. Simply boil some water and scrub with a soft brush. These, if anything, hearken back to the days of blacksmiths and hand crafterd wares. I have read complaints about eggs sticking and the like. Definitely note that these are NOT non stick skillets, but they are useful for a wide variety of things besides eggs. For example, we heat tortillas in ours, saute our vegetables, make skillet cakes, etc....additionally, they can go from the stovetop into the oven, so if you are, for example, pan frying a steak, you would sear one side, turn it over, then finish in the oven. This is useful for the abovementioned skillet cakes, corn bread, and a wide variety of other things. You are only limited by your imagination when it comes to these iron skillets.Enjoy!

I got the Z22 to replace a well-used and well-loved Palm Vx whose battery died.  I thought with the advances in hardware in the last six or seven years, I'd be able to get a great machine at a reasonable price.  Boy, was I wrong.The biggest problem I have with the Z22 is that compared with the Vx, the screen is crap.  It's ugly to look at, the pixelation is obvious, and the touchscreen function is not very good.  Many things I could do with a fingernail on the Vx require the stylus on the Z22.  The color is a nice frill but it's crappy color and unpleasant to look at. I continually have to fiddle with the contrast control to compensate for different viewing conditions, and in bright sunlight it's basically unusuable. I much preferred the Vx's four shades of gray.I'm also annoyed that the backward compatibility is not there: most of my old applications run, but a significant fraction (maybe 20%) do not.  Some wedge the machine and require a reset.I also expected I would get better performance from a 2008 machine than from a 2001 machine.  Except for one application (CacheMate), the Z22 actually performs the same as or *worse* than the Vx.  In particular, Launcher III is noticeably slow.  I do like the extra memory but I never ran out of memory on the Vx and the CPU is disappointing.Finally, the sound stinks.  The sounds are not as good, and inside the hard 'air case' I can barely hear the sounds, even turned up to high.  With the Vx I could hear easily even inside the clamshell hard case.So, in summary, the Palm platform is still great, and color is amusing, but if you order the Z22 be aware that you're getting a cheap machine where corners have been cut on screen and CPU performance.  If this matters to you, get something else.  If you don't care that the screen is ugly and the machine is slow, the case looks nice and pockets easily, and in my opinion the Palm platform is still the best personal organizer in the world.

I've had quite a few "sporting event radios" that I take to Lacrosse, Football and Basketball games to listen to the broadcast and I think I now have the perfect combination:Get this radio and also a set of the Panasonic RP-HC-55 Noise cancelling earbuds and you will be in heaven.The radio is lighter than most, the round dials are not stuck out so far as to accidently hit them, the battery life is better than average, the clip is substantial and I clip it to the back velcro band in my cap.The Panasonic 55's block out a high percentage of the crap (senseless noise) which goes on at most games. I am still amazed when, during a game, I pop the earbuds out to see what I'm filtering out. They are light, have a volume control slide, and most of all they stay in which, when you first look at them, you would not expect. I also use them at the gym (more senseless noise) and they really shine when you are flying.Nice tandem, it really improves the game.BTW - If anyone knows of a better set of noise cancelling earbuds please comment here.
